chunks.text: Neurodegenerative diseases (NDs), such as Alzheimer’s disease (AD), Parkinson’s disease (PD), amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), Huntington’s disease (HD), frontotemporal lobar dementia (FTD), and Lewy body disease (LBD) are debilitating disorders that are gradually increasing in prevalence in our aging society. In recent decades, advancements in research methodologies have yielded novel insights into both the phenotypic manifestations and molecular underpinnings of these common NDs. Studies have identified several pathogeneses that are shared among these diseases (Ross and Poirier, 2004; Frost and Diamond, 2010; Amor et al., 2014; Soto and Pritzkow, 2018; Vaquer-Alicea and Diamond, 2019; Wells et al., 2019). For example, many of these NDs exhibit overlapping gene variants and share pathogenic proteins. Specific toxic proteins associated with the diseases include extracellular amyloid-beta (Aβ) and phosphorylated tau (pTau), which are linked to AD (Rajendran and Krishnan, 2024; Pradeepkiran et al., 2024); α-synuclein (α-syn, SNCA) in PD (Mehra et al., 2019); Cu/Zn superoxide dismutase (SOD1) (Kaur et al., 2016), TAR DNA-binding protein 43 (TDP-43) (Baralle et al., 2013), fused in sarcoma (FUS) (Mackenzie et al., 2010), and chromosome 9 open-reading frame 72 (C9ORF72) (Geng and Cai, 2024) in ALS; pTau, C9ORF7 (Balendra and Isaacs, 2018), and progranulin (Petkau and Leavitt, 2014) in FTD; huntingtin (HTT) (Zheng and Diamond, 2012) in HD; and α-syn (Noguchi-Shinohara and Ono, 2023) in LBD. These proteins serve as classic pathological hallmarks of their respective diseases. The underlying genetic mutations associated with the pathogenic proteins in these common NDs either dictate or contribute to the progression of these disorders. Therefore, a clear understanding of the relationship between genetic mutations and NDs is crucial for uncovering the disease-specific mechanisms as well as potential common pathways. Although each ND has its own unique pathogenesis, current studies have demonstrated that there are also shared pathogenic mechanisms across different NDs (Ross and Poirier, 2004; Frost and Diamond, 2010; Amor et al., 2014; Soto and Pritzkow, 2018; Vaquer-Alicea and Diamond, 2019; Wells et al., 2019). Based on these findings, a better understanding of the overlapping and interconnected pathogeneses among common NDs may reveal novel mechanisms or therapeutic targets for specific NDs or for NDs as a whole.

Current investigations into the shared or interactive pathogeneses among different NDs are primarily focused on disease-candidate genes, molecular pathways, and gene networks, aiming to provide support for the existence of common molecular mechanisms (Arneson et al., 2018; Soto and Pritzkow, 2018; Liao et al., 2022; Vasconcelos et al., 2023). Associated studies have found valuable evidence for shared molecular dysfunctions and disorders with the pathogeneses of multiple NDs. However, these findings require further validation through extensive studies on the common pathogeneses of these diseases.

For this review paper, we systematically and multi-dimensionally retrieved and analyzed research information on the pathogenesis of six NDs at the genetic, transcriptomic, molecular pathway and gene network levels. We focused on molecular pathogenesis with the aim of providing more objective and comprehensive insights into these common NDs, summarizing the shared molecular mechanisms identified among them to draw conclusive insights. Our review of the similarities among the potential pathogeneses of these six NDs is organized based on current investigations, and the shared factors associated with genetics, gene mutations, pathways, and networks are discussed. Our objective is to provide an overview of the existing research on pathogenesis, linking recent findings related to the six NDs to identify promising common mechanisms. The biological processes underlying neurodegeneration in various NDs ultimately lead to neural cell death, including that of neurons, which contributes to the development of these diseases. We believe that damaged neural cells can be prevented from dying or recovered through interventions that halt or reverse these related pathogenic processes, particularly those that target multiple pathways simultaneously. Understanding the exact pathogeneses of these diseases is crucial for developing effective treatments. To this end, we sought to identify potential common pathogeneses among the NDs by reviewing recent novel findings, offering clues for further research into more precise and valuable pathogenic mechanisms and optimal therapeutic strategies.

In this review, we conducted a systematic literature retrieval on the pathogenesis of six common NDs using several databases, including ISI Processing, PubMed, ProQuest, OpenGray, Scopus, Web of Science, Cochrane Library, EmBase, Google Scholar, WanFang, and relevant medical guidelines. We used search terms such as “neurodegenerative diseases,” “Alzheimer’s disease,” “Parkinson’s disease,” “amyotrophic lateral sclerosis,” “Huntington’s disease,” “frontotemporal lobar dementia,” and “Lewy body disease,” combined with “pathogenesis.”

Additionally, we refined key search terms to extensively explore the literature regarding the potential common pathogenesis of the six NDs. The retrieved literature primarily included review articles, major textbook chapters, abstracts, and reports from relevant meetings. We focused on literature published over nearly the past 30 years, up until the end of October 2024, while not excluding frequently referenced older studies with high citation counts. The final reference list was compiled based on both the originality and relevance of the literature to our review topic, as well as the eligibility of the studies, their quality, and their compatibility with the present review through evaluating and analyzing titles, abstracts, and full articles (Figure 1).

Timeline showing the potential common pathogenesis of AD, PD, ALS, HD, FTD and LBD as documented in the literature.

In this review, we analyzed literature from the past 30 years, spanning from 1997 to 2024. Although the major pathogenesis of NDs remains incompletely understood, researchers have identified numerous important mechanisms over the years, providing crucial clues and evidence for the clinical treatment of NDs. Key findings include the roles of excitotoxicity and mitochondrial dysfunction, which have contributed to the development of Edaravone and Riluzole for ALS treatment. Additionally, the discovery of SOD1 genetic mutations has opened new avenues for gene-modified therapeutics for NDs. Other important factors such as neuroinflammation, the toxic production and aggregation of specific pathogenic proteins, the failure of neural stem cells, and the disruption of gut microbiota homeostasis have also been recognized as contributing to the pathogenesis of NDs, creating new opportunities for novel treatment strategies. AD: Alzheimer’s disease; ALS: amyotrophic lateral sclerosis; FTD: frontotemporal lobar dementia; HD: Huntington’s disease; LBD: Lewy body disease; NDs: neurodegenerative diseases; PD: Parkinson’s disease; SOD1: Cu/Zn superoxide dismutase.

Aging is an inevitable biological process in the human life cycle that is characterized by the progressive physiological deterioration of various organs and an increased risk of multiple diseases, including NDs (Hou et al., 2019). Among the various conditions encountered during aging, NDs are the most prevalent in the older adult population (Hou et al., 2019). The mechanisms underlying neurodegeneration in these disorders are highly complex and not yet fully understood. Among the numerous risk factors associated with neurodegeneration in the pathogenesis of NDs, aging is considered one of the most significant (Hou et al., 2019).

Aging is a crucial risk factor associated with several common NDs, such as AD (Sengoku, 2020), PD (Reeve et al., 2014), ALS (Niccoli et al., 2017), HD (Iskusnykh et al., 2024), FTD (Niccoli et al., 2017), and LBD (Walker et al., 2019). Nine biological hallmarks of aging are related to the development of NDs: genomic instability (Peze-Heidsieck et al., 2021), telomere attrition (Gao et al., 2022), epigenetic alterations (Ghosh and Saadat, 2023; la Torre et al., 2023), loss of proteostasis (Labbadia and Morimoto, 2015), mitochondrial dysfunction (Johnson et al., 2021; Klemmensen et al., 2024), cellular senescence (Lee et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024), deregulated nutrient sensing (Guo et al., 2022), stem cell exhaustion (Jagaraj et al., 2024), and altered intercellular communication (Hill, 2019; Huo et al., 2021). These critical biological mechanisms of aging play significant roles in ND pathogenesis (López-Otín et al., 2013; Hou et al., 2019).

The nine biological hallmarks of aging are typically categorized into primary, antagonistic, and integrative aging biomarkers. The primary biomarkers consist of genomic instability, epigenetic alterations, telomere attrition, and dysproteostasis. The antagonistic aging biomarkers encompass compensatory responses to the primary aging-related lesions and include mitochondrial dysfunction, cellular senescence, and downregulation of nutrient sensing. The integrative aging biomarkers result from the cumulative effects of the primary and antagonistic biomarkers and include stem cell exhaustion and altered intercellular communication (López-Otín et al., 2013).

Among the aging biomarkers, cellular senescence, genomic instability, and dysproteostasis are closely associated with protein aggregation in various NDs, including AD and PD (Wilson et al., 2023). Neurodegeneration in these NDs is particularly linked to age-related changes, suggesting there are connections between NDs and aging-related alterations in the central nervous system (CNS) microenvironment, genomic instability, epigenetic modifications, and dysproteostasis. While aging is recognized to be one of the crucial risk factors for NDs, the precise mechanisms by which it contributes to neurodegeneration and whether age-related changes in the brain serve as early indicators of neurodegenerative processes remain unclear and require further investigation. Several recent molecular studies have reported that abnormal aggregations of α-syn (Li et al., 2023a, b), phosphorylated tau (Guarnieri et al., 2024), and Aβ (Liu et al., 2024a, b) are related to aging (Sengupta and Kayed, 2022). However, there is currently no direct evidence that these pathogenic proteins of NDs actively participate in neurodegeneration during human aging.

Nonetheless, some mechanisms linking aging and the pathogenesis of NDs have been made relatively clear. For example, CNS cells, such as neurons and glial cells, may be susceptible to age-dependent alterations such as DNA methylation. DNA methylation is an important epigenetic modification that occurs during aging (Unnikrishnan et al., 2019; Soto-Palma et al., 2022) and is involved in DNA methyltransferase-mediated DNA damage that can induce NDs (Mattson, 2003). Additionally, mitochondrial dysfunction, which is associated with aging, promotes neurodegeneration by generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) and enhancing neuroinflammation in NDs (Lin and Beal, 2006).

Aging is a significant risk factor for AD. Age-related disorders include oxidative stress, DNA damage, and mitochondrial dysfunction. Moreover, a progressive accumulation of senescent cells leads to chronic inflammatory responses. This inflammation is accompanied by the accumulation of toxic proteins, resulting in tau aggregation and Aβ deposition, which damage neuronal synapses and contribute to cognitive impairment in AD. These processes involve various forms of cellular damage, including DNA, protein, and lipid damage, as well as changes in DNA methylation, histone modification, and the dysregulation of non-coding RNAs. Additionally, mitochondrial dysfunction, deregulated nutrient sensing, and the senescence of neurons, astrocytes, microglia, oligodendrocytes, immune cells, and cerebrovascular endothelial cells all play roles in AD pathology. Other contributing factors include stem cell exhaustion, altered intercellular communication, chronic neuroinflammation, and dysbiosis (Liu et al., 2024a, b).

A major pathological feature of PD is the formation of Lewy bodies, composed of the toxic protein α-syn, which result from mutations in the α-syn gene in dopaminergic neurons. This phenomenon is linked to various pathogenic mechanisms associated with aging and multiple NDs. These mechanisms include inflammatory pathway activation by α-syn in glial cells through interactions with immune cells. Natural aging impairs the cellular processes responsible for clearing toxic substances, leading to chronic neuroinflammatory responses and the accumulation of internal α-syn, which ultimately contributes to the death of dopaminergic neurons. The interplay between aging, α-syn pathogenicity, and α-syn interactions with specific types of neural cells in the CNS are indicators of the α-syn-driven pathogenesis of PD. This understanding may pave the way for novel therapeutic approaches for treating PD (Zhang et al., 2023).

In patients with HD, aging primarily affects the morphology of the cerebellum, leading to alterations in cerebellar circuits predominantly involved in motor control. This results in a deficiency of synapses and dendrites, contributing to the pathogenesis of HD. Aging is additionally associated with the apoptosis of cerebellar cells that is driven by iron-dependent ferroptosis, oxidative stress, DNA damage, and alterations in DNA modifications. Other contributing factors include progressive mitochondrial dysfunction, the activation of microglia and oligodendrocytes, and neuroinflammation related to aging (Iskusnykh et al., 2024).

Genetic factors are recognized to be major contributors to the pathogenesis of neurodegeneration in NDs, either in the context of monogenic hereditary disorders or complex, multifactorial sporadic diseases (Pihlstrøm et al., 2017). Over the past 20 years, the identification of genes and risk loci associated with NDs has significantly advanced our understanding of the pathogenic mechanisms and pathways involved in these disorders. Through large-scale research efforts, new study designs and advancements in methodologies have been made that have rapidly expanded our knowledge of the genomic and genetic architectures underlying the pathogenesis of NDs (Pihlstrøm et al., 2017).

All NDs are associated with significant genetic phenotypes. Based on estimates, the genetic heritability of AD, PD, and ALS is approximately 58% (Gatz et al., 2006), 40% (Hamza and Payami, 2010), and 60% (Al-Chalabi et al., 2010), respectively. Rare genetic mutations associated with NDs display Mendelian hereditary forms. For example, the amyloid precursor protein (APP) (Orobets and Karamyshev, 2023) and presenilin genes (PSEN1 and PSEN2) (Lanoiselée et al., 2017; Neuner et al., 2020; Andrade-Guerrero et al., 2023) are linked to AD, while mutations in SNCA (Stefanis, 2012), Parkin 2 (von Coelln et al., 2004), PTEN-induced putative kinase 1 (PINK1) (Cherian and Divya, 2020), microtubule-associated protein tau (MAPT) (Bekris et al., 2010), and leucine-rich repeat kinase 2 (LRRK2) (Xiong and Yu, 2024) are associated with PD. In ALS, genes such as cytosolic SOD1 (Kaur et al., 2016), alsin 2 (Finsterer and Burgunder, 2014), senataxin (Tsui et al., 2023), and vesicle-associated membrane protein B (VAPB) (Zhang et al., 2015) have been implicated as causative factors. However, the majority of NDs exhibit non-Mendelian hereditary forms and involve complex pathogenesis with numerous genetic variants. These complex genetic forms of NDs were difficult to detect and identify prior to the advent of genome-wide association studies (GWAS). However, with the increasing application of GWAS, several risk genes and loci associated with the pathogenesis of NDs have been identified. For instance, the apolipoprotein E locus has been shown to have a strong relationship with the onset of clinical AD and PD, although the specific alleles or loci may differ between these diseases (Li et al., 2017; Neuner et al., 2020). Additionally, HLA-DRB5 and MAPT have emerged as common potential genes for both AD and PD based on GWAS results (Zhou and Wang, 2017; Little et al., 2017; Kim, 2018; Pan et al., 2021), and the C9ORF72 repeat expansion has been identified as a shared genetic factor in both ALS and frontotemporal dementia (FTD) (Jiang et al., 2022). At the pathway level, vesicle-mediated transport is a common process involved in multiple NDs, including AD, PD, and ALS. Furthermore, nine pathways, including synaptic signaling, neuronal projection development, and proteolysis, represent common mechanisms in the pathogenesis of AD and PD (Ramanan and Saykin, 2013; Shang et al., 2015; Ahmad et al., 2017).

By applying current analytical strategies, numerous pathways have been identified that are common among prevalent NDs. These shared pathways involve various internal cellular processes, including apoptosis, autophagy, mitochondrial functions, oxidative damage, and proteasome activity. Additionally, they encompass partial tissue environments related to cellular adhesion, endocytosis, neurotransmission, and prionoid transmission factors. Systemic environments involving inflammation and immune responses, and lipid metabolism, as well as endocrine and vascular factors, have also been implicated as disease factors. Furthermore, biological processes related to development and aging—such as epigenetics, neurotrophic factors, and telomere dynamics—have been highlighted as being involved in NDs (Ahmad et al., 2017; Gan et al., 2018; Sanghai and Tranmer, 2023). From the overrepresented pathways identified via updated GWAS, it has been demonstrated that internal cellular processes—including various cellular mechanisms, transportation, macromolecule complex binding, cellular activation, vesicle-mediated transport, proteolysis, and kinase binding—are significant factors in common NDs. Additionally, pathways related to neural systems, such as synaptic signaling and neuronal projections, are also involved (Cooper-Knock et al., 2012). In a study examining gene interactions in AD and PD, a gene model was constructed using a transcription factor binding network curated through MetaCore software. This study revealed that several GWAS candidate genes for AD and PD are closely related to coherent networks coordinated by transcription factors SP1 and AP-1. Moreover, overlapping genes associated with ALS, PD, and AD were identified, particularly those related to neuroinflammatory gene signatures. These signatures were found to correlate with RNA splicing and protein turnover in ALS and PD. Mitochondrial dysfunction genes were also identified as being common among PD and AD cases (Cooper-Knock et al., 2012).

The first mutation in the SNCA gene was discovered in a subset of PD patients in 1997, highlighting the strong genetic link to PD pathogenesis. Currently, seven pathogenic mutations and/or potential risk genes associated with PD have been identified in patients. The established PD genes among these include SNCA, LRRK2, VPS35, RAB32, PRKN, PINK1, and PARK7/DJ-1. The pathogenic mutations contribute directly or indirectly to the aggregation and production of toxic SNCA protein or are closely associated with these processes (Lim and Klein, 2024).

Although most ALS cases are sporadic, a smaller proportion are familial (fALS). The hexanucleotide repeat expansion (GGGGCC) located in the first intron of the C9ORF72 gene is a common genetic cause of both fALS and sporadic ALS, underscoring the disease’s strong genetic component. ALS primarily involves the degeneration and death of motor neurons; however, glial cells also play significant protective roles in the nervous system and contribute to the pathophysiological processes of ALS through non-cell-autonomous mechanisms. Microglia regulate phagocytosis, immune responses, neuroinflammation, and immune surveillance and activation. In addition, oligodendrocytes myelinate neuronal axons in the CNS to facilitate synaptic transmission and provide metabolic support to neurons. Pathogenic mutations in various ALS-related genes are known to lead to the activation of microglia and oligodendrocytes, triggering the pathogenesis of ALS through these mechanisms (Jagaraj et al., 2024).

FTD shares mutations of several common genes with ALS, including C9ORF72, TDP-43, sequestosome 1, FUS, VCP, CHCHD10, and tank-binding kinase 1. These genes are involved in a number of the mechanisms that contribute to the pathogenesis of FTD, such as protein misfolding, oxidative stress, axonal transport impairment, neuroinflammation, and immune responses. These processes are closely associated with neuroinflammatory and immune cells, including astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, microglia, and lymphocytes. Overall, cellular microenvironment processes closely linked to genetic disease factors—particularly neuroinflammation and immune responses—are suggested to be pivotal factors in the pathogenesis of FTD (De Marchi et al., 2023; Table 1).

Genes and signaling pathways associated with the risk of neurodegenerative diseases

AD: Alzheimer’s disease; ALS: amyotrophic lateral sclerosis; APOE: apolipoprotein E; APP: amyloid precursor protein; Aβ: amyolid-beta; C9orf72: chromosome 9 open-reading frame 72; FTD: frontotemporal lobar dementia; GBA: glucocerebrosidase; HD: Huntington’s disease; HTT: huntingtin; LBD: Lewy body disease; LRRK2: leucine-rich repeat kinase 2 or dardarin; MAPT: microtubule-associated protein tau; PD: Parkinson’s disease; SNCA: α-synuclein; SOD1: Cu/Zn superoxide dismutase.

chunks.section: Progressive Neuron Dysfunction/Death and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: Abnormal neuronal death induced by excessive programmed cell death (PCD) in specific regions of the CNS is a hallmark of the pathology associated with NDs such as AD, PD, ALS, and HD (Moujalled et al., 2021). The pathogenesis of NDs is multifactorial and complex, involving various defects in different cellular processes, including oxidative stress (Niedzielska et al., 2016), excitotoxicity (Doble, 1999), mitochondrial dysfunction (Klemmensen et al., 2024), protein misfolding (Sami et al., 2017), and inflammation (Stephenson et al., 2018). There is a considerable amount of evidence supporting the role of PCD in the pathogenesis of various NDs (Moujalled et al., 2021). However, a critical question remains: are the defects in PCD signaling and neuronal death key contributors to the insults that lead to NDs, or are they merely secondary responses? Additionally, how different PCD pathways and related processes interact to cause the demise of neuronal cells and other cell types in the context of NDs remains unclear.

chunks.section: Progressive Neuron Dysfunction/Death and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: The mechanisms behind neuronal death in the CNS have long been challenging to decipher. Under normal physiological conditions, neuronal death in the adult brain is strictly regulated, even among the older adult population. However, excessive neuronal death in specific regions of the CNS represents a fundamental pathological feature of various NDs (Chi et al., 2018; Moujalled et al., 2021). In a healthy mature CNS, extensive neuronal death is rare; however, neuronal death tends to increase with age in certain brain regions. Although studies have detected differences in neuronal numbers between younger and older populations, the overall loss of neurons is still relatively limited. In contrast, significantly more neuronal death occurs in specific regions of the brain in individuals with various NDs than in those of age-matched controls, and this loss is correlated with the onset and progression of these diseases (Chi et al., 2018; Moujalled et al., 2021). Generally, mature neurons in the CNS exhibit a strong resistance to cell death compared to immature neurons. They maintain cellular homeostasis and longevity by managing various stresses. However, when faced with severe pathological environments, such as those present in NDs, this resistance can be overwhelmed, leading to neuronal death (Chi et al., 2018; Moujalled et al., 2021).

chunks.section: Progressive Neuron Dysfunction/Death and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: Current studies on the pathogenesis of NDs are focused on identifying key pathological changes involved in the formation and aggregation of specific pathogenic proteins (Ross and Poirier, 2004; Soto and Pritzkow, 2018; Wilson et al., 2023). These pathogenic proteins create pathological stress and are cytotoxic to neurons, leading to excessive excitotoxicity and the generation of ROS. This process results in synaptic dysfunction, the impairment of protein degradation systems, endoplasmic reticulum stress, DNA damage, mitochondrial dysfunction, inflammation, and reentry into the cell cycle (Chi et al., 2018). Neuronal death in NDs poses significant challenges to understanding the neurodegenerative pathogenesis. While many mechanisms by which these pathogenic proteins initiate neuronal death in NDs have been partially elucidated, further research is needed.

chunks.section: Progressive Neuron Dysfunction/Death and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: Selective neuron death in specific regions of the CNS is a prominent pathological feature of various NDs. Thus identifying and measuring neuron numbers in these distinct regions can significantly aid our understanding of the occurrence and pathogenesis of NDs (Chi et al., 2018; Moujalled et al., 2021). Another feature prevalent across various NDs is the abnormal aggregation of proteins. Although the composition and location of these aberrant toxic protein aggregates differ among the various NDs, it is clear that their generation and accumulation represent significant changes and shared mechanisms of pathogenesis in diseases such as AD, PD, ALS, HD, FTD, and LBD (Taylor et al., 2002; Ross and Poirier, 2004; Chung et al., 2018; Ruz et al., 2020). The aggregation of toxic proteins in neural cells is closely associated with critical pathological developments, such as neuron death, in various NDs. However, we should exercise caution when evaluating the role of specific aberrant proteins in the pathogenesis of NDs, as it remains unclear whether the aggregation of certain proteins is harmful or has protective effects against neuronal death.

chunks.section: Progressive Neuron Dysfunction/Death and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: The pathways of neuron death in the pathogenesis of NDs may involve apoptosis or necroptosis. It has been suggested that initiators or executors of these processes are activated in NDs (Hajimohamadreza and Treherne, 1997; Nishimoto et al., 1997; Desjardins and Ledoux, 1998; Friedlander, 2003). Additionally, some authors have proposed that other cell death pathways, including necroptosis, may also contribute to the pathogenesis of NDs (Shao et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017; Chi et al., 2018; Dionísio et al., 2020).

Apoptosis primarily involves two pathways: the intrinsic (mitochondrial) pathway and the extrinsic (death receptor) pathway. The intrinsic pathway is regulated by pro-apoptotic and anti-apoptotic members of the BCL-2 protein family, including BCL-2, BCL-XL, MCL-1, BCL-W, and A1/BFL1. Critical apoptotic initiators known as BH3-only proteins, such as BIM, PUMA, BID, BMF, BAD, HRK, BIK, and NOXA, bind with high affinity to anti-apoptotic BCL-2 proteins. These interactions release or directly activate BAX, leading to the formation of oligomers that permeabilize the outer mitochondrial membrane. Consequently, this process promotes the release of cytochrome c and activates caspase-activating factors, which in turn trigger a caspase cascade that cleaves hundreds of proteins, ultimately inducing cell death. The extrinsic pathway is initiated by the binding of ligands to members of the tumor necrosis factor (TNF) receptor superfamily that contain intracellular death domains. This binding activates the formation of signaling complexes that lead to the activation of caspase-8 and downstream effector caspases, such as caspase-3 and -7. The extrinsic pathway can also interact with the intrinsic apoptosis pathway by activating the pro-apoptotic BH3-only protein BID, thus modulating cellular apoptosis through caspase-8-mediated proteolytic processes (Offen et al., 2000; Harada and Grant, 2003).

With regards to the neuron death pathways involved in ND pathogenesis, apoptosis has long been suggested to be a primary mechanisms of neuron death in NDs. Research has shown that nearly all Lewy-body-positive neurons exhibit positivity for the pro-apoptotic factor BAX, indicating a significant aggregation of pro-apoptotic proteins occurs in the pathogenesis of NDs (Camins et al., 2008). Additionally, the identification of mediators associated with apoptosis and necroptosis, such as tumor suppressor p53, mitogen-activated protein kinases, and necrostatin-1, suggests that both apoptotic and necroptotic processes contribute to neuron death pathways in NDs (Deigner et al., 2000; Yuan and Yankner, 2000; Jellinger, 2001; Li, 2021). Therefore, apoptosis and necroptosis represent critical common mechanisms in the pathogenesis of NDs.

chunks.section: Protein Misfolding, Aggregation, Conformation, and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text:
The three-dimensional architectures of proteins are determined by their distinct amino acid sequences, and the construction of functional frameworks in cells and tissues is essential for cellular metabolism and biological signal communication. While the majority of protein folding is specific, some proteins exhibit a degree of structural flexibility that allows them to adapt their shape to perform certain physiological functions. As with other biological processes, the structures of proteins dictate their functions. Consequently, protein folding is a critical biological process for maintaining efficient cellular quality control systems, which ensures normal protein production and elimination are maintained (Dobson, 2003).

When certain pathogenic factors alter the cellular environment, they can lead to protein misfolding, resulting in the production of abnormal protein structures. These aberrant proteins disrupt the normal processes involved in scavenging and eliminating abnormal, pathogenic, or toxic proteins, causing them to aggregate and progressively deposit in intracellular and/or extracellular spaces. This accumulation contributes to the development of specific diseases, such as NDs (Soto and Pritzkow, 2018). Recent insights into the mechanisms of protein misfolding and aggregation indicate that the characteristic aggregation patterns and distinctive deposition sites of specific proteins are closely associated with the pathogenesis of NDs (Ross and Poirier, 2004).

chunks.section: Protein Misfolding, Aggregation, Conformation, and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: Another common pathogenic process in NDs is the progressive accumulation of protein aggregations, typically referred to as amyloid proteins, in specific intercellular or extracellular compartments (Soto, 2003). Although the protein aggregation processes involved in different NDs are distinct, the intermediates, end-products, and major features of protein misfolding are remarkably similar (Soto, 2003). The seeding-nucleation mechanisms of protein misfolding and aggregation have been increasingly characterized, described in detail, and modeled kinetically (Meisl et al., 2017; Zampar et al., 2024). While there are commonalities in the pathogeneses of NDs, there are also potential differences, including clinical symptoms, prevalence, risk factors, affected regions of the CNS, injured cell types, and implicated genes. Moreover, each ND is often associated with the misfolding and aggregation of a distinct protein that forms deposits in certain cellular locations, such as the cytoplasm, nuclei, cytoplasmic membranes, or extracellular space (Ross and Poirier, 2004; Sami et al., 2017; Soto and Pritzkow, 2018). Despite the similarities in the gross structural signatures of aggregation and cross-beta conformations, alterations in the detailed structures reveal significant differences among the proteins involved in specific NDs. Variations in the sequences, cellular locations, and biophysical characteristics of the aggregated proteins likely result in different mechanisms of cellular toxicity (Roy et al., 2022).

chunks.section: Protein Misfolding, Aggregation, Conformation, and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: The presence of protein aggregations is a hallmark of multiple NDs, including AD, PD, ALS, HD, FTD, and LBD. Traditionally, each disease is associated with specific protein aggregations. For example, AD is characterized by the aggregation of Aβ and tau proteins, while α-synucleinopathies such as PD and LBD involve the aggregation of α-syn. In ALS, protein aggregates include SOD1, TDP-43, FUS, and C9ORF72 dipeptide repeat proteins, while HD features the aggregation of HTT protein. FTD is associated with the aggregation of tau, C9ORF72, and Pick bodies, and LBD is characterized by the aggregation of insoluble Lewy bodies. However, it has been observed that the aggregation of a single protein can occur across multiple NDs, and one ND may involve the aggregation of several different proteins. For instance, protein aggregation has been identified in AD, HD, and various forms of FTD. Similarly, Aβ aggregation has also been found in PD and LBD. Tau aggregation, while primarily associated with tauopathies, has also been detected in prion diseases, α-synucleinopathies, and cognitively healthy aging individuals. TDP-43 aggregation is typically linked to FTD and ALS, but it has also been observed in AD, progressive supranuclear palsy, corticobasal degeneration, multiple system atrophy, LBD, and other NDs. These findings highlight the complexities of protein aggregations in NDs, suggesting that common interactions and pathogeneses may exist across the different diseases. Further research into these intricate shared mechanisms may provide a better understanding of the distinctive and common pathogeneses of these fatal conditions (Ross and Poirier, 2004; Calabrese et al., 2022).

chunks.section: Protein Misfolding, Aggregation, Conformation, and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: One of the most important findings in the pathogenesis of NDs is the misfolding, aggregation, and accumulation of proteins, which lead to cellular dysfunction, loss of synaptic connections, and lesions in the CNS. Although distinct proteins are involved in different NDs, the processes of protein misfolding and aggregation show remarkable similarities across these diseases. NDs are increasingly understood to share common cellular and molecular mechanisms, including protein aggregation and the formation of specific inclusion bodies. Protein aggregates typically consist of fibrils formed by proteins with cross-beta conformations, including amyloid proteins. There is a partial, but not complete, overlap among the types of abnormal proteins deposited in affected cells and the degenerative cells themselves. These inclusions and visible protein aggregates may represent a common stage in the molecular cascades underlying the pathogenesis of NDs. Furthermore, the earlier steps in these cascades may represent more direct mechanisms of disease pathogenesis than the formation of inclusions (Ross and Poirier, 2004).

chunks.section: Protein Misfolding, Aggregation, Conformation, and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: The shared characteristics of NDs suggest there is a common underlying pathogenesis. The most common feature of these diseases is the deposition of amyloid proteins and the degeneration of the CNS network. Amyloids are ordered assemblies of proteins that may act as self-replicating templates that reproduce by adding monomers. Various amyloid proteins, such as Aβ and tau, exhibit properties similar to those of infectious prion-like proteins, including discrete self-replicating amyloid structures and the ability to propagate aggregates across cells, leading to transmissible neuropathologies. This indicates that the prion-like phenomenon of amyloid transcellular propagation may be a common mechanism in the pathogenesis of various NDs, including AD and PD. The fundamental and distinct patterns of neuropathology, such as cell entry, amplification, transcellular movement, and the spread of amyloid proteins throughout the nervous system, represent potential common pathways in the development of NDs (Vaquer-Alicea and Diamond, 2019).

chunks.section: Protein Misfolding, Aggregation, Conformation, and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: The most common NDs, AD, PD, ALS, and FTD, share several features of protein aggregation, despite exhibiting various distinct clinical symptoms. For instance, AD is characterized by the presence of senile plaques composed of aggregated Aβ peptides and neurofibrillary tangles formed by intraneuronal tau aggregation. Similar protein accumulations are observed in PD (Bourdenx et al., 2017), indicating a common pathogenesis. TDP-43 aggregation is associated with ALS, while tau protein aggregation (MAPT) is observed in both FTD and AD, implying there are shared pathogenic mechanisms among these diseases (Montalbano et al., 2020).

chunks.section: Protein Misfolding, Aggregation, Conformation, and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: Protein aggregations are often composed of a single protein or a mixture of multiple heterogeneous proteins of varying sizes and structures (Hayden and Teplow, 2013). The latest studies have demonstrated that several common proteins, including Aβ and tau, often coexist in aggregates (Sengupta and Kayed, 2022; Li et al., 2023a, b; Guarnieri et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024a, b). These findings suggest that there may be a significant common pathogenesis underlying AD and PD, despite the considerable heterogeneity in their clinical manifestations (Melki, 2015; Narasimhan et al., 2017). Research has shown that more than seven different diseases are associated with abnormal tau aggregation, including AD, FTD, progressive supranuclear palsy, corticobasal degeneration, argyrophilic grain disease, and chronic traumatic encephalopathy (Williams, 2006). While three diseases are linked to tau deposition, PD, multiple system atrophy, and LBD (Goedert et al., 2017). These findings further support the notion that multiple neurodegenerative diseases, including AD, PD, FTD, and LBD, share a common pathogenesis.

chunks.section: Protein Misfolding, Aggregation, Conformation, and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: The coexistence of multiple distinct protein aggregations among various NDs is supported by substantial amounts of evidence (Brown et al., 1998; Giasson et al., 2003; Clinton et al., 2010; Morales et al., 2013; Spires-Jones et al., 2017). A typical pathogenic sign in AD is the simultaneous presence of intracellular tau neurofibrillary tangles and extracellular Aβ plaques (Morales et al., 2013). Although neurofibrillary tangles and plaques may form independently, recent studies have provided evidence that Aβ promotes tau abnormalities through direct protein-protein interactions (Guo et al., 2006; Soto and Pritzkow, 2018). Neuropathological studies have also shown that nearly half of AD patients exhibit TDP-43 deposition (Uchikado et al., 2006) and/or aggregation (Josephs et al., 2016). In PD and associated synucleinopathies, the prevalence of mixed pathologies is even higher: approximately 80% of patients show detectable Aβ deposition, around 50% exhibit tau aggregation, and about 30% display TDP-43 deposition (Spires-Jones et al., 2017).

chunks.section: Protein Misfolding, Aggregation, Conformation, and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: The extensive pathological overlap in protein aggregations across distinct CNS regions in different NDs may be closely linked to the complex and multifaceted phenotypes of these disorders. Evidence from pathological and clinical analyses suggests that NDs can be triggered by a specific type of protein aggregation that acts as a driving force for the initial clinical manifestations. This initial aggregation leads to the accumulation of other protein aggregates, inducing secondary changes that alter or expand CNS impairments and clinical symptoms (Spires-Jones et al., 2017). Supporting this hypothesis is the fact that the pathogenesis of PD typically presents with classic motor symptoms in the early stages. Over time, the majority of PD patients develop a series of non-motor symptoms and more complex motor symptoms (Poewe et al., 2017; Outeiro et al., 2019; Chahine et al., 2023). The emergence of these symptoms is speculated to be induced by the initial protein aggregation. Additionally, protein deposits in certain CNS regions can contribute to non-motor symptoms on their own, as some of these symptoms arise from Lewy bodies that contain pathogenic protein aggregates (Poewe et al., 2017; Outeiro et al., 2019; Tsoi et al., 2023).

chunks.section: Protein Misfolding, Aggregation, Conformation, and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: Although the occurrence of cross-seeding via direct interactions among several common pathogenic proteins, such as tau, Aβ (Murakami and Ono, 2022), TDP-43, and SOD1 (Koch et al., 2016; Pokrishevsky et al., 2024), has been confirmed by in vitro experiments, it is important to note that there are alternative explanations for the cross-seeding observed in different NDs (Montalbano et al., 2020). Other factors contributing to cross-seeding include enhanced cell vulnerability, impaired clearance mechanisms, neuroinflammation, and the activation of indirect signaling transduction pathways, all of which can lead to increased protein misfolding (Spires-Jones et al., 2017). Furthermore, properties closely associated with seeding or cross-seeding, such as the stereotypical pathological progressions observed in certain NDs, should also be considered, as they may help explain the existence of selective neuronal vulnerability (Lee et al., 2024a, b).

chunks.section: Protein Misfolding, Aggregation, Conformation, and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: Protein aggregations in NDs can also be cross-seeded by aggregates from systemic disorders related to peripheral tissues. One of the strongest pieces of evidence supporting this mechanism is the interaction between AD and type 2 diabetes (T2D) (Hamzé et al., 2022; Michailidis et al., 2022). T2D is associated with the accumulation of islet amyloid polypeptide in the pancreas, and interestingly, T2D patients are at an increased risk of developing AD (Kim and Feldman, 2015), with approximately 80% of AD cases showing a history of T2D or abnormal glucose metabolism (Ryu et al., 2019). Transgenic animal models expressing both human Aβ and IAPP have been shown to have exacerbated AD-like pathologies (Moreno-Gonzalez et al., 2017). Both islet amyloid polypeptide and amyloid plaques co-localized in parenchymal deposits in the brains of these animals, suggesting Aβ and IAPP may interact directly to worsen diseases such as T2D and AD. Furthermore, inoculating IAPP aggregates from the pancreas into the brains of AD transgenic mice resulted in more severe AD pathologies and significantly greater memory impairment compared to control mice (Moreno-Gonzalez et al., 2017). Cross-seeding mechanisms have been confirmed in in vitro experiments, which demonstrated that the presence of IAPP seeds accelerated Aβ aggregations, with both types of peptides forming partially similar fibrils (Moreno-Gonzalez et al., 2017).

chunks.section: Protein Misfolding, Aggregation, Conformation, and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: In recent years, emerging evidence suggests that pathological protein aggregations leading to NDs may be initiated by seeds from functional amyloids (Ross and Poirier, 2004). Multiple proteins have been found to naturally aggregate as non-pathogenic amyloid structures that contribute to protein function modulation or even the acquisition of novel biological activities. These functional amyloids are present in organisms ranging from bacteria to humans (Fowler et al., 2007), indicating that the formation of these structures is not necessarily linked to pathological processes. The possibility that the protein misfolding and aggregations that cause NDs might be triggered by cross-seeding with functional amyloids has not been extensively researched (Chiti and Dobson, 2006). However, one recent study reported that bacterial amyloids might exert effects on such aggregations (Soto and Pritzkow, 2018).

chunks.section: Protein Misfolding, Aggregation, Conformation, and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: Calmodulin-binding proteins and calcium dysregulation are early and persistent factors in the pathogenesis of multiple NDs. Calmodulin serves as the initial and primary sensor of cytosolic calcium levels and has garnered interest as a major regulator of neurodegeneration due to its crucial role in calcium dysregulation and its effects on calcium signaling. Calmodulin plays a critical role in NDs by binding to proteins and regulating protein abundance, with many of its functions participating across several NDs. Notably, calmodulin has a common function in the production of protein aggregates in AD, PD, ALS, FTD, HD, and LBD. Calmodulin not only binds to Aβ, pTau, and mutant HTT but also converts them into toxic protein aggregates by regulating transglutaminase 2. Additionally, multiple calmodulin-binding proteins have been suggested as promising primary drug targets, and using combinations of these proteins could offer insights into the pathogenesis and progression of several NDs (O’Day, 2023), including AD (O’Day et al., 2015), PD, ALS, FTD, HD, and LBD (Bourdenx et al., 2017; Azam et al., 2021; O’Day and Huber, 2022).

chunks.section: Propagation of Protein Aggregation and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text:
The present findings in the field of protein propagation have revealed that aggregating proteins can self-propagate through seeding—the spread of pathological abnormalities between cells and tissues in a manner similar to the behavior of infectious prions in prionoid diseases. These findings have important implications for our understanding of the mechanisms that initiate and drive NDs (Vaquer-Alicea and Diamond, 2019).

A growing body of evidence demonstrates that prionoid protein propagation of misfolded proteins is one of the core mechanisms underlying NDs. The discovery of several common pathogenic elements, pathological effects, and protein homeostasis involved in the pathogenesis of AD, PD, ALS, and HD has extended the pathogenic processes from neurons to glial cells. These add to the complexity of disease progression and can promote the initiation of protective processes in neural cells that reduce the propagation of pathological protein aggregation (Wells et al., 2019).

chunks.section: Propagation of Protein Aggregation and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: NDs are a group of debilitating disorders primarily characterized by the gradual loss of function and cell death in the CNS and peripheral nervous system. The majority of neurons cannot regenerate to replace dead neural cells, leading to the accumulation of lesions and permanent damage. Although these NDs exhibit various etiologies and pathologies, each type involves the degeneration of distinct neurons and the gradual loss of different nervous functions, with the specific neurons and functional losses varying among the different NDs. Prionoid propagation represents a common and classic form of protein aggregation in the pathogenesis of NDs and is driven by template-directed protein misfolding. Prionoids, similar to prion proteins, utilize a seeding mechanism to induce template-based conformational changes that transform native proteins into their prionoid form. Prionoid transmission then occurs between host neurons. The seeding properties of prionoids resemble those of aggregated proteins, with misfolded and aggregated proteins spreading to neighboring neural cells in a manner akin to that of infectious prion particles (Aguzzi and Rajendran, 2009; Aguzzi and Lakkaraju, 2016; Wells et al., 2019).

chunks.section: Propagation of Protein Aggregation and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: Multiple common NDs, such as AD, PD, HD, and ALS, are now considered to be based on prionoid pathology. Despite extensive research, our understanding of the details of prionoid propagation processes in the context of NDs remains limited. In addition, there are few effective treatments or cures for prionoid diseases. Prionoid proteins can misfold into multiple pathological conformations, which can then propagate to normal proteins, and this template function is facilitated by the exposure of hydrophobic amino acid side chains that are normally buried within the protein’s interior (Prusiner, 1998). Prionoid proteins typically possess two common structures, β sheets and polypeptides, making them prone to forming aggregates, including fibrils (Aguzzi and Lakkaraju, 2016). These properties of prionoid proteins promote the intracellular aggregation of misfolded proteins, facilitating the formation of stable inclusion bodies by recruiting normal proteins. Extracellular protein fibrils can also enter and aggregate in other cells, enabling the intercellular transmissibility of prionoid proteins (Aguzzi and Lakkaraju, 2016).

chunks.section: Propagation of Protein Aggregation and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: Various NDs demonstrate different mechanisms of pathogenesis, yet they all ultimately result in distinctive neuronal death within the CNS, leading to symptoms associated with the loss or gain of neuronal functions. The major gain-of-function mechanisms include prionoid protein misfolding, which is often accompanied by some functional losses (Allison et al., 2017). Recently, the mechanisms underlying the gain and loss of neuronal functions have been identified as participating in the pathogenesis of AD, HD, and ALS (Leighton and Allison, 2016). Generally, gain-of-function mechanisms include the activation of autophagy, protein aggregation, axon dysfunction, and cellular stress, while loss-of-function mechanisms involve protein sequestration, synaptic dysfunction, and DNA impairment. Symptoms of NDs may arise from the loss and/or gain of functions, contributing to conditions such as denervation, mitochondrial dysfunction, excitotoxicity, and oxidative stress (Wells et al., 2019).

chunks.section: Propagation of Protein Aggregation and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: When a pathological Aβ protein homogenate derived from the brains of AD patients was injected into APP model transgenic mice, the animals exhibited time- and concentration-dependent development of plaques as well as neuronal pathology (Meyer-Luehmann et al., 2006). The Aβ protein is known to be capable of propagating directly between the neurites of interfacing neurons (Nath et al., 2012). Similarly, extracellularly aggregated tau protein, but not tau protein monomers, propagates from the extracellular space to the intracellular environment during the pathogenesis of AD (Frost and Diamond, 2009). Microglia package the misfolded and aggregated tau proteins into nanoscale vesicles, which are secreted as exosomes by all mammalian cells. While this process can help control the accumulation of pathogenic proteins within cells, it can also propagate aberrant proteins between adjacent brain regions, thereby accelerating the spread of pathological proteins in the pathogenesis of NDs (Asai et al., 2015).

chunks.section: Propagation of Protein Aggregation and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: Similarly, soluble endogenous proteins are recruited and converted into fibrils, which propagate among cells via the intracellular spaces and form Lewy body-like aggregates in the cytoplasm during the pathogenesis of PD (Luk et al., 2009). Evidence suggests that these proteins are secreted into the extracellular space via atypical exocytotic processes under normal physiological conditions. However, the amounts secreted significantly increase due to both proteasomal and mitochondrial dysfunctions in PD pathogenesis (Rocha et al., 2018), and this extracellular internalization may lead to seeded endogenous aggregation as the disease progresses (Rodriguez et al., 2018; Hoffmann et al., 2019). While the precise mechanisms of the cellular protein internalization involved in PD pathogenesis remain a topic of debate, potential mechanisms include endocytosis, micropinocytosis, and cell surface-protein-mediated uptake (Rodriguez et al., 2018).

chunks.section: Propagation of Protein Aggregation and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: Large internalizing aggregates of HTT polyQ in the neurons of HD patients bind to cellular plasma membranes to form HTT inclusion bodies. The aggregations of HTT protein, along with their self-sustaining seeding and fragmentation abilities, are inherited as cells differentiate (Bonavita et al., 2023). Experimental evidence shows that mutant HTT (mHTT) protein may propagate across synapses in transgenic mice and Drosophila melanogaster models (Pecho-Vrieseling et al., 2014; Babcock and Ganetzky, 2015). The propagated HTT protein can either recruit endogenous HTT monomers and oligomers to propagate seed aggregations (Sancho et al., 2011) or translocate to the nuclei, resulting in cell death. Interestingly, shorter peptides with fewer polyQ repeats are no less toxic than longer peptides, suggesting that the pathological roles of polyQ repeats may be more related to their aggregation efficiencies than their toxicities (Wellington et al., 2002).

chunks.section: Propagation of Protein Aggregation and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: In ALS, mutant SOD1 (mSOD1)-positive aggregates form granules with a fibril morphology rich in β-sheet structures, facilitating the template-based misfolding of wild-type SOD1 protein in fALS patients and fALS transgenic mouse models (Kato et al., 2000). In in vitro experiments, mSOD1 aggregation was shown to accelerate or seed the aggregation of soluble SOD1 protein (Brotherton et al., 2013). The intercellular propagation of mSOD1 requires the aggregated proteins to be released into extracellular spaces, followed by their internalization into other cells through micropinocytosis, which allows the internalized protein aggregates to seed endogenous SOD1 aggregation (Cicardi et al., 2021). The ability of self-propagation via fibril fragments suggests that fibril breakage may be a critical component of the self-propagation process (Lee and Kim, 2015; Wells et al., 2019).

NDs are chronic, progressive conditions primarily affecting the CNS and are characterized by the occurrence of progressive damage to neuronal structures and functions. Current evidence indicates that neuroinflammation plays a significant role in the pathophysiological processes underlying NDs and is closely associated with major conditions, including AD (Calsolaro and Edison, 2016), PD (Wang et al., 2022), ALS (McCauley and Baloh, 2019), HD (Soto, 2003), FTD (Bright et al., 2019), and LBD (Surendranathan et al., 2015).

The roles of cellular and molecular pathways, along with the activation of inflammatory mediators within neuroinflammatory systems, are critical biological processes involved in the pathogenesis of AD (Ahluwalia and Vellas, 2003; Calsolaro and Edison, 2016). AD is characterized by the significant accumulation of Aβ protein; however, we still lack a clear understanding of its causative pathogenesis. The disappointing results from several anti-amyloid treatment strategies have raised concerns that the amyloid cascade hypothesis alone cannot fully explain the neuronal damage associated with AD pathogenesis.

Neuroinflammation plays significant roles in the pathogenesis of NDs, although there is ongoing debate about whether its effects are protective or harmful (Calsolaro and Edison, 2016). The deposition of Aβ protein in the context of AD initiates a cascade of neuroinflammatory responses, including the activation of microglial cells and astrocytes, which leads to the expression of various pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory cytokines. These neuroinflammatory reactions are crucial pathophysiological processes in AD. Pro-inflammatory cytokine signaling exerts multiple effects, both neurodegenerative and neuroprotective, during the disease’s progression. The induction of pro-inflammatory signals results in the release of immunological mediators that affect neuronal function and lead to cell death, and a reduction in anti-inflammatory mechanisms can further activate neuroinflammation. Various pathways, including nuclear factor kappa B (NF-κB), p38 MAPK, Akt/mTOR, caspase, nitric oxide, and COX, are involved in stimulating neural immune cells, such as astrocytes and microglia, to release inflammatory cytokines, including tumor necrosis factor, interleukins, and chemokines, all of which participate in the pathogenesis of AD. Research has found that PPAR-γ agonists enhance the phagocytosis of Aβ protein (Mandrekar-Colucci et al., 2012) and decrease the levels of inflammatory cytokine IL-1β (Thakur et al., 2023). Moreover, gut microbiota, and its connection to neuroinflammation, (Thakur et al., 2023) also plays a role in the pathogenesis of AD.

PD is primarily caused by the aberrant accumulation of pathogenic proteins in dopaminergic neurons of the substantia nigra in the midbrain, and the consequent neuroinflammation exerts key effects in the neurodegenerative pathogenesis of PD. Neuroinflammatory processes that contribute to the neurodegeneration involve the activation of microglial cells, the upregulation of pro-inflammatory factors, and disturbances in gut microbiota, all of which facilitate the release of inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1β, IL-2, IL-6, IL-10, and TNF-α. Neuroinflammation has been closely linked to the pathogenesis of PD, as evidenced by increased levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines in the blood and/or CSF (Cohen et al., 2024). Activated microglial cells secrete several pro-inflammatory cytokines, including TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 (Dzamko, 2023), as well as major histocompatibility complex class II (Kulski et al., 2024), all of which are associated with neuronal damage in the context of PD.

The abnormal aggregation of insoluble proteins exerts significant effects in the pathogenesis of PD. Protein misfolding contributes to the dysregulation of microglial Toll-like receptor (TLR)2 or TLR4-mediated signaling pathways, which are associated with pathogen-associated molecular patterns and damage-associated molecular patterns. This dysregulation activates myeloid differentiation primary response 88 and NFκB, ultimately triggering the production of TNF-α and IL-1β (Li et al., 2021). Neuroinflammation is activated through immunological reactions involved in the degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in PD. Additionally, gut microbiota, metabolites, and cytokines may play roles in the interplay of processes underlying the pathogenesis of PD (Salim et al., 2023).

Neuroinflammation is a pathophysiological reaction involving resident glial cells, such as astrocytes and microglia, as well as circulating immune cells, e.g., monocytes, neutrophils, and lymphocytes, in response to infections, injury, or the degeneration of the CNS. Neuroinflammation is primarily characterized by innate immune responses rather than adaptive immune responses in the pathogenesis of ALS, similar to that of other NDs (Prinz and Priller, 2017). In addition to astrocytes and microglial cells, T cells and non-resident innate immune cells, such as dendritic cells, macrophages, and mast cells, are also activated during the pathogenesis of ALS. However, the roles of these immune cells remain poorly understood in the context of this condition (McCauley and Baloh, 2019).

HD is a genetic neurodegenerative disorder caused by an expansion of CAG repeats in the HTT gene. The clinical manifestations of HD include movement, cognitive, and behavioral dysfunctions. Inflammation occurs in HD patients prior to symptom onset, with reactive microglial cells, astrocytes, and inflammatory factors frequently observed in the pathogenesis, indicating changes in microglial and astrocyte function are involved in HD onset (Soto, 2003). The activation of the innate immune system contributes to rapid general responses to various pathogens and stressors, while the adaptive immune system generates highly specific responses primarily modulated by T or B lymphocytes. There is a considerable amount of evidence supporting the activation of innate immune cells within the CNS, while fewer studies have reported on the roles of the adaptive immune system in HD. Inflammation may be stimulated by glial responses to neurodegeneration or by the immune cell activation that results from mHTT expression; in addition, these two pathways may exert synergistic effects in HD (Soto, 2003). In general, neither the mechanisms nor the contributions of inflammation to the pathogenesis of HD have been fully elucidated, and the roles of neuroinflammation in HD are less well understood than those in other HDs.

FTD encompasses a spectrum of NDs characterized by various pathological features with genetic variability and complex pathogenic mechanisms. As the study of pathogenesis of FTD progresses, evidence is mounting that suggests neuroinflammation plays a role in its development. This includes cortical inflammation, microglial activation, astrogliosis, and the abnormal expression of inflammation-related proteins. Moreover, significant evidence from genetic studies indicates that multiple FTD-related genes are related to neuroinflammation within the pathogenesis of FTD (Bright et al., 2019; De Marchi et al., 2023).

Neuroinflammation is increasingly being recognized as a key factor in the pathogenesis of NDs, but it remains unclear whether it plays a protective or damaging role in LBD. Research on LBD has provided substantial evidence for the involvement of inflammatory pathologies in its neurodegeneration (Noori et al., 2021; Becchi et al., 2023), with much of the evidence being derived from studies on LBD pathology and biomarkers (Griffin et al., 2006; Mrak and Griffin, 2007).

Mitochondrial dysfunction is proposed to play a significant role in various NDs, including AD (Hroudová et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2020; Kalani et al., 2023), PD (Gautier et al., 2014), ALS (Cozzolino and Carrì, 2012), HD (Carmo et al., 2018), FTD (Anoar et al., 2021), and LBD (Spano et al., 2015). Increasing evidence suggests that defects in mitochondrial metabolism, particularly within electron transport chains, exert key effects in the pathogenesis of NDs. Structural and functional damage to mitochondria hampers their ability to generate sufficient ATP and, conversely, leads to the production of pro-apoptotic factors and ROS, which are involved in the initial stages of multiple mitochondrial disorders, including NDs. Mitochondrial dysfunction can be triggered by mutations in mitochondrial and nuclear DNA sequences that code for mitochondrial components or by environmental factors. Common pathogenic mechanisms associated with mitochondrial damage include ROS generation, impairments in mitochondrial dynamics, the dysfunction of electron transport chain complexes, and interactions between pathogenic proteins and mitochondria (Hroudová et al., 2014). Normal mitochondrial function is critical for maintaining neuronal structure and function due to the high energy consumption of neurons. In the context of AD, mitochondrial dysfunction disrupts mitochondrial autophagy, which is responsible for eliminating dysfunctional mitochondria. This impairment directly or indirectly induces high levels of iron in neural cells and subsequently interferes with mitochondrial autophagy and mtDNA release. This cascade of events leads to the production of pro-inflammatory factors and activates cyclic guanosine monophosphate–adenosine monophosphate synthase and stimulators of interferon genes pathways, promoting and exacerbating the development of NDs, including AD (Kalani et al., 2023).

NDs comprise a group of disorders characterized by progressive neuronal dysfunction and degeneration. NDs are highly heterogeneous, affecting various neuronal subpopulations and involving multiple origins and pathogenic mechanisms. Despite this diversity, evidence for some common pathological features among these diseases has emerged. Increasingly, observations are being made that suggest mitochondrial dysfunction plays a pivotal role in neurodegeneration across various NDs, including AD, PD, ALS, HD, FTD, and LBD. Impairments in mitochondrial function can occur at any stage of ND pathogenesis. Within the spectrum of NDs, current evidence indicates that mitochondrial dysfunction exerts similar effects during neuronal demise. PD has shown a particularly strong association with mitochondrial damage events, such as reduced complex I activity and oxidative impairment, in postmortem studies (Camilleri and Vassallo, 2014; Gautier et al., 2014). Additionally, the pathogenesis of PD is closely linked to mutations in more than five distinct genes, including parkin, DJ-1, PINK1, and LRRK2. Understanding the pathogenesis of neurodegeneration in selectively vulnerable dopaminergic neurons in PD could provide valuable insights into potential treatment options. To investigate these mechanisms, numerous in vitro and in vivo models of PD have been developed to mimic the disease’s insults. These model studies have yielded valuable data regarding the damaging mechanisms affecting dopaminergic neurons, including mitochondrial dysfunction (Gautier et al., 2014). While PD is recognized as an ND related to aging, the exact relationship between aging and PD remains unclear. Mitochondrial dysfunction is considered a major pathogenic factor and a central part of the etiology and is closely associated with the selective death of dopaminergic neurons in PD. Specifically, mitochondrial dysfunction contributes to various aspects of PD pathogenesis, including ROS production, calcium overload, the activation of neuroinflammatory pathways, apoptosis, mitophagy, mitochondrial biogenesis, and mitochondrial dynamics (Moradi Vastegani et al., 2023).

Abundant evidence implicates mitochondrial dysfunction as a major element in the pathogenesis of ALS. Recent studies suggest that various interrelated aspects of mitochondrial dysfunction play significant roles in ALS pathogenesis, challenging previous perceptions (Cunha-Oliveira et al., 2024; Landry et al., 2024). Mitochondrial dysfunction alters numerous physiological functions and properties of mitochondria, and these alterations may contribute to the internal susceptibilities of long-lived and postmitotic motor neurons to energy deficits, calcium mishandling, and oxidative stress, ultimately facilitating motor neuron death in the context of ALS (Cozzolino and Carrì, 2012).

Mitochondrial dysfunction, which has been identified as an early factor contributing to the selective neurodegeneration of the striatum and cerebral cortex in HD, is driven by a polyglutamine expansion in the pathogenic HD gene. Mitochondria synthesize energy and several metabolites, and their maximal bioenergetic performance relies on their transportation along microtubules. They also regulate intracellular calcium homeostasis through interactions with the endoplasmic reticulum and produce free radicals, which participate in cell death processes. The majority of these mitochondrial activities have been shown to play roles in the pathogenesis of HD, potentially contributing to neuronal dysfunction in this condition (Carmo et al., 2018).

FTD and ALS are NDs characterized by a combined decline in movement and cognitive function. Although these two conditions exhibit distinct clinical manifestations, they are considered to be similar subtypes within the neurodegenerative spectrum, as they show significant overlap in their pathogeneses. Mitochondrial dysfunction is one these overlapping mechanisms and has been linked to both FTD and ALS. Several studies have identified mitochondrial abnormalities in various animal models, such as the Drosophila transgenic model, and partially elucidated the molecular mechanisms underlying the toxicity of gene mutations associated with FTD and ALS pathogenesis (Anoar et al., 2021; Nemtsova et al., 2023).

The main hallmark of LBD pathology is the formation of Lewy bodies, which are composed of aberrant α-syn in specific regions of the CNS. The CNS is particularly vulnerable to oxidative damage, such as that caused by ROS, which can easily lead to NDs. Comparative studies on the potential pathogenesis of LBD have been conducted using various models of mitochondrial dysfunction (Gu et al., 1998; Jin et al., 2024). The results from these studies confirmed the hypothesis that α-syn damages mitochondrial functions and increases sensitivity to cell death through factors such as cytochrome C (Hashimoto et al., 1999). Mitochondria play crucial roles in neurodegeneration and the production of Lewy bodies. The abnormal accumulation of α-syn can lead to the release of cytochrome C, which subsequently activates apoptosis cascades (Spano et al., 2015). LBD and AD are the most common forms of dementia in older adult populations. Previous studies have shown that cognitive alterations in NDs are related to synaptic loss, particularly via processes involving synaptic proteins such as α-syn. Furthermore, recent research has demonstrated that the aberrant aggregation and accumulation of the synaptic protein α-syn are critical for plaque formation in the pathogenesis of AD (Del Giudice et al., 2024). The presence of Lewy bodies in the pathogenesis of both LBD (Leak et al., 2024) and PD (Bayati and McPherson, 2024) further reinforces the hypothesis that α-syn plays a crucial role in the pathogenesis of NDs. Although the mechanisms underlying the functional alterations and α-syn aggregation that lead to neurodegeneration in diseases such as AD, PD, and LBD are not yet fully understood, recent evidence suggests α-syn has roles in synaptic lesions and neurotoxicity, potentially through the generation of amyloid-like fibrils and the dysfunction of mitochondria (Hashimoto and Masliah, 1999).

chunks.section: Axon Transportation Defect and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: Axonal transport refers to the long-range, bidirectional transport of cellular components within axons, which allows cargo to be moved from the cell body to the terminals (anterograde axonal transport) and from the synapse back to the cell body (retrograde axonal transport). The directionality of cargo movement is controlled by the molecular motor proteins kinesin and dynein, which operate based on their polarity. Kinesins are essential for synaptic function and facilitate the anterograde transport of cargo, while dynein governs retrograde axonal transport. These molecular motor proteins are responsible for carrying various types of cargo, including synaptic vesicles, organelles, and even RNA (Brady ST, Morfini, 2017).

chunks.section: Axon Transportation Defect and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: Neurons communicate within neural systems by transmitting information along their axons to synapses. The normal function of axonal transport plays a crucial role in the transportation of proteins, organelles, vesicles, neurotrophins, and other elements between neural cells and between neural cells and body cells. Molecular motors, i.e., kinesin and dynein, are responsible for transporting these elements along the neuronal axon. Proper axonal transport is essential for maintaining neuronal viability and function (Chevalier-Larsen and Holzbaur, 2006; Peng et al., 2020), and defects in axonal transport are considered significant contributors to the development and progression of NDs (Chevalier-Larsen and Holzbaur, 2006; De Vos and Hafezparast, 2017; Berth and Lloyd, 2023; Vasconcelos et al., 2023).

chunks.section: Axon Transportation Defect and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: The core pathogenesis of NDs involves the misfolding, fibrillization, and aggregation of pathogenic proteins, which produce the hallmark lesions associated with specific NDs. Impaired axonal transport is believed to play a mechanistic role in several common NDs, such as AD (Wang et al., 2015; Vasconcelos et al., 2023), PD (Berth and Lloyd, 2023), ALS (Chevalier-Larsen and Holzbaur, 2006; De Vos and Hafezparast, 2017), HD (Roze et al., 2010), FTD (Adalbert et al., 2018; Combs et al., 2021), and LBD (Yamamoto, 2021). However, this hypothesis has not been thoroughly validated through extensive research. Mutations in genes encoding the motor proteins responsible for axonal transport have provided direct evidence that defective transport is a contributor to the pathogenesis of NDs, and this has been further supported by findings from neurodegeneration animal models (Roy et al., 2005). Increasing evidence suggests that Aβ peptides, the characteristic pathogenic proteins of AD, disrupt axonal transport, contributing to the development of the disease. There appear to be both direct and indirect relationships between defective axonal transport and the pathogenesis of AD (Vasconcelos et al., 2023).

chunks.section: Axon Transportation Defect and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: A substantial body of evidence indicates that defects in axonal transport play critical roles in the pathogenesis of AD. Axonal transport is crucial for maintaining neuronal function and requires four basic components, cytoskeletons, motor proteins, ATP, and adaptor proteins, which work together to ensure the specific transport of materials and the active regulation of movement. Pathogenic factors associated with AD disrupt these four components of axonal transport through various signaling pathways, leading to axonal transport deficits. These factors interact in overlapping, synergistic, and cascading pathways during pathogenesis (Wang et al., 2015). Axonal transport defects have been found to initiate neurodegeneration or axonopathy, resulting in neuronal death in several common NDs. Although the exact mechanisms underlying axonal degeneration remain unclear, multiple elements involved in axonal transport appear to be potential contributors to neurodegeneration in the pathogenesis of NDs such as AD. The destabilization and fragmentation of microtubules, loss of molecular motor function, deregulation of motor activity, and protein aggregation—exacerbated by deficits in cellular scavenging pathways such as the autophagy-lysosome pathway and the ubiquitin-proteasome pathway—can induce axonal degeneration and disrupt axonal transport across many NDs. The maintenance of microtubules and regulation of motor proteins are essential for normal axonal transport and the maturation of autophagosomes during axonal growth. The disruption of long-distance transport further contributes to axonal degeneration in the pathogenesis of AD (White et al., 2016).

chunks.section: Axon Transportation Defect and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: PD is an ND characterized by prominent damage to dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta. Misfolded presynaptic proteins aggregate during axonal transport, forming Lewy bodies within the cytoplasm of dopaminergic neurons, which contributes to neuronal damage in the pathogenesis of PD. Postmortem analyses and animal model studies have determined that axonal dysfunction, a loss of presynaptic terminals, and a loss of motor proteins contribute to deficits in axonal transport. As a result, misfolded proteins cannot be transported out of neuronal bodies, leading to their progressive aggregation in the cytoplasm and the formation of the Lewy body inclusions involved in the pathogenesis of PD (Volpicelli-Daley, 2017).

chunks.section: Axon Transportation Defect and Neurodegenerative Diseases
chunks.text: ALS, FTD, and LBD also share similar pathogeneses, characterized by the progressive aggregation of misfolded pathogenic proteins such as SOD1, FUS, C9ORF72, and tau. These aggregations represent hallmark pathological alterations and key pathogenic elements in the development of these NDs. Therefore, it is hypothesized that deficits in axonal transport hinder the removal of these aberrant misfolded proteins from the neuronal body and cytoplasm, accelerating their aggregation and toxicity to neural cells, including neurons. This damage to specific neuronal populations may contribute to the onset of these NDs (Chevalier-Larsen and Holzbaur, 2006; Berth and Lloyd, 2023). While our understanding of the precise pathogeneses of these diseases is limited, a deficit in axonal transport appears to be a significant pathophysiological feature in the development of several common NDs and thus warrants further exploration.

The human gut microbiota, which includes a diverse array of microorganisms, engages in bidirectional communication with the CNS through the brain–gut axis. This interaction exerts significant effects on physiological processes and the pathogenesis of multiple diseases, including neurodegenerative disorders. Increasingly, evidence is highlighting the critical role of gut microbes in both normal brain function and pathological conditions, particularly in NDs such as AD, PD, and ALS (Quigley, 2017; Chen et al., 2021; Loh et al., 2024).

The aberrant aggregation of toxic proteins is a major cause of NDs such as AD (Jiang et al., 2017), PD (Hirayama and Ohno, 2021; Pant et al., 2022), ALS (McCombe et al., 2019), HD (Khoshnan, 2024), FTD (Hashim and Makpol, 2022), and LBD (Mateo et al., 2023). As research on the relationship between the pathogenesis of NDs and gut microbiota deepens, it has been suggested that a loss of gut microbiota homeostasis plays a significant role in the pathogenesis of these diseases and is thus drawing increasing attention from researchers. Studies have reported that disturbances in gut microbiota are closely associated with the accumulation of toxic proteins in NDs (Hashim and Makpol, 2022; Zhang et al., 2023a,b; Ekwudo et al., 2024; Lee et al., 2024a, b; Mateo et al., 2024); some of these pathogenic proteins may be directly produced by gut microbiota and transported to the CNS via vagus nerve fibers. Additionally, toxic proteins generated by gut microbiota can enter the peripheral circulation through the intestinal barrier or cross the blood–brain barrier, leading to neuroinflammation and the production of inflammatory factors and cytokines that further induce the generation of toxic proteins. These toxic proteins activate microglia, contributing to the development of NDs. Interestingly, certain gut microbiota and their metabolites have also been shown to exert neuroprotective effects. Therefore, disruptions to the dynamic homeostasis of gut microbiota may represent a common underlying mechanism in the pathogenesis of NDs (Quigley, 2017; Loh et al., 2024).

The gut microbiota consists of complex communities of microbial species, and alterations in these communities can affect not only various gut diseases but also numerous CNS disorders, including AD. A hallmark of AD pathogenesis is the progressive cognitive impairment driven by the accumulation of Aβ.
