Compared to Monarch Initiative, DGIdb is a relational SQL database that uses Entrez, HGNC and GO identifiers for genes, and mostly ChEMBL and Wikidata identifiers for drugs [38].

chunks.section: Bio-semantics-based computational approaches to drug repurposing
chunks.text: The resulting Reep network of genes and drugs is used to train a ML model and predict newly repurposed drugs to the disease of interest [31].

The pipeline was tested against HD as a case study. The input for the pipeline is Monarch Initiative’s proprietary URI for the disease: ‘MONDO:0007739’.

Information from the network of biological associations to the disease of interest are stored as dictionaries (i.e. nodes) or list of dictionaries (i.e. edges, which store subject node, relationship, object node and notes). Here’s an example:

Only a portion of the nodes are useful for the drug re-purposing: anything other then diseases, phenotypes, genes and drugs are not used at latter steps (Tables 1 and 2). Moreover, not all drugs can be used during the drugsimilarity.py step since not all the URIs can be mapped to known IDs to be converted into SMILES. Of the 69,907 drugs available in DGIdb, only 12,872 (18.4%) have ChEMBL or Wikidata identifiers that are potentially mappable to SMILES representations (Section “Database APIs for biological, gene-drug associations anddrug-drug similarity”), and of these, 11,603 (16.6%) were successfully mapped (i.e. 11,596 from ChEMBL and 7 from Wikidata). This mapping limitation affects the drug similarity network construction, but it does not impact the primary gene-drug association predictions, as the drug similarity step serves to enhance negative sample selection rather than define the prediction space. Nodes and edges from the network of associations to HD that are used for drug repurposing are listed in Tables 1 and 2—with counts from a reference run performed on October 7th 2025. This represents a substantial expansion compared to the network constructed by Reep, which comprised 2189 nodes and 17,467 edges using June 2022 API versions [31], demonstrating the value of updated data infrastructure and the inclusion of all DGIdb drugs—rather than only those already associated with the disease of interest (Section “Database APIs for biological, gene-drug associations anddrug-drug similarity”).

Compared to the full network (Fig. 1), the smaller network (Fig. 2) of 1000 (26.50%) genes, 600 drugs (29.97%) and 970 other mappable nodes (10.00%) generated with the same distribution of centrality scores had much lower edge density: 267 gene-to-drug edges (1.62%), 131 gene-to-gene edges (4.81%), and 513 other edges (4.81%) (Table 9). Appendix A presents the validation of the subset network as a representation of the full network for the purpose of iteration-consensus testing.Fig. 1Full network of associations to HD: highly interconnected nodes (mostly diseases in dark blue, genes in light blue and phenotypes in blue) at the centre and less interconnected nodes (mostly drugs in red and other entities in orange) at the fringes joined by biological associations (blue lines) and gene-to-drug edges (red lines); the full network comprises 15,476 nodes and 32,520 edges from the October 7, 2025 data snapshot (Tables 1 and 2)Fig. 2Network made up of a subset of nodes from the full network in Figure 1: generated according to the random sampling method described in Section “Pipeline upgrades”; nodes are sampled to retain the same proportions according to labels in Table 1; statistical validation (Appendix A) confirms preservation of betweenness centrality distributions (i.e. p>0.05, KS-test) and representative topology, enabling iteration-based evaluation while reducing computational requirements and time frame

Full network of associations to HD: highly interconnected nodes (mostly diseases in dark blue, genes in light blue and phenotypes in blue) at the centre and less interconnected nodes (mostly drugs in red and other entities in orange) at the fringes joined by biological associations (blue lines) and gene-to-drug edges (red lines); the full network comprises 15,476 nodes and 32,520 edges from the October 7, 2025 data snapshot (Tables 1 and 2)

Network made up of a subset of nodes from the full network in Figure 1: generated according to the random sampling method described in Section “Pipeline upgrades”; nodes are sampled to retain the same proportions according to labels in Table 1; statistical validation (Appendix A) confirms preservation of betweenness centrality distributions (i.e. p>0.05, KS-test) and representative topology, enabling iteration-based evaluation while reducing computational requirements and time frame

The results from the generation of negative samples (Section “Generation of semantically-valid negative samples based on centrality measures”) vary depending on the centrality measure used: nodes that score higher will lead to more edges containing it being deemed valid negative samples. Gene node ‘HGNC:644’ (AR, Androgen Receptor) for example is the top scoring according to the degree centrality measure (0.0479), yet it is not according to eigenvector and in-betweenness centrality measures (respectively 0.2930 with max=0.3143, and 0.0739 with max=0.0852).

Moreover, this step still takes into consideration hub nodes that are not relevant for this purpose: genes such as ‘HGNC:11998’ (TP53) (i.e. a transcription factor for apoptosis that is silenced in tumors) are highly interconnected because of their nature of home-keeping genes. Yet they cannot be automatically ignored due to the lack of a database that annotates them accordingly and that uses an API for mapping call requests.

To validate the contribution of our combined centrality and similarity-based approach, we performed an ablation study comparing four negative sampling strategies on the full HD network using GCN: random negatives as a baseline, similarity-only, centrality-only, and ‘combined’ (i.e. centrality+similarity). All strategies achieved high discrimination (i.e. AUROC 0.91−0.92, Table 3), with the combined approach demonstrating the best performance across metrics (i.e. AUROC=0.9175, AUPRC=0.6904, F1=0.5503, Table 3), representing modest –yet consistent—improvements over the other sampling methods (e.g. +0.5% AUROC, +1.8% AUPRC, +7.0% F1-score to baseline). The smaller-than-anticipated effect size (Table 3) is attributed to the design of negative samples generation: we only consider gene-to-drug edges for training and prediction, thus even randomly generated valid positive edges are informative enough to support prediction. Nonetheless, the ‘combined’ approach provides the best performance (Table 3). Our approach—mediated also by Node2Vec’s fit to the sparse nature of biological networks (Section “Parallelisation in gene-drug embeddings”)—allows to reduce computational times in the prediction phase (Section “Parallelisation in gene-drug embeddings”). Detailed ablation methodology, comprehensive metrics from Table 3, and comparison plots are provided in Appendix D.Table 3Negative sampling ablation study summary: ‘combined’ (i.e. centrality+similarity) strategy outperforms random baseline with modest but consistent improvementsStrategyAUROCAUPRCF1-scoreRandom (baseline)0.91290.67820.5144Combined0.91750.69040.5503Improvement+0.5%+1.8%+7.0%Full results comparing all four strategies (i.e. random, similarity-only, centrality-only, combined) with comprehensive metrics and analysis are provided in Appendix D

Negative sampling ablation study summary: ‘combined’ (i.e. centrality+similarity) strategy outperforms random baseline with modest but consistent improvements

Full results comparing all four strategies (i.e. random, similarity-only, centrality-only, combined) with comprehensive metrics and analysis are provided in Appendix D

Due to the differences between the two ML methods (i.e. XGBoost and GCN) in the kind of embeddings to feed for training, the time required to building them changes quite a lot. Where the pipeline running the XGBoost method requires to compute any interaction (i.e. present as positive or valid negative, or missing as invalid negative) between gene and drug nodes (e.g. for Huntington’s disease run: 3031 * 4582 = 13,888,042), the pipeline running the GCN method only requires to fill the matrix with the known positive and valid negative associations (i.e. 28,260 + 17,956 = 46,216). GCN embedding generation completes in approximately 48 s, requiring only 0.3% of the time needed for XGBoost embedding generation (i.e. approximately 4.4 h).

Both methods also require the construction of an empty prediction matrix for all possible combinations between genes in the network and all drugs from DGIdb (i.e. 3031 * 69,907 = 211,888,117), but this step take considerably less time due to not having to iterate through edges to determine the presence or absence of an association. Nonetheless, the entire process still takes from half an hour to almost 24 h: for the purpose of saving time, the emb_l=1 parameter allows to load existing embeddings files instead of computing them provided that they exist (i.e. same disease, same date -although the date can be brute forced with the set_date() utility function at the beginning of the function for this specific purpose). On non-specialised computational hardware (e.g. laptop with 10-core CPU @3.2GHz in our case), the complete embedding generation process ranges from 30 min—with GCN on the subset network—to approximately 20 h—with XGBoost on full network. Parallel processing reduces these times by a factor proportional to the number of available cores.

Comparison of ML model computational performance by parameter optimisation setting: each model (i.e. GCN and XGBoost) is run with different parameter optimisation settings (‘ml_depth’) to show the difference in increase of runtime between respectively a GNN and a boosting tree at maximum settings

Quantitative performance metrics in Table 4 demonstrate GCN’s superior discrimination ability across all model configurations, while Table 5 shows average runtimes and CPU usage for the full pipeline on the smaller artificial network with parameter emb_l=1 parameter, therefore the only steps that were performed are the ones involving the ML step. Results show that for the smaller network there is no big difference between using the ‘light’ or the ‘ultralight’ parameter optimisation degrees of complexity, and this is also true when considering just the training and prediction times with the full network.Fig. 3Performance evaluation of GCN single-run predictions on full network: Left panel shows receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve comparing GCN performance (red) against random baseline (dotted black), demonstrating excellent discrimination ability with AUROC=0.9236. Right panel shows precision-recall curve with GCN achieving AUPRC=0.7077 (red) substantially outperforming the random baseline at 0.0909 (dotted black), confirming strong predictive performance for gene-to-drug edge prediction on the HD network

Performance evaluation of GCN single-run predictions on full network: Left panel shows receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve comparing GCN performance (red) against random baseline (dotted black), demonstrating excellent discrimination ability with AUROC=0.9236. Right panel shows precision-recall curve with GCN achieving AUPRC=0.7077 (red) substantially outperforming the random baseline at 0.0909 (dotted black), confirming strong predictive performance for gene-to-drug edge prediction on the HD network

Single-run predictions on the full network demonstrate substantial differences between models in both prediction volume and discrimination ability. Quantitative evaluation using an 80/20 train/test split –with held-out edges from the original network– reveals that GCN achieves excellent raw discrimination (i.e. AUROC=0.9313 [0.9222, 0.9407], AUPRC=0.7280 [0.6979, 0.7577], F1=0.5922) while XGBoost shows limited discrimination ability (i.e. AUROC=0.3958 [0.3778, 0.4135], AUPRC=0.0706 [0.0649, 0.0769], F1=0.1667) (Fig. 3). When probability filtering is applied (i.e. prob>0.65, cluster>0.8), GCN maintains strong performance (i.e. AUROC=0.9236 [0.9139, 0.9323], AUPRC=0.7077 [0.6752, 0.7405], F1=0.5590) with perfect Precision@10, while XGBoost performance shows mixed results (AUROC=0.4152 [0.3950, 0.4364], AUPRC=0.0749 [0.0685, 0.0819], F1=0.1667) (Fig. 3). Compared to Reep’s reported F1-score of 0.867, our models return lower values due to architectural differences: in fact compared to their version that only predicted repurposed drugs from a smaller network only with drugs already associated to the disease of interest, our model takes into consideration a much larger and more sparse network where predictions are hard to be quantified with F1 scores. Moreover, our dual-model approach with consensus-based ranking provides additional robustness through model complementarity as demonstrated in Section “Prediction iterations as a solution for seed variability in ML methods” and in Appendix C.Fig. 4XGBoost single run prediction: Full network of associations to HD from Figure 1 showing newly predicted gene-to-drug edges in green via the XGBoost model as described in Section “Prediction of repurposed drugs in binary models with non-binary datasets”Fig. 5GCN single run prediction: Full network of associations to HD from Figure 1 showing newly predicted gene-to-drug edges in green via the GCN model as described in Section “Prediction of repurposed drugs in binary models with non-binary datasets”

XGBoost single run prediction: Full network of associations to HD from Figure 1 showing newly predicted gene-to-drug edges in green via the XGBoost model as described in Section “Prediction of repurposed drugs in binary models with non-binary datasets”

GCN single run prediction: Full network of associations to HD from Figure 1 showing newly predicted gene-to-drug edges in green via the GCN model as described in Section “Prediction of repurposed drugs in binary models with non-binary datasets”

In terms of prediction volume, starting from a network with 251 positive gene-to-drug edges and approximately 5 M possible gene-to-drug edges, the two models exhibit markedly different behavior: XGBoost returns between 20 and 160 predicted edges respectively with 0.95 and 0.6 probability filter, while GCN returns approximately 120,000 (0.95 filter) to 500,000 (0.65 filter) edges. This indicates that GCN is substantially more lenient in positive prediction even at high probability thresholds.

Convergence analysis across both single-run and iteration-based predictions (Section “Prediction iterations as a solution for seed variability in ML methods”) identified several drugs predicted consistently by both models across different evaluation frameworks, suggesting robust cross-model agreement on high-priority candidates (Fig. 6, detailed analysis in Appendix C).

XGBoost 10-iteration run prediction: Subset network of associations to HD from Fig. 3 showing newly predicted gene-to-drug edges in green via 10 iterations of the XGBoost model as described in Section "Prediction iterations as a solution for seed variability in ML methods" (detailed distribution in Fig. 9)

GCN 200-iteration run prediction: Subset network of associations to HD from Fig. 3 showing newly predicted gene-to-drug edges in green via 200 iterations of the GCN model as described in Section "Prediction iterations as a solution for seed variability in ML methods" (detailed distribution in Fig. 10)

Distribution of occurrences for predicted edges in XGBoost 10-iteration run: predicted edges follow a binomial distribution, with few edges being predicted consistently in all 10 iterations; this distribution indicates substantial seed-dependent variability in XGBoost predictions, with most edges appearing in fewer iterations (see Appendix B for detailed stability analysis)

Distribution of occurrences for predicted edges in GCN 200-iteration run: predicted edges do not follow a specific distribution with all edges at least being predicted in 182 off all 200 iterations (i.e. 91% minimum occurrence rate), and the majority of edges predicted in all 200 iterations. This highly skewed distribution (i.e. all edges being predicted in at least 182 runs) indicates substantially higher prediction stability for GCN compared to XGBoost (Fig. 9) (see Appendix B for detailed stability analysis)

Consensus predictions from multiple iterations demonstrate dramatically different performance characteristics (Table 4). For GCN, consensus across 200 iterations achieves excellent discrimination (i.e. AUROC=0.9865 [0.9764, 0.9946], AUPRC=0.9131 [0.8546, 0.9610]) with outstanding top-K performance (i.e. Precision@10=1.0, Recall@100=0.9242), recovering 92.42% of test edges among the top 100 predictions. In contrast, XGBoost consensus across 10 iterations shows poor discrimination (i.e. AUROC=0.4556 [0.3719, 0.5367], AUPRC=0.1023 [0.0678, 0.1429]) with limited recall (i.e. Recall@100=0.1667), indicating that XGBoost suffers more from lower link prediction efficiency in 80/20 train/split conditions than GCN. We hypothise that the removal of that many positive gene-to-drug edges deeply affect the outcome of prediction, due to the sparce nature of biological networks—especially in RDs.

Figure 11 presents a heatmap comparing prediction confidences for 20 selected drugs across different evaluation frameworks (single-run on full network, single-run on subset network, and iteration-based consensus on subset network for both XGBoost and GCN). The heatmap reveals systematic patterns in prediction confidence: drugs consistently predicted by both models across evaluation frameworks represent high-confidence candidates (e.g. Ergocristine, Fig. 11), while drugs with framework-specific confidence patterns highlight the complementary nature of single-run and iteration-based approaches.Fig. 11Prediction confidence for 20 selected drugs across evaluation frameworks: Heatmap showing prediction probabilities for drugs identified through convergence analysis, evaluated across XGBoost and GCN using both the single-run approach on both full network and subset network, and the iteration-based consensus approach on the subset network. Colour represents prediction confidence (darker red = higher probability). Consistent high-confidence predictions across all six frameworks represent robust candidates, while framework-specific patterns reveal differences in model calibration and the stabilising effect of iteration-based consensus. Notably, Ergocristine has higher confidence in the GCN iteration consensus approach on the subset network than in the single run approach on the full network, in support of our thesis that iterations can lead to candidates that are overlooked over single runs. White cells indicate random predictions

Prediction confidence for 20 selected drugs across evaluation frameworks: Heatmap showing prediction probabilities for drugs identified through convergence analysis, evaluated across XGBoost and GCN using both the single-run approach on both full network and subset network, and the iteration-based consensus approach on the subset network. Colour represents prediction confidence (darker red = higher probability). Consistent high-confidence predictions across all six frameworks represent robust candidates, while framework-specific patterns reveal differences in model calibration and the stabilising effect of iteration-based consensus. Notably, Ergocristine has higher confidence in the GCN iteration consensus approach on the subset network than in the single run approach on the full network, in support of our thesis that iterations can lead to candidates that are overlooked over single runs. White cells indicate random predictions

The iteration-based consensus approach reveals an important pattern: some drugs without obvious disease-gene associations may still hold therapeutic relevance when evaluated through pharmacological mechanisms rather than network proximity alone. Ergocristine (CHEMBL1201247) exemplifies this pattern. While not being directly associated with HD-related genes in public databases, Ergocristine demonstrated higher and more stable prediction confidence in iteration-based consensus (i.e. 200 iterations on GCN) compared to single-run predictions on both full and subset networks (detailed probability distributions in Appendix C). Notably, Ergocristine emerged from the convergence analysis as one of the drugs predicted by both XGBoost and GCN across different evaluation frameworks: it appears in the overlap between models on both full network single runs and subset network iteration runs, demonstrating robust cross-model agreement. According to PubChem classification, Ergocristine functions as a Peripheral Nervous System Agent with mechanisms involving neurotransmitter modulation and motor pathway regulation–pharmacologically relevant to HD given the disease’s characteristic GABAergic medium spiny neuron degeneration and motor dysfunction [2]. While this higher prediction confidence may be coincidental, it also suggests that iteration-based consensus could distinguish genuine signal from stochastic noise–and surface biologically plausible candidates that might be overlooked or underweighted in single-run predictions.

Convergence analysis using relaxed thresholds (Appendix C) confirms that filtering creates artificial divergence: when analysed without probability filters, consensus predictions from both models demonstrate strong agreement at top ranks–100% overlap at top-10 (i.e. Jaccard=1.0), 95% at top-20, 94% at top-50, and 96% at top-100 (Fig. 12). This indicates that despite substantial differences in discrimination ability and confidence calibration, both models fundamentally identify similar high-priority drug candidates for HD.

Information from the network of biological associations to the disease of interest are stored as dictionaries (i.e. nodes) or list of dictionaries (i.e. edges, which store subject node, relationship, object node and notes). Here’s an example:

Only a portion of the nodes are useful for the drug re-purposing: anything other then diseases, phenotypes, genes and drugs are not used at latter steps (Tables 1 and 2). Moreover, not all drugs can be used during the drugsimilarity.py step since not all the URIs can be mapped to known IDs to be converted into SMILES. Of the 69,907 drugs available in DGIdb, only 12,872 (18.4%) have ChEMBL or Wikidata identifiers that are potentially mappable to SMILES representations (Section “Database APIs for biological, gene-drug associations anddrug-drug similarity”), and of these, 11,603 (16.6%) were successfully mapped (i.e. 11,596 from ChEMBL and 7 from Wikidata). This mapping limitation affects the drug similarity network construction, but it does not impact the primary gene-drug association predictions, as the drug similarity step serves to enhance negative sample selection rather than define the prediction space. Nodes and edges from the network of associations to HD that are used for drug repurposing are listed in Tables 1 and 2—with counts from a reference run performed on October 7th 2025. This represents a substantial expansion compared to the network constructed by Reep, which comprised 2189 nodes and 17,467 edges using June 2022 API versions [31], demonstrating the value of updated data infrastructure and the inclusion of all DGIdb drugs—rather than only those already associated with the disease of interest (Section “Database APIs for biological, gene-drug associations anddrug-drug similarity”).

Compared to the full network (Fig. 1), the smaller network (Fig. 2) of 1000 (26.50%) genes, 600 drugs (29.97%) and 970 other mappable nodes (10.00%) generated with the same distribution of centrality scores had much lower edge density: 267 gene-to-drug edges (1.62%), 131 gene-to-gene edges (4.81%), and 513 other edges (4.81%) (Table 9). Appendix A presents the validation of the subset network as a representation of the full network for the purpose of iteration-consensus testing.Fig. 1Full network of associations to HD: highly interconnected nodes (mostly diseases in dark blue, genes in light blue and phenotypes in blue) at the centre and less interconnected nodes (mostly drugs in red and other entities in orange) at the fringes joined by biological associations (blue lines) and gene-to-drug edges (red lines); the full network comprises 15,476 nodes and 32,520 edges from the October 7, 2025 data snapshot (Tables 1 and 2)Fig. 2Network made up of a subset of nodes from the full network in Figure 1: generated according to the random sampling method described in Section “Pipeline upgrades”; nodes are sampled to retain the same proportions according to labels in Table 1; statistical validation (Appendix A) confirms preservation of betweenness centrality distributions (i.e. p>0.05, KS-test) and representative topology, enabling iteration-based evaluation while reducing computational requirements and time frame

The results from the generation of negative samples (Section “Generation of semantically-valid negative samples based on centrality measures”) vary depending on the centrality measure used: nodes that score higher will lead to more edges containing it being deemed valid negative samples. Gene node ‘HGNC:644’ (AR, Androgen Receptor) for example is the top scoring according to the degree centrality measure (0.0479), yet it is not according to eigenvector and in-betweenness centrality measures (respectively 0.2930 with max=0.3143, and 0.0739 with max=0.0852).

Moreover, this step still takes into consideration hub nodes that are not relevant for this purpose: genes such as ‘HGNC:11998’ (TP53) (i.e. a transcription factor for apoptosis that is silenced in tumors) are highly interconnected because of their nature of home-keeping genes. Yet they cannot be automatically ignored due to the lack of a database that annotates them accordingly and that uses an API for mapping call requests.

To validate the contribution of our combined centrality and similarity-based approach, we performed an ablation study comparing four negative sampling strategies on the full HD network using GCN: random negatives as a baseline, similarity-only, centrality-only, and ‘combined’ (i.e. centrality+similarity). All strategies achieved high discrimination (i.e. AUROC 0.91−0.92, Table 3), with the combined approach demonstrating the best performance across metrics (i.e. AUROC=0.9175, AUPRC=0.6904, F1=0.5503, Table 3), representing modest –yet consistent—improvements over the other sampling methods (e.g. +0.5% AUROC, +1.8% AUPRC, +7.0% F1-score to baseline). The smaller-than-anticipated effect size (Table 3) is attributed to the design of negative samples generation: we only consider gene-to-drug edges for training and prediction, thus even randomly generated valid positive edges are informative enough to support prediction. Nonetheless, the ‘combined’ approach provides the best performance (Table 3). Our approach—mediated also by Node2Vec’s fit to the sparse nature of biological networks (Section “Parallelisation in gene-drug embeddings”)—allows to reduce computational times in the prediction phase (Section “Parallelisation in gene-drug embeddings”). Due to the differences between the two ML methods (i.e. XGBoost and GCN) in the kind of embeddings to feed for training, the time required to building them changes quite a lot. Where the pipeline running the XGBoost method requires to compute any interaction (i.e. present as positive or valid negative, or missing as invalid negative) between gene and drug nodes (e.g. for Huntington’s disease run: 3031 * 4582 = 13,888,042), the pipeline running the GCN method only requires to fill the matrix with the known positive and valid negative associations (i.e. 28,260 + 17,956 = 46,216). GCN embedding generation completes in approximately 48 s, requiring only 0.3% of the time needed for XGBoost embedding generation (i.e. approximately 4.4 h).

Both methods also require the construction of an empty prediction matrix for all possible combinations between genes in the network and all drugs from DGIdb (i.e. 3031 * 69,907 = 211,888,117), but this step take considerably less time due to not having to iterate through edges to determine the presence or absence of an association. Nonetheless, the entire process still takes from half an hour to almost 24 h: for the purpose of saving time, the emb_l=1 parameter allows to load existing embeddings files instead of computing them provided that they exist (i.e. same disease, same date -although the date can be brute forced with the set_date() utility function at the beginning of the function for this specific purpose). On non-specialised computational hardware (e.g. laptop with 10-core CPU @3.2GHz in our case), the complete embedding generation process ranges from 30 min—with GCN on the subset network—to approximately 20 h—with XGBoost on full network. Parallel processing reduces these times by a factor proportional to the number of available cores.

Nonetheless, the ‘combined’ approach provides the best performance (Table 3). Our approach—mediated also by Node2Vec’s fit to the sparse nature of biological networks (Section “Parallelisation in gene-drug embeddings”)—allows to reduce computational times in the prediction phase (Section “Parallelisation in gene-drug embeddings”). Detailed ablation methodology, comprehensive metrics from Table 3, and comparison plots are provided in Appendix D.Table 3Negative sampling ablation study summary: ‘combined’ (i.e. centrality+similarity) strategy outperforms random baseline with modest but consistent improvementsStrategyAUROCAUPRCF1-scoreRandom (baseline)0.91290.67820.5144Combined0.91750.69040.5503Improvement+0.5%+1.8%+7.0%Full results comparing all four strategies (i.e. random, similarity-only, centrality-only, combined) with comprehensive metrics and analysis are provided in Appendix D

chunks.section: Generation of semantically-valid negative samples based on centrality measures
chunks.text:
Negative sampling ablation study summary: ‘combined’ (i.e. centrality+similarity) strategy outperforms random baseline with modest but consistent improvements

Full results comparing all four strategies (i.e. random, similarity-only, centrality-only, combined) with comprehensive metrics and analysis are provided in Appendix D

Due to the differences between the two ML methods (i.e. XGBoost and GCN) in the kind of embeddings to feed for training, the time required to building them changes quite a lot. Where the pipeline running the XGBoost method requires to compute any interaction (i.e. present as positive or valid negative, or missing as invalid negative) between gene and drug nodes (e.g. for Huntington’s disease run: 3031 * 4582 = 13,888,042), the pipeline running the GCN method only requires to fill the matrix with the known positive and valid negative associations (i.e. 28,260 + 17,956 = 46,216). GCN embedding generation completes in approximately 48 s, requiring only 0.3% of the time needed for XGBoost embedding generation (i.e. approximately 4.4 h).

Both methods also require the construction of an empty prediction matrix for all possible combinations between genes in the network and all drugs from DGIdb (i.e. 3031 * 69,907 = 211,888,117), but this step take considerably less time due to not having to iterate through edges to determine the presence or absence of an association. Nonetheless, the entire process still takes from half an hour to almost 24 h: for the purpose of saving time, the emb_l=1 parameter allows to load existing embeddings files instead of computing them provided that they exist (i.e. same disease, same date -although the date can be brute forced with the set_date() utility function at the beginning of the function for this specific purpose). On non-specialised computational hardware (e.g. laptop with 10-core CPU @3.2GHz in our case), the complete embedding generation process ranges from 30 min—with GCN on the subset network—to approximately 20 h—with XGBoost on full network. Parallel processing reduces these times by a factor proportional to the number of available cores.

chunks.section: ML training and prediction in enriched KG
chunks.text: Single-run predictions on the full network demonstrate substantial differences between models in both prediction volume and discrimination ability. Quantitative evaluation using an 80/20 train/test split –with held-out edges from the original network– reveals that GCN achieves excellent raw discrimination (i.e. AUROC=0.9313 [0.9222, 0.9407], AUPRC=0.7280 [0.6979, 0.7577], F1=0.5922) while XGBoost shows limited discrimination ability (i.e. AUROC=0.3958 [0.3778, 0.4135], AUPRC=0.0706 [0.0649, 0.0769], F1=0.1667) (Fig. 3). When probability filtering is applied (i.e. prob>0.65, cluster>0.8), GCN maintains strong performance (i.e. AUROC=0.9236 [0.9139, 0.9323], AUPRC=0.7077 [0.6752, 0.7405], F1=0.5590) with perfect Precision@10, while XGBoost performance shows mixed results (AUROC=0.4152 [0.3950, 0.4364], AUPRC=0.0749 [0.0685, 0.0819], F1=0.1667) (Fig. 3). Compared to Reep’s reported F1-score of 0.867, our models return lower values due to architectural differences: in fact compared to their version that only predicted repurposed drugs from a smaller network only with drugs already associated to the disease of interest, our model takes into consideration a much larger and more sparse network where predictions are hard to be quantified with F1 scores. Moreover, our dual-model approach with consensus-based ranking provides additional robustness through model complementarity as demonstrated in Section “Prediction iterations as a solution for seed variability in ML methods” and in Appendix C.Fig. 4XGBoost single run prediction: Full network of associations to HD from Figure 1 showing newly predicted gene-to-drug edges in green via the XGBoost model as described in Section “Prediction of repurposed drugs in binary models with non-binary datasets”Fig. 5GCN single run prediction: Full network of associations to HD from Figure 1 showing newly predicted gene-to-drug edges in green via the GCN model as described in Section “Prediction of repurposed drugs in binary models with non-binary datasets”

chunks.section: ML training and prediction in enriched KG
chunks.text:
XGBoost single run prediction: Full network of associations to HD from Figure 1 showing newly predicted gene-to-drug edges in green via the XGBoost model as described in Section “Prediction of repurposed drugs in binary models with non-binary datasets”

GCN single run prediction: Full network of associations to HD from Figure 1 showing newly predicted gene-to-drug edges in green via the GCN model as described in Section “Prediction of repurposed drugs in binary models with non-binary datasets”

In terms of prediction volume, starting from a network with 251 positive gene-to-drug edges and approximately 5 M possible gene-to-drug edges, the two models exhibit markedly different behavior: XGBoost returns between 20 and 160 predicted edges respectively with 0.95 and 0.6 probability filter, while GCN returns approximately 120,000 (0.95 filter) to 500,000 (0.65 filter) edges. This indicates that GCN is substantially more lenient in positive prediction even at high probability thresholds.

chunks.text: Convergence analysis across both single-run and iteration-based predictions (Section “Prediction iterations as a solution for seed variability in ML methods”) identified several drugs predicted consistently by both models across different evaluation frameworks, suggesting robust cross-model agreement on high-priority candidates (Fig. 6, detailed analysis in Appendix C).

chunks.section: ML training and prediction in enriched KG
ML training and prediction in enriched KG
chunks.text:
XGBoost 10-iteration run prediction: Subset network of associations to HD from Fig. 3 showing newly predicted gene-to-drug edges in green via 10 iterations of the XGBoost model as described in Section "Prediction iterations as a solution for seed variability in ML methods" (detailed distribution in Fig. 9)

GCN 200-iteration run prediction: Subset network of associations to HD from Fig. 3 showing newly predicted gene-to-drug edges in green via 200 iterations of the GCN model as described in Section "Prediction iterations as a solution for seed variability in ML methods" (detailed distribution in Fig. 10)

Distribution of occurrences for predicted edges in XGBoost 10-iteration run: predicted edges follow a binomial distribution, with few edges being predicted consistently in all 10 iterations; this distribution indicates substantial seed-dependent variability in XGBoost predictions, with most edges appearing in fewer iterations (see Appendix B for detailed stability analysis)

chunks.section: ML training and prediction in enriched KG
chunks.text:
Distribution of occurrences for predicted edges in GCN 200-iteration run: predicted edges do not follow a specific distribution with all edges at least being predicted in 182 off all 200 iterations (i.e. 91% minimum occurrence rate), and the majority of edges predicted in all 200 iterations. This highly skewed distribution (i.e. all edges being predicted in at least 182 runs) indicates substantially higher prediction stability for GCN compared to XGBoost (Fig. 9) (see Appendix B for detailed stability analysis)

Consensus predictions from multiple iterations demonstrate dramatically different performance characteristics (Table 4). For GCN, consensus across 200 iterations achieves excellent discrimination (i.e. AUROC=0.9865 [0.9764, 0.9946], AUPRC=0.9131 [0.8546, 0.9610]) with outstanding top-K performance (i.e. Precision@10=1.0, Recall@100=0.9242), recovering 92.42% of test edges among the top 100 predictions. In contrast, XGBoost consensus across 10 iterations shows poor discrimination (i.e. AUROC=0.4556 [0.3719, 0.5367], AUPRC=0.1023 [0.0678, 0.1429]) with limited recall (i.e. Recall@100=0.1667), indicating that XGBoost suffers more from lower link prediction efficiency in 80/20 train/split conditions than GCN. We hypothise that the removal of that many positive gene-to-drug edges deeply affect the outcome of prediction, due to the sparce nature of biological networks—especially in RDs.

chunks.section: ML training and prediction in enriched KG
chunks.text: Figure 11 presents a heatmap comparing prediction confidences for 20 selected drugs across different evaluation frameworks (single-run on full network, single-run on subset network, and iteration-based consensus on subset network for both XGBoost and GCN). The heatmap reveals systematic patterns in prediction confidence: drugs consistently predicted by both models across evaluation frameworks represent high-confidence candidates (e.g. Ergocristine, Fig. 11), while drugs with framework-specific confidence patterns highlight the complementary nature of single-run and iteration-based approaches.Fig. 11Prediction confidence for 20 selected drugs across evaluation frameworks: Heatmap showing prediction probabilities for drugs identified through convergence analysis, evaluated across XGBoost and GCN using both the single-run approach on both full network and subset network, and the iteration-based consensus approach on the subset network. Colour represents prediction confidence (darker red = higher probability). Consistent high-confidence predictions across all six frameworks represent robust candidates, while framework-specific patterns reveal differences in model calibration and the stabilising effect of iteration-based consensus. Notably, Ergocristine has higher confidence in the GCN iteration consensus approach on the subset network than in the single run approach on the full network, in support of our thesis that iterations can lead to candidates that are overlooked over single runs. White cells indicate random predictions

chunks.section: ML training and prediction in enriched KG
chunks.text: The iteration-based consensus approach reveals an important pattern: some drugs without obvious disease-gene associations may still hold therapeutic relevance when evaluated through pharmacological mechanisms rather than network proximity alone. Ergocristine (CHEMBL1201247) exemplifies this pattern. While not being directly associated with HD-related genes in public databases, Ergocristine demonstrated higher and more stable prediction confidence in iteration-based consensus (i.e. 200 iterations on GCN) compared to single-run predictions on both full and subset networks (detailed probability distributions in Appendix C). Notably, Ergocristine emerged from the convergence analysis as one of the drugs predicted by both XGBoost and GCN across different evaluation frameworks: it appears in the overlap between models on both full network single runs and subset network iteration runs, demonstrating robust cross-model agreement. According to PubChem classification, Ergocristine functions as a Peripheral Nervous System Agent with mechanisms involving neurotransmitter modulation and motor pathway regulation–pharmacologically relevant to HD given the disease’s characteristic GABAergic medium spiny neuron degeneration and motor dysfunction [2]. While this higher prediction confidence may be coincidental, it also suggests that iteration-based consensus could distinguish genuine signal from stochastic noise–and surface biologically plausible candidates that might be overlooked or underweighted in single-run predictions.

chunks.section: ML training and prediction in enriched KG
ML training and prediction in enriched KG
ML training and prediction in enriched KG
chunks.text: Convergence analysis using relaxed thresholds (Appendix C) confirms that filtering creates artificial divergence: when analysed without probability filters, consensus predictions from both models demonstrate strong agreement at top ranks–100% overlap at top-10 (i.e. Jaccard=1.0), 95% at top-20, 94% at top-50, and 96% at top-100 (Fig. 12). This indicates that despite substantial differences in discrimination ability and confidence calibration, both models fundamentally identify similar high-priority drug candidates for HD.

The aim of this work was to develop a structured and modular approach for drug repurposing pipelines that can be implemented in cloud computing environments—enabling scalability—and that can be modified by replacing or adding modules with different functionalities—enabling reusability. Several steps also accept specific seeds for non-deterministic operations, or store the randomly generated ones if none are provided.

The entire pipeline has been divided into Python scripts (Table 6) and functional models (Fig. 13).Fig. 13Pipeline modules: each block represents one of the functional modules from Table 6 that focuses on a specific set of operations to process data in the biological association network of interestTable 6Pipeline functions divided into modules: each module focuses on a specific set of operations to process data in the biological association network of interest, as described in its respective methods section; visual depictions in Fig. 13ScriptModuleOperationsSectionMonarch.pyDatabase callsFetching biological associations up to the n-th degree of distance—third by default—given a disease ID as inputSection “Database APIs for biological, gene-drug associations anddrug-drug similaritydgidb.pyDatabase callsFetching gene-to-drug edges given a gene ID as inputSection “Database APIs for biological, gene-drug associations anddrug-drug similaritydrugsimilarity.pyDrug-to-drugGenerating drug-to-drug edges based on 2D feature similarity between drugsSection “Database APIs for biological, gene-drug associations anddrug-drug similaritynegsamples.pyNegative samplesGenerating semantically valid negative samples by considering node centrality, gene-to-gene edges, and drug-to-drug edges above a certain similarity thresholdSection “Generation of semantically-valid negative samples based on centrality measures”embeddings_{ml_method}.pyEmbeddingsConverting gene-to-drug edges into node embeddings according to requirements for the specific ML methodSection “Parallelisation in gene-drug embeddings”networkmodel_{ml_method}.pyDrug predictionPredicts drugs based on association to genes and prediction probability via XGBoost or GCNSections “Prediction of repurposed drugs in binary models withnon-binary datasets”, “Prediction iterations as a solution for seed variability in MLmethods”

Pipeline modules: each block represents one of the functional modules from Table 6 that focuses on a specific set of operations to process data in the biological association network of interest

Pipeline functions divided into modules: each module focuses on a specific set of operations to process data in the biological association network of interest, as described in its respective methods section; visual depictions in Fig. 13

We extended the work of Reep to accommodate for the new versions of the Monarch KG v3 FastAPI (https://api-v3.monarchinitiative.org/v3/api/) and the GraphQL 5.0 DGIdb API (https://dgidb.org/api/graphql) [31, 38]. Provided a disease URI, the pipeline fetches all biological associations to it up to the third degree of distance (d.o.d.). Monarch Initiative returns JSON files in the Biolink model format for each node query listing all nodes associated [53], and the pipeline then performs more calls based on the nodes returned. DGIdb queries are performed for all gene nodes from Monarch Initiative’s calls, and it returns JSON files in the GraphQL format for each gene node listing all drug nodes associated [38]. The pipeline accepts Monarch Initiative’s proprietary disease URIs (i.e. ‘MONDO:’) directly, while the more widely used ‘OMIM:’ IDs are not supported. Bianchi developed a custom JavaScript tool (https://github.com/NCMBianchi/OMIM-converter, [73]) that leverages Monarch Initiative’s entity mapping endpoints to ensure accurate cross-referencing between identifier systems. All datasets and results presented in this study were retrieved or based on datasets via these APIs on October 7th and 10th, 2025 at the latest.

Moreover, the previous version of the tool by Reep only took into consideration drugs already in the network of associations to the disease for the newly predicted gene-to-drug edges [31]. We added a step that fetches all the drugs in DGIdb, and we handled them separately.

The generation of drug-to-drug edges was mostly untouched compared to Reep: provided ‘chembl:’ and ‘wikidata:’ drug nodes’ URIs, pairwise similarity is computed by fetching the SMILES notation [31, 74] representing the molecule’s structure for each drug, and converting them to RDkit 2-dimensional objects. Those are then used to build 4096-bit ECFP vectors representing the presence or absence of a given feature in the molecule, which are used to compute pair-wise similarity according to Tanimoto’s coefficient [29, 75] –maintaining the same approach used by Reep for molecular similarity assessment, although with a lower similarity threshold (i.e. 0.5 instead of 0.92 [31]) to accommodate for a higher number of weighted edges for negative samples generation (Section “Generation of semantically-valid negative samples based on centrality measures”) and GCN training (4.2).

For the purpose of testing prediction efficiency, runtimes and CPU strain on several runs within manageable time-frames (i.e. less than 10 h), we generated a smaller network of 1000 gene nodes, 600 drug nodes and 10% of all other nodes associated to the disease of interest and 4400 other drugs from DGIdb by computing the ‘PageRank’ centrality score for all the nodes, and selected a subset with the same distribution of centrality scores. More on this in Appendix A.

Due to the lack of databases which store structured information on known lack of associations between gene and drugs, we generated semantically-valid negative samples (Section “Algorithm for semantic generation of negative samples”). Specifically, we aim to discriminate between valid and invalid negative samples among the missing gene-drug associations (i.e. nodes not connected by edges) in the KG, to ignore invalid negative samples during prediction.

By iterating through each positive sample (i.e. gene-to-drug edges fetched from DGIdb, labelled ‘1’), a centrality score is computed for each gene and drug node (i.e. degree, centrality, eigenvector centrality or in-betweenness centrality depending on the user input). Whenever either of the nodes has a centrality score above a certain threshold, all the genes at one d.o.d. of that given gene and all the drugs at one d.o.d. of that drug above a certain similarity threshold are taken into consideration. Any combination of gene-to-drug edges from this pool is deemed semantically valid (i.e. labelled ‘0’).

ML methods require information provided in vectors to perform a prediction: one node embedding dataset with all information for training with nodes and edges, and one for prediction only with nodes. Both mapping datasets contain all biological entity nodes and drug nodes associated to the disease of interest, while the prediction mapping dataset also contains all other drug nodes in DGIdb, to make predictions also outside of the associations to the disease of interest.

Node embeddings are generated using Node2Vec as implemented in the node2vec Python package [62], which creates dense vector representations through biased random walks on the network followed by skip-gram learning to predict node contexts [60]. In contrast to Reep, who employed RDF2Vec—no longer supported—with maximum walk depth of 4 and 10 walks per entity [31], our Node2Vec implementation provides more flexible control through parametrised biased random walks to the advantage of biological networks that are inherently sparse.

In our implementation, we used non-default parameters for embedding dimensions, walk length and number of walks. Embedding dimensions was set to 64 (i.e. default = 128) to avoid overfitting in biological networks, which are sparse compared to the social networks Node2Vec was developed for. Walk length instead was set to 30 (i.e. default = 80) due to the nodes in our KG never being more than 5 d.o.d. from each other—far less than in social networks. Finally, the number of walks was set to 200 (i.e. default = 10) to ensure adequate sampling of the low proportion of positive gene-drug associations in our KG—only 28,260 out of 13.8M possible pairs, as reported in the Results section (Table 2 and in Section “Parallelisation in gene-drug embeddings”). The higher number of walks also improves embedding stability across iterative runs with different random seeds (more in Section “Prediction iterations as a solution for seed variability in MLmethods”).

To improve computational efficiency, the entire pipeline implements a caching mechanism—triggered by the emb_l=1 parameter– that enables loading of previously computed embeddings when processing the same disease and date combination, avoiding redundant computation. The date parameter can be manually set using the set_date() utility function to enable reuse of existing embedding files. For computationally intensive tasks such as generating embeddings for all possible gene-drug combinations in the prediction matrix, parallel processing distributes the workload across multiple CPU cores—configurable via the n_jobs parameter—significantly reducing runtime compared to sequential processing.

While the GCN model from the PyTorch-geometric library only requires the training node embedding map of all the gene-to-drug edges in the network of associations (i.e. positive samples labelled ‘1’, and valid negative samples labelled ‘0’) [47, 48], XGBoost also requires the training node embedding map for all the possible gene-to-drug edges not in the network (i.e. invalid negative samples, labelled ‘-1’) [59], which have to be computed by iterating through genes and drugs at this point of the pipeline. This step is extremely time-consuming, therefore we implemented a script that breaks the list of drugs into chunks, and each chunk is processed by a different CPU core concurrently. The maximum number of CPU cores to use can be set by the user.

Node embeddings are then fed to the appropriate model for training and subsequent prediction. Several parameters are made available to the user (Table 7) to customise the ML prediction step.Table 7Sample of the parameters for the run_networkmodelml_method() function from the ‘Drug Prediction’ module: ‘run_jobs’, ‘run_depth’ (Table 5) and ‘batch_size’ regulate the ML prediction step; ‘prob_input’ and ‘clust_input’ regulate the filtering of predicted edges according to confidence metrics, compared to Reep’s single filtering threshold of 0.9 [31]; ‘model_type’ is a conditional parameter when using GCN and not XGBoostParameterDefaultDescriptionrun_jobsHalf of CPU coresNumber of CPU cores to use for training/predictionrun_depth‘ultralight’Depth of parameter optimisation (‘ultralight’, ‘light’, ‘full’)prob_input0.65Probability threshold for filtering predictionsclust_input0.8Cluster threshold for ranking drugsbatch_s2000Batch size for predictionmodel_type [PyG]‘gcn’Type of Graph Neural Network model (‘gcn’, ‘gat’, ‘sage’)

Sample of the parameters for the run_networkmodelml_method() function from the ‘Drug Prediction’ module: ‘run_jobs’, ‘run_depth’ (Table 5) and ‘batch_size’ regulate the ML prediction step; ‘prob_input’ and ‘clust_input’ regulate the filtering of predicted edges according to confidence metrics, compared to Reep’s single filtering threshold of 0.9 [31]; ‘model_type’ is a conditional parameter when using GCN and not XGBoost

The GCN model takes as training input the node embeddings that only consider existing edges with various labels (i.e. ‘1’ for positive samples, ‘0’ for valid negative samples), thus it only requires one run and it returns all edges that are predicted as positive. XGBoost instead mostly requires a binary mapping of edges that are deemed either positive or negative [59]. To take advantage of the negative samples information, the model runs twice: once where valid negative samples are considered as positive, and once where they are considered as negative. Results from both runs are pooled: any edge that appeared at list once is ranked, and the prediction probabilities is always averaged across runs with ‘0.5’ (random) for runs where the edge does not appear.

For quantitative evaluation of model performance, we employed an 80/20 train/test split on gene-to-drug edges from DGIdb. Of the 69,907 total drugs in their database, only 11,603 (16.6%) could be successfully mapped to SMILES representations required for drug similarity calculations (detailed mappability analysis in Section “Database APIs for biological, gene-drug associations anddrug-drug similarity”), constraining the prediction space to drugs with available structural information. In each evaluation run, 80% of known gene-drug associations were randomly selected for training –including both positive and semantically-valid negative samples generated as described in section “Generation of semantically-valid negative samples based on centrality measures”—while the remaining 20% were held out for testing. This split ratio follows standard ML practice, providing sufficient training data while maintaining an adequate test set for robust performance estimation. Split selection was performed using a fixed random seed (i.e. seed=42) to ensure reproducibility across experiments. For iteration-based evaluations, the same train/test split was maintained across all iterations–only the model’s random seed was varied–ensuring that performance metrics reflect model stability rather than data variability. Prediction efficiency is also estimated by verifying the presence of a smaller subset gene-to-drug edges (i.e. n=5) that were in the original network, but that were removed ahead of training: such edges were hand-picked based on their centrality score to be relevant enough in the network, yet not too relevant (Table 8).

XGBoost model training employs randomised hyperparameter search using scikit-learn’s RandomizedSearchCV() with repeated stratified k-fold cross-validation. The pipeline offers three optimisation depth settings (run_depth parameter): ultralight, light, and full, which control both the hyperparameter search space and cross-validation complexity. The full setting explores a comprehensive parameter grid including min_child_weight (i.e. seven values between 2 and 30), gamma (i.e. nine values between 0 and 6), regularisation parameters (i.e. reg_alpha and reg_lambda, six values each between 0 and 10), subsample and colsample_bytree (i.e. uniform distributions respectively between 0.5 and 1.0, and between 0.2 and 1.0), max_depth (i.e. seven values between 4 and 16), n_estimators (i.e. seven values between 35 and 100), and learning_rate (i.e. uniform from 0 to 0.3), with 10-split 5-repeat cross-validation and 20 random iterations. The light and ultralight settings use reduced parameter grids and fewer cross-validation splits (i.e. 3/2 and 2/1 splits/repeats respectively) to balance optimisation quality with computational efficiency (Table 5). Due to the dual-run embedding XGBoost strategy described above, two separate models are trained with optimised hyperparameters, and predictions from each of the two runs are subsequently pooled.Fig. 14Degree distribution comparison between full and subset networks: Left panel shows degree distribution density plots for full network (blue) and subset network (orange), demonstrating that the subset preserves the characteristic peak near zero degree typical of biological networks— even though with higher counts due to the smaller network mostly having 1 d.o.d. associations (Appendix A). Right panel presents degree distribution on log-log scale, confirming that both networks exhibit scale-free topology with similar power-law decay patterns. The subset network maintains representative coverage of high-degree hub nodes while reducing overall network size by approximately 95%, enabling computationally feasible iteration-based evaluations. Peaks in both curves represent highly interconnected gene hubs. Complete statistical validation including betweenness centrality preservation (KS-test p>0.05), and entity type proportions are provided in Appendix A

Right panel presents degree distribution on log-log scale, confirming that both networks exhibit scale-free topology with similar power-law decay patterns. The subset network maintains representative coverage of high-degree hub nodes while reducing overall network size by approximately 95%, enabling computationally feasible iteration-based evaluations. Peaks in both curves represent highly interconnected gene hubs. Complete statistical validation including betweenness centrality preservation (KS-test p>0.05), and entity type proportions are provided in Appendix A

To enable comprehensive evaluation through multiple iterations while maintaining manageable computational requirements, we generated a representative subset network (i.e. 1000 genes, 600 drugs) using PageRank centrality-based stratified sampling that preserves the topological characteristics of the full network (i.e. p>0.05 for betweenness centrality distributions; see Appendix A for complete methodology and statistical validation; Fig. 14), while node type proportions were intentionally enriched for genes and drugs to maximise prediction space. Single-run evaluations and ablation studies were performed on the full network, while iteration-based consensus predictions were evaluated on this validated subset network due to computational constraints (see Appendix A for detailed justification).Table 8Gene-to-drug edges removed for prediction efficiency check: curated subset of edges involving key gene nodes in the network of associations to HD; removed according to section “Prediction of repurposed drugs in binary models with non-binary datasets”; validated in section “Prediction iterations as a solution for seed variability in ML methods”Gene IDGene nameDrug IDDrug nameScoreHGNC:20,772TUBB3CHEMBL1173445LARGAZOLE0.0434HGNC:4617GSK3BCHEMBL546797CHEMBL5467970.0355HGNC:4851HTTCHEMBL581880CHEMBL5818800.0576HGNC:4851HTTCHEMBL472437KAMEBANIN0.0432HGNC:3594FASNCHEMBL210208PYROGALLOL RED0.0773

Results from all iterations are collected in a mapping dictionary tracking prediction probabilities across runs. Gene-to-drug edges are then ranked using a consensus strategy based on two criteria: occurrence count across iterations, and average prediction probability. By default, only the top 5% of predictions at a maximum of 100 edges (ml_iter_max_edges=100) from each prediction iteration are labelled as positive and returned in the final results. This consensus approach reduces sensitivity to random seed variation and improves prediction stability (detailed iteration analysis in Appendix B).

We extended the work of Reep to accommodate for the new versions of the Monarch KG v3 FastAPI (https://api-v3.monarchinitiative.org/v3/api/) and the GraphQL 5.0 DGIdb API (https://dgidb.org/api/graphql) [31, 38]. Provided a disease URI, the pipeline fetches all biological associations to it up to the third degree of distance (d.o.d.). Monarch Initiative returns JSON files in the Biolink model format for each node query listing all nodes associated [53], and the pipeline then performs more calls based on the nodes returned. DGIdb queries are performed for all gene nodes from Monarch Initiative’s calls, and it returns JSON files in the GraphQL format for each gene node listing all drug nodes associated [38]. The pipeline accepts Monarch Initiative’s proprietary disease URIs (i.e. ‘MONDO:’) directly, while the more widely used ‘OMIM:’ IDs are not supported. Bianchi developed a custom JavaScript tool (https://github.com/NCMBianchi/OMIM-converter, [73]) that leverages Monarch Initiative’s entity mapping endpoints to ensure accurate cross-referencing between identifier systems. All datasets and results presented in this study were retrieved or based on datasets via these APIs on October 7th and 10th, 2025 at the latest.

Moreover, the previous version of the tool by Reep only took into consideration drugs already in the network of associations to the disease for the newly predicted gene-to-drug edges [31]. We added a step that fetches all the drugs in DGIdb, and we handled them separately.

The generation of drug-to-drug edges was mostly untouched compared to Reep: provided ‘chembl:’ and ‘wikidata:’ drug nodes’ URIs, pairwise similarity is computed by fetching the SMILES notation [31, 74] representing the molecule’s structure for each drug, and converting them to RDkit 2-dimensional objects. Those are then used to build 4096-bit ECFP vectors representing the presence or absence of a given feature in the molecule, which are used to compute pair-wise similarity according to Tanimoto’s coefficient [29, 75] –maintaining the same approach used by Reep for molecular similarity assessment, although with a lower similarity threshold (i.e. 0.5 instead of 0.92 [31]) to accommodate for a higher number of weighted edges for negative samples generation (Section “Generation of semantically-valid negative samples based on centrality measures”) and GCN training (4.2).

For the purpose of testing prediction efficiency, runtimes and CPU strain on several runs within manageable time-frames (i.e. less than 10 h), we generated a smaller network of 1000 gene nodes, 600 drug nodes and 10% of all other nodes associated to the disease of interest and 4400 other drugs from DGIdb by computing the ‘PageRank’ centrality score for all the nodes, and selected a subset with the same distribution of centrality scores. More on this in Appendix A.

Due to the lack of databases which store structured information on known lack of associations between gene and drugs, we generated semantically-valid negative samples (Section “Algorithm for semantic generation of negative samples”). Specifically, we aim to discriminate between valid and invalid negative samples among the missing gene-drug associations (i.e. nodes not connected by edges) in the KG, to ignore invalid negative samples during prediction.

Whenever either of the nodes has a centrality score above a certain threshold, all the genes at one d.o.d. of that given gene and all the drugs at one d.o.d. of that drug above a certain similarity threshold are taken into consideration. Any combination of gene-to-drug edges from this pool is deemed semantically valid (i.e. labelled ‘0’).

Both mapping datasets contain all biological entity nodes and drug nodes associated to the disease of interest, while the prediction mapping dataset also contains all other drug nodes in DGIdb, to make predictions also outside of the associations to the disease of interest.

In contrast to Reep, who employed RDF2Vec—no longer supported—with maximum walk depth of 4 and 10 walks per entity [31], our Node2Vec implementation provides more flexible control through parametrised biased random walks to the advantage of biological networks that are inherently sparse.

In our implementation, we used non-default parameters for embedding dimensions, walk length and number of walks. Embedding dimensions was set to 64 (i.e. default = 128) to avoid overfitting in biological networks, which are sparse compared to the social networks Node2Vec was developed for. Walk length instead was set to 30 (i.e. default = 80) due to the nodes in our KG never being more than 5 d.o.d. from each other—far less than in social networks. Finally, the number of walks was set to 200 (i.e. default = 10) to ensure adequate sampling of the low proportion of positive gene-drug associations in our KG—only 28,260 out of 13.8M possible pairs, as reported in the Results section (Table 2 and in Section “Parallelisation in gene-drug embeddings”). The higher number of walks also improves embedding stability across iterative runs with different random seeds (more in Section “Prediction iterations as a solution for seed variability in MLmethods”).

The date parameter can be manually set using the set_date() utility function to enable reuse of existing embedding files. For computationally intensive tasks such as generating embeddings for all possible gene-drug combinations in the prediction matrix, parallel processing distributes the workload across multiple CPU cores—configurable via the n_jobs parameter—significantly reducing runtime compared to sequential processing.

While the GCN model from the PyTorch-geometric library only requires the training node embedding map of all the gene-to-drug edges in the network of associations (i.e. positive samples labelled ‘1’, and valid negative samples labelled ‘0’) [47, 48], XGBoost also requires the training node embedding map for all the possible gene-to-drug edges not in the network (i.e. invalid negative samples, labelled ‘-1’) [59], which have to be computed by iterating through genes and drugs at this point of the pipeline. Single-run evaluations and ablation studies were performed on the full network, while iteration-based consensus predictions were evaluated on this validated subset network due to computational constraints (see Appendix A for detailed justification).Table 8Gene-to-drug edges removed for prediction efficiency check: curated subset of edges involving key gene nodes in the network of associations to HD; removed according to section “Prediction of repurposed drugs in binary models with non-binary datasets”; validated in section “Prediction iterations as a solution for seed variability in ML methods”Gene IDGene nameDrug IDDrug nameScoreHGNC:20,772TUBB3CHEMBL1173445LARGAZOLE0.0434HGNC:4617GSK3BCHEMBL546797CHEMBL5467970.0355HGNC:4851HTTCHEMBL581880CHEMBL5818800.0576HGNC:4851HTTCHEMBL472437KAMEBANIN0.0432HGNC:3594FASNCHEMBL210208PYROGALLOL RED0.0773

chunks.section: ML training and prediction in enriched KG
ML training and prediction in enriched KG
chunks.text: Results from all iterations are collected in a mapping dictionary tracking prediction probabilities across runs. Gene-to-drug edges are then ranked using a consensus strategy based on two criteria: occurrence count across iterations, and average prediction probability. By default, only the top 5% of predictions at a maximum of 100 edges (ml_iter_max_edges=100) from each prediction iteration are labelled as positive and returned in the final results. This consensus approach reduces sensitivity to random seed variation and improves prediction stability (detailed iteration analysis in Appendix B).

chunks.section: Prediction of repurposed drugs in binary models with non-binary datasets
chunks.text:
Node embeddings are then fed to the appropriate model for training and subsequent prediction. Several parameters are made available to the user (Table 7) to customise the ML prediction step.Table 7Sample of the parameters for the run_networkmodelml_method() function from the ‘Drug Prediction’ module: ‘run_jobs’, ‘run_depth’ (Table 5) and ‘batch_size’ regulate the ML prediction step; ‘prob_input’ and ‘clust_input’ regulate the filtering of predicted edges according to confidence metrics, compared to Reep’s single filtering threshold of 0.9 [31]; ‘model_type’ is a conditional parameter when using GCN and not XGBoostParameterDefaultDescriptionrun_jobsHalf of CPU coresNumber of CPU cores to use for training/predictionrun_depth‘ultralight’Depth of parameter optimisation (‘ultralight’, ‘light’, ‘full’)prob_input0.65Probability threshold for filtering predictionsclust_input0.8Cluster threshold for ranking drugsbatch_s2000Batch size for predictionmodel_type [PyG]‘gcn’Type of Graph Neural Network model (‘gcn’, ‘gat’, ‘sage’)

chunks.section: Prediction of repurposed drugs in binary models with non-binary datasets
chunks.text: The GCN model takes as training input the node embeddings that only consider existing edges with various labels (i.e. ‘1’ for positive samples, ‘0’ for valid negative samples), thus it only requires one run and it returns all edges that are predicted as positive. XGBoost instead mostly requires a binary mapping of edges that are deemed either positive or negative [59]. To take advantage of the negative samples information, the model runs twice: once where valid negative samples are considered as positive, and once where they are considered as negative. Results from both runs are pooled: any edge that appeared at list once is ranked, and the prediction probabilities is always averaged across runs with ‘0.5’ (random) for runs where the edge does not appear.

chunks.section: Prediction of repurposed drugs in binary models with non-binary datasets
chunks.text: For quantitative evaluation of model performance, we employed an 80/20 train/test split on gene-to-drug edges from DGIdb. Of the 69,907 total drugs in their database, only 11,603 (16.6%) could be successfully mapped to SMILES representations required for drug similarity calculations (detailed mappability analysis in Section “Database APIs for biological, gene-drug associations anddrug-drug similarity”), constraining the prediction space to drugs with available structural information. In each evaluation run, 80% of known gene-drug associations were randomly selected for training –including both positive and semantically-valid negative samples generated as described in section “Generation of semantically-valid negative samples based on centrality measures”—while the remaining 20% were held out for testing. This split ratio follows standard ML practice, providing sufficient training data while maintaining an adequate test set for robust performance estimation. Split selection was performed using a fixed random seed (i.e. seed=42) to ensure reproducibility across experiments. For iteration-based evaluations, the same train/test split was maintained across all iterations–only the model’s random seed was varied–ensuring that performance metrics reflect model stability rather than data variability. Prediction efficiency is also estimated by verifying the presence of a smaller subset gene-to-drug edges (i.e. n=5) that were in the original network, but that were removed ahead of training: such edges were hand-picked based on their centrality score to be relevant enough in the network, yet not too relevant (Table 8).

chunks.section: Prediction of repurposed drugs in binary models with non-binary datasets
chunks.text: XGBoost model training employs randomised hyperparameter search using scikit-learn’s RandomizedSearchCV() with repeated stratified k-fold cross-validation. The pipeline offers three optimisation depth settings (run_depth parameter): ultralight, light, and full, which control both the hyperparameter search space and cross-validation complexity. The full setting explores a comprehensive parameter grid including min_child_weight (i.e. seven values between 2 and 30), gamma (i.e. nine values between 0 and 6), regularisation parameters (i.e. reg_alpha and reg_lambda, six values each between 0 and 10), subsample and colsample_bytree (i.e. uniform distributions respectively between 0.5 and 1.0, and between 0.2 and 1.0), max_depth (i.e. seven values between 4 and 16), n_estimators (i.e. seven values between 35 and 100), and learning_rate (i.e. uniform from 0 to 0.3), with 10-split 5-repeat cross-validation and 20 random iterations. The light and ultralight settings use reduced parameter grids and fewer cross-validation splits (i.e. 3/2 and 2/1 splits/repeats respectively) to balance optimisation quality with computational efficiency (Table 5). Due to the dual-run embedding XGBoost strategy described above, two separate models are trained with optimised hyperparameters, and predictions from each of the two runs are subsequently pooled.Fig. 14Degree distribution comparison between full and subset networks: Left panel shows degree distribution density plots for full network (blue) and subset network (orange), demonstrating that the subset preserves the characteristic peak near zero degree typical of biological networks— even though with higher counts due to the smaller network mostly having 1 d.o.d. associations (Appendix A). Right panel presents degree distribution on log-log scale, confirming that both networks exhibit scale-free topology with similar power-law decay patterns. The subset network maintains representative coverage of high-degree hub nodes while reducing overall network size by approximately 95%, enabling computationally feasible iteration-based evaluations. Peaks in both curves represent highly interconnected gene hubs. Complete statistical validation including betweenness centrality preservation (KS-test p>0.05), and entity type proportions are provided in Appendix A
