The body weight of mice was measured in grams every week after surgery.
The mice were kept in mix genotype with three to five mice per cage. After surgery, mice were monitored weekly for survival for at least 13 months.

Genomic DNA of mouse brain and cell line samples were extracted by a FastPure DNA isolation kit (Vazyme, DC102-01) following manufacturer’s instructions and were sonicated to 300 to 500 bp. In addition, biotinylated 5′-CTC AGG TTC TGC TTT TAC CTG CG-3′ was used for primer extension followed by bridge adapter ligation. 5′-CCG AGG CCT CCG GGG ACT GC-3′ was used for nested PCR followed by tagged PCR with primers compatible for Illumina HiSeq sequencing. The raw data processing was described before (56). Editing efficiency was the percentage of indels and translocations to total events, and the off-target translocation was the percentage of junctions at off-target site to the total editing events.

The sequence of disrupted human HTT gene in BAC226Q mouse was characterized by Sanger sequencing. Mouse brain genomic DNA was extracted as previously described. Genomic DNA (100 ng) was amplified with human HTT-specific primer (hsHTT forward: 5′-CTG CCG GGA CGG GTC CAA GAT-3′; and hsHTT reverse: 5′-TGC AGC GGC TCC TCA GCC AC-3′) with the KAPA 2G Robust HotStart PCR kit (Roche, KE5507). The resulting PCR products of human HTT exon 1 fragments were purified with the QIAquick PCR purification kit (QIAGEN, 28104) according to the manufacturer’s instructions and sequenced by Sanger sequencing.

The total stratal mRNA from 4-month-old non–Tg-SaCas9, BAC226Q-SaCas9, BAC226Q-SaCas9-HTTg1, and BAC226Q-SaCas9-HTTg1/gCas9 mice were isolated by FreeZol Reagent (Vazyme, R711-01). The RNAs were sent to Beijing Novogene Technology for transcriptome sequencing and analysis. Total RNA (10 μg) for each qualified sample was used in the NEBNext Ultra RNA library prep kit (E7530L, New England Biolabs) to build the library. After cluster generation, the library preparations were sequenced on an Illumina NovaSeq platform, and 150-bp paired-end reads were generated. Index of the reference genome was built using Hisat2 v2.0.5, and paired-end clean reads were aligned to the reference genome using Hisat2 v2.0.5. Feature Counts v1.5.0-p3 was used to count the number of reads for each gene, and, then, the FPKM of each gene was calculated according to the length of the gene and reads count mapped to this gene. For differential gene analysis, the number of reads was normalized using DESeq software. The Benjamini and Hochberg’s method was used for adjusting the resulting P values, and genes with absolute fold change > 1 and P < 0.05 were considered DEGs.

Unless otherwise mentioned, one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Tukey’s multiple comparisons test or two-way ANOVA with Tukey’s multiple comparisons test was used for statistical comparison. Survival data were analyzed by log-rank (Mantel Cox) testing. Results were expressed as a means ± SEM. Statistical tests were performed and graphs were generated using GraphPad Prism (GraphPad Software Inc.). Statistical significance was defined as P < 0.05. Statistical significance level was set as follows: *P < 0.05; **P < 0.01; ***P < 0.001.

Non-coding RNAs (ncRNAs) are a diverse group of RNA molecules that play important roles in the central nervous system (CNS). Among these, circular RNAs (circRNAs) and enhancer RNAs (eRNAs) have become important in neurodegeneration. CircRNAs are more stable than linear ncRNAs due to their closed-loop structure. eRNAs, which come from enhancer regions, serve as regulators of gene expression in both space and time. Both types are found in high amounts in the brain, where they influence synaptic plasticity, neuroinflammation, and protein balance, linking them to neurodegenerative diseases [1, 2].

CircRNAs and eRNAs have gained attention as key regulatory molecules among non-coding RNAs. CircRNAs have a unique closed-loop structure that makes them very stable and abundant in the mammalian brain, particularly in synapses. Their specific expression patterns suggest that they are crucial for neurodevelopment and neuronal function [3–6]. In contrast, eRNAs, produced from enhancer regions, play a vital role in gene expression, shaping chromatin structure and enhancing interactions between enhancers and promoters [7]. This review aims to gather existing evidence on how circRNAs and eRNAs work in the development of Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, Huntington’s disease, and Multiple Sclerosis. Besides examining the mechanisms, we will explore their potential as disease-specific biomarkers that can be found in biofluids and as targets for new RNA-based therapies. By providing a clear view of their functions, this review hopes to link molecular insights with clinical uses, opening up new possibilities for diagnosis and treatment in neurology. Although this review discusses two distinct classes of noncoding RNAs—circRNAs and eRNAs—their inclusion within a single framework is justified by their complementary roles as master regulators of the integrity of gene expression in the nervous system. CircRNAs function primarily at the posttranscriptional level, using their stability to fine-tune gene expression through miRNA spongification, protein silencing, and translational regulation [1]. In contrast, eRNAs function at the transcriptional level, directly facilitating enhancer-promoter interaction and chromatin remodeling to initiate gene expression programs [7]. This multilevel regulatory axis is particularly important in postmitotic neurons, where precise gene control is essential for maintaining synaptic plasticity, neuronal identity, and cellular homeostasis. The simultaneous dysregulation of these regulatory layers may create destructive feeding loops in neurodegeneration, where transcriptional abnormalities (mediated by eRNAs) and posttranscriptional dysregulation (mediated by circRNAs) synergistically drive disease progression. Although the current literature on eRNAs in neurodegeneration is less extensive than on circRNAs, a difference that we acknowledge, emerging evidence strongly suggests their parallel importance in shaping the molecular landscape of neurodegenerative disorders. This narrative review synthesizes the available evidence on circRNAs and eRNAs in four major neuropathologies (AD, PD, HD, MS). We focus on identifying converging themes, such as their roles in protein aggregation, neuroinflammation, and synaptic integrity, and critically evaluate their potential as a novel class of integrative diagnostic and therapeutic targets.

chunks.section: Circular RNAs (circRNAs): biogenesis and multimodal functions
chunks.text: Circular RNAs are a large class of single-stranded, covalent RNA molecules produced by a process called “reverse splicing,” in which a 5‘downstream splice site is ligated to a 3’ upstream splice site of a pre-mRNA [8, 9]. This unique structure, which lacks free 5‘and 3’ ends, confers exceptional stability to RNA exonucleases and gives circRNAs a much longer half-life than their linear counterparts [10]. Their biogenesis is regulated by cis-acting elements (e.g., reverse complementarity motifs such as Alu repeats in flanking introns) and trans-acting factors (e.g., RNA-binding proteins such as Quaking (QKI) and Muscleblind (MBL)) [11]. Once formed, circRNAs are predominantly located in the cytoplasm, although a subset can localize to the nucleus, and their function is largely determined by their subcellular location. The functional repertoire of circRNAs extends far beyond their initial description and includes several key mechanistic roles:

chunks.section: Circular RNAs (circRNAs): biogenesis and multimodal functions
chunks.text:
The most widely studied function involves circRNAs that act as competing endogenous RNAs (ceRNAs) or “miRNA sponges” [12]. By possessing multiple binding sites for specific microRNAs, circRNAs can sequester them and prevent miRNAs from interacting with their target mRNAs. This sponging activity leads to the re-repression of miRNA target genes and effectively increases their expression post-transcriptionally. A classic example is ciRS-7 (also known as CDR1as), which contains more than 70 conserved binding sites for miR-7 and acts as a potent supersponge in neural tissues [13].

circRNAs can interact with RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) to act as bait or protein scaffolds. As a decoy, a circRNA can bind to and cleave a specific RBP, inhibiting its normal function. Conversely, as a scaffold, a circRNA can bring multiple proteins into proximity to facilitate the formation of functional complexes [14]. For example, circFoxo3 interacts with the senescence-associated proteins ID-1 and E2F1 and the transcription factor HIF1α, promoting cardiac aging [15].

circRNAs remaining in the nucleus can influence gene expression at the transcriptional level. Some circRNAs, such as circEIF3J and circPAIP2, can interact with the U1 small nuclear ribonucleoprotein (snRNP) and RNA polymerase II at the promoter of their parental genes, thereby enhancing their transcription [16].

chunks.section: Circular RNAs (circRNAs): biogenesis and multimodal functions
chunks.text: Although most circRNAs are non-coding, a subset containing an internal ribosome entry site (IRES) and an open reading frame (ORF) can be translated into functional peptides or proteins, often in response to cellular stress [1]. These circRNA-derived peptides can exert dominant-negative effects or have unique biological activities.

The most widely studied function involves circRNAs that act as competing endogenous RNAs (ceRNAs) or “miRNA sponges” [12]. By possessing multiple binding sites for specific microRNAs, circRNAs can sequester them and prevent miRNAs from interacting with their target mRNAs. This sponging activity leads to the re-repression of miRNA target genes and effectively increases their expression post-transcriptionally. A classic example is ciRS-7 (also known as CDR1as), which contains more than 70 conserved binding sites for miR-7 and acts as a potent supersponge in neural tissues [13].

circRNAs can interact with RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) to act as bait or protein scaffolds. As a decoy, a circRNA can bind to and cleave a specific RBP, inhibiting its normal function. Conversely, as a scaffold, a circRNA can bring multiple proteins into proximity to facilitate the formation of functional complexes [14]. For example, circFoxo3 interacts with the senescence-associated proteins ID-1 and E2F1 and the transcription factor HIF1α, promoting cardiac aging [15].

circRNAs remaining in the nucleus can influence gene expression at the transcriptional level. Some circRNAs, such as circEIF3J and circPAIP2, can interact with the U1 small nuclear ribonucleoprotein (snRNP) and RNA polymerase II at the promoter of their parental genes, thereby enhancing their transcription [16].

Although most circRNAs are non-coding, a subset containing an internal ribosome entry site (IRES) and an open reading frame (ORF) can be translated into functional peptides or proteins, often in response to cellular stress [1]. These circRNA-derived peptides can exert dominant-negative effects or have unique biological activities.

chunks.section: Enhancer RNAs (eRNAs): biogenesis and transcriptional roles
chunks.text:
Enhancer RNAs are a class of relatively unstable, non-coding RNAs that are transcribed bidirectionally from active enhancer regions [17]. Enhancers are cis-terminal regulatory elements of DNA that control the spatial and temporal precision of gene expression. Transcription at these sites, which produces eRNAs, is a characteristic of functionally active enhancers. The production of eRNAs is closely linked to enhancer activation, which involves the recruitment of transcription factors, co-activators (e.g., p300/CBP), and the Mediator complex that leads to the recruitment of RNA polymerase II [18]. The main functions of eRNAs are intrinsically linked to their role in facilitating enhancer-promoter interaction and target gene transcriptional activation:

A leading model postulates that eRNAs play a role in the formation and stabilization of chromatin loops that place enhancers in close physical proximity to their target gene promoters [19]. This looping is essential for the transport of the transcriptional machinery from enhancer to promoter. For example, eRNAs transcribed from the enhancer of the *Dlx5/6* locus (Evf2) help recruit the helper CBP to stabilize the enhancer-promoter complex [20].

chunks.section: Enhancer RNAs (eRNAs): biogenesis and transcriptional roles
chunks.text:
eRNAs can directly interact with key helper complexes and recruit them to enhancer sites. Studies have shown that eRNAs can bind to the MED12 subunit of the mediator complex and the co-activator CBP, thereby promoting the assembly of a more potent transcriptional machinery and stimulating histone acetylation, which in turn opens chromatin and facilitates transcription [21].

The transcriptional action of eRNAs themselves or their products can affect the local chromatin state. This can include the relocation of nucleosomes or the recruitment of chromatin remodelers, which make the enhancer and its target promoter more accessible to the transcriptional machinery [22].

Some eRNAs are involved in the release of stalled RNA polymerase II to induce productive elongation at target gene promoters. They can achieve this by interacting with and dissociating the negative elongation factor (NELF) complex that allows the polymerase to advance along the gene body [23].

A leading model postulates that eRNAs play a role in the formation and stabilization of chromatin loops that place enhancers in close physical proximity to their target gene promoters [19]. This looping is essential for the transport of the transcriptional machinery from enhancer to promoter. For example, eRNAs transcribed from the enhancer of the *Dlx5/6* locus (Evf2) help recruit the helper CBP to stabilize the enhancer-promoter complex [20].

eRNAs can directly interact with key helper complexes and recruit them to enhancer sites. Studies have shown that eRNAs can bind to the MED12 subunit of the mediator complex and the co-activator CBP, thereby promoting the assembly of a more potent transcriptional machinery and stimulating histone acetylation, which in turn opens chromatin and facilitates transcription [21].

The transcriptional action of eRNAs themselves or their products can affect the local chromatin state. This can include the relocation of nucleosomes or the recruitment of chromatin remodelers, which make the enhancer and its target promoter more accessible to the transcriptional machinery [22].

chunks.section: Release of paused RNA polymerase II
chunks.text: Some eRNAs are involved in the release of stalled RNA polymerase II to induce productive elongation at target gene promoters. They can achieve this by interacting with and dissociating the negative elongation factor (NELF) complex that allows the polymerase to advance along the gene body [23].

chunks.section: Involvement of circRNAs and eRNAs in the brain
chunks.text: Growing evidence points out the role of circRNAs in brain development and disease. The mammalian brain has the highest concentration of circRNAs among tissues. Their presence in synapses suggests they play roles in how neurons function [4–6]. Their expression changes over time and varies by brain region and sex, linking them to neuronal activity and mental health issues [6, 24–27]. During neurodevelopment, circRNA levels peak in the second trimester, which aligns with synaptogenesis and plasticity. This peak correlates with axon guidance, dendrite formation, and neuron growth [4, 28]. CircRNA expression drops after development but stabilizes in mature neurons, where it maintains a strong association with synaptic plasticity [4, 29]. Neural circRNAs increase with age, often showing an inverse relationship with the linear transcript levels of their host genes [25, 26, 30–32]. This age-related rise in synaptosomes suggests a role in synaptic decline [33, 34]. For example, the primate-conserved circGRIA1, which comes from an AMPA receptor gene, builds up in the aging prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. This accumulation disrupts synaptic plasticity and calcium regulation by cis-regulating Gria1 mRNA [34]. Additionally, aging neurons exhibit unique circRNAs, primarily found in synaptosomes, which are not present in linear forms [33].

chunks.section: Involvement of circRNAs and eRNAs in the brain
chunks.text: eRNAs, first discovered in neurons during activity-dependent enhancer transcription [19], are vital for gene regulation in response to stimuli. For instance, Fos-associated eRNAs in rat neurons are crucial for the activity-dependent induction of Fos and the patterns of neuronal firing [35]. In cases of ischemic stroke, 77 eRNAs were found to be upregulated. Knocking down eRNA_06347 and eRNA_093384 increased the size of infarcts, suggesting that they play a protective role [36]. Simon et al. explored the function of eRNAs derived from enhancers that regulate activity-responsive immediate early genes associated with cognitive functions. The authors demonstrated that neuronal activity dynamically influences eRNA expression from these loci. These findings imply that RNAs transcribed from neuronal enhancers are significant contributors to epigenetic landscapes and gene regulatory mechanisms. There is a connection between enhancer activity and brain function [37]. This finding functionally links eRNA transcription directly to neuronal signaling, positioning them as dynamic mediators that translate electrical activity into sustained changes in gene expression. This mechanism is critical for synaptic plasticity and memory formation, and its dysregulation could provide a molecular bridge between synaptic dysfunction and the transcriptional changes observed in neurological diseases such as Alzheimer’s disease.

chunks.section: Involvement of circRNAs and eRNAs in the brain
chunks.text: In a recent study, J. Betti et al. created in silico models to analyze the genetically regulated expression of enhancer RNAs across 49 different cell and tissue types, assessing the extent of their genetic control. Using the estimated genetically regulated expression for eRNAs and canonical genes from a large-scale DNA biobank alongside high-resolution Hi-C contact data, the authors developed a deep learning-based model to predict pairwise three-dimensional chromatin contact frequency for both enhancer-enhancer and enhancer-gene pairs in the cerebellum and whole blood. Importantly, the incorporation of genetically regulated expression of enhancer RNAs offers significant tissue-specific predictive capabilities, which points to the potential role of these transcripts in influencing spatial chromatin organization. The eRNA model, which includes a total of 14,471 unique enhancers expressed across 49 cell and tissue types, identified 64 eRNAs located in the brain, representing 34 distinct eRNAs [22]. This computational evidence significantly advances our understanding by demonstrating that eRNAs are not merely transcriptional byproducts, but may actively contribute to the three-dimensional genomic architecture of the human brain. Thus, disruption of these eRNA-mediated chromatin interactions could be a novel mechanism underlying the dysregulation of gene expression networks in neurodegenerative diseases.

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text:
Neurodegenerative disorders are a group of progressive, irreversible diseases characterized by the slow degeneration and loss of neurons in specific regions of the brain and spinal cord. These conditions are associated with disruptions in essential cellular processes, including autophagy, apoptosis, oxidative stress, neuroinflammation, protein misfolding, mitochondrial dysfunction, and NAs. These pathological alterations ultimately result in functional, cognitive, and motor impairments. The abnormal expression of genes involved in these biological processes, particularly circRNAs and eRNAs, plays important roles in neurodegenerative disorders such as AD, PD, HD, and MS [38–40].

When assessing results from animal models (such as mice, rats, and C. elegans), it is important to evaluate the level of evolutionary conservation of the related ncRNAs and their target genes. A lot of circRNAs and regulatory enhancer sequences revealed important sequence and functional homology between rodents and humans, confirming the practical importance of such models [35, 41]. However, species-specific variations in ncRNA expression, splicing, and regulatory networks need exact confirmation in human cells or tissues to completely determine their pathophysiological significance in human disorders.

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a neurodegenerative disrder characterized by an uncertain onset and a steady progression that results in cognitive impairments in older individuals. This condition is associated with the exacerbation of amyloid β (Aβ) and the hyperphosphorylation of tau, which leads to the formation of neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs) and the failure of neurons [42–45]. Aβ is the primary component in AD. Excessive accumulation of Aβ has been implicated in the induction of neuroinflammation, oxidative stress, and ionic calcium overload, which are believed to be underlying factors contributing to neuronal malfunction and subsequent cognitive disorders [46, 47].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Circular RNAs exhibit an accumulation phenomenon in the brains of older individuals. However, it is important to note that the changes observed in circRNAs due to aging are distinct from those linked to the progression of AD [48, 48–53]. They also exhibit abnormal expression patterns in the brain, cerebrospinal fluid, and serum of individuals affected by AD. It has been identified that a large number of circRNAs are involved in Aβ clearance, neurotrophic signaling, neuroinflammation, and oxidative stress pathways [54–56]. The excessive deposition of Aβ in the cerebral cortex expedites the progression of AD [57]. The process of cleavage by β-secretase and γ-secretase in the APP (amyloid precursor protein) produces Aβ protein, which is primarily eliminated through autophagy in brain cells [54–56]. We summarize the circRNAs that can be effective in AD in Table 1, Supplementary Table S1, and Fig. 1. GO enrichment analysis suggested that a significant number of circRNAs differentially expressed in AD are associated with pathways involved in Aβ turnover. For example, ciRS-7 represents a candidate circRNA that shows high expression levels in healthy human brain but is downregulated in brains of individuals with AD, potentially implicating it in Aβ-related pathways [72]. In a study conducted by Shi et al., it was found that expression of ciRS-7 inhibited the translation of NF-κB and promoted its localization in the cytoplasm, thereby allowing for the derepression of UCHL1 expression, which facilitates the degradation of APP and BACE1. Furthermore, APP decreased the levels of ciRS-7, indicating a reciprocal regulatory relationship between ciRS-7 and APP. These findings elucidated a molecular mechanism that associates reduced ciRS-7 expression with AD, implying that ciRS-7 could serve as a valuable target in the formulation of therapeutic approaches for AD and suggesting a possible neuroprotective function of ciRS-7 [73]. Zhao et al. reported that a deficiency in the sponging activity of ciRS-7 leads to an excess of ambient miRNA-7, which appears to drive the selective down-regulation of miRNA-7-sensitive mRNA targets, including the one that encodes the ubiquitin conjugating enzyme E2A (UBE2A). UBE2A typically functions as a key effector in the ubiquitin-26S proteasome system, facilitating the clearance of amyloid peptides through proteolysis. ciRS-7 is reduced in the brains of individuals with sporadic Alzheimer’s disease (AD), thereby contributing to the accumulation of amyloid and the development of senile plaque deposits [74]. Similarly, CircCwc27, a newly identified circRNA derived from exons, is highly conserved and expressed in abundance within neurons. It diminished the recruitment of Pur-α (purine-rich element-binding protein A or PURA) to the promoters of genes associated with Alzheimer’s disease (AD) by binding to Pur-α. This interaction led to a reduction in Aβ load, helped prevent cognitive decline in AD, and resulted in the downregulation of several genes linked to AD, including APP, Mme, dopamine receptor D1 (Drd1), protein phosphatase 1 regulatory inhibitor subunit 1B (Ppp1r1b), neurotrophic tyrosine kinase receptor type 1 (Ntrk1), and LIM homeobox 8 (Lhx8) [60]. Furthermore, it is worth noting that there is another circRNA, circAβ-a, that acted as a template for the biosynthesis of an Aβ-related protein (Aβ175) in HEK293 cells. Moreover, the distinctive C-terminal peptide of Aβ175 was detected in human brain samples. Additionally, Aβ175 underwent processing into Aβ peptides, indicating a novel pathway for Aβ generation that may provide new insights into the molecular mechanisms contributing to the development of Alzheimer’s disease. This mechanism significantly differs from the traditional Aβ accumulation resulting from the pathophysiological dysregulation of full-length APP proteolytic processing [75].Table 1Key circRNAs and eRNAs in Alzheimer’s disease and their applications as diagnostic/prognostic biomarkersncRNAExpression ChangeCell line studyAnimal studyClinical sampleTarget/PathwayFunctionClinical PotentialReferencesciRS-7Down--Brain and retinal tissue from sporadic Alzheimer’s disease patients and healthy controlsmiR-7ceRNA sponge miR-7; translational repression of UBE2A and EGFR; impaired amyloid clearance and cell signalingA reduced biomarker for AD diagnosis and a therapeutic target to restore UBE2A-dependent amyloid clearance and restore EGFR signaling to reduce β-amyloid accumulation.[58]circHDAC9DownN2a cellsAPP/PS1 model miceSerum from individuals with MCI and ADmiR-138Reduced circHDAC9 expression allows miR-138 to repress Sirt1. n = 3; hippocampal tissue-miR-1896/miR-1955-5p/miR-7030-3p/miR-7033-3p/miR-542-5pSponge ceRNA miR-1896/1955-5p/7030-3p/7033-3p/542-5p; regulation of autophagosome, exostosis, apoptosis, RNA transfer, and splicingBiomarker and therapeutic target of autophagy and vesicular cycling regulation in AD[68]mmu_circRNA_012,180, mmu_circRNA_013,636Down-3-month-old SAMP8 mice-mmu-miR-7231-3pceRNA sponging Top-5 miRNAs to regulate target mRNAs involved in metabolic control, neurotrophin signaling and neuronal development pathways in the hippocampusPotential biomarker and therapeutic target for restoring circRNA-miRNA networks and associated metabolic and neuronal signaling dysregulation in Alzheimer’s disease.[69]circ_0002945UpSK-N-SH human neurons, human primary neurons (HPNs)-Serum from 26 Alzheimer’s disease patients and 26 healthy controlsmiR-431-5p, TNFAIP1circ_0002945 acts as a ceRNA by recruiting miR-431-5p, releasing TNFAIP1 and aggravating Aβ-induced ER stress and apoptosis in neurons.circ_0002945 has potential as a serum biomarker for the diagnosis and prediction of AD and a therapeutic target for inhibiting the circ_0002945/miR-431-5p/TNFAIP1 axis to reduce ER stress and apoptosis.[70]AANCR (eRNA)UpAstrocytes--APOEThis eRNA, which is transcribed from an enhancer upstream of the APOE gene, increases APOE expression and induces an inflammatory phenotype in astrocytes, which contributes to the pathogenesis of AD.Potential therapeutic target for modulating APOE-mediated inflammatory responses.[71]Fig. 1The regulatory roles of circRNAs in Alzheimer’s disease. This shematic highlights some key circRNAs and how they function in the context of AD. For instance, ciRS-7 plays a role in regulating Aβ production and microglial activation through the miR-7/UBE2A pathway. Then there’s circ-AXL, which modulates neuroinflammation and apoptosis via miR-328/BACE1. Additionally, circHOMER1 and circCORO1C are known to influence γ-secretase activity and app expression. Other circRNAs, like circRIMS2, are also implicated in synaptic dysfunction, signaling pathways, and neuronal death. Together, these circRNAs form a crucial network that contributes to the pathogenesis of Alzheimer’s disease

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text:
Key circRNAs and eRNAs in Alzheimer’s disease and their applications as diagnostic/prognostic biomarkers

The regulatory roles of circRNAs in Alzheimer’s disease. This shematic highlights some key circRNAs and how they function in the context of AD. For instance, ciRS-7 plays a role in regulating Aβ production and microglial activation through the miR-7/UBE2A pathway. Then there’s circ-AXL, which modulates neuroinflammation and apoptosis via miR-328/BACE1. Additionally, circHOMER1 and circCORO1C are known to influence γ-secretase activity and app expression. Other circRNAs, like circRIMS2, are also implicated in synaptic dysfunction, signaling pathways, and neuronal death. Together, these circRNAs form a crucial network that contributes to the pathogenesis of Alzheimer’s disease

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: A study conducted by Wu et al. [76] identified a novel circRNA named circAPP, which was found to be upregulated in Aβ-treated microglia and in the hippocampus of APP/PS1 mice. This research demonstrated the regulatory role of circAPP in microglial polarization in AD through the miR-1906/CLIC1 axis. Specifically, circAPP interacts with miR-1906, modulating its expression and consequently increasing the expression and activity of the ion channel CLIC1. In contrast, overexpression of miR-1906 was shown to modulate microglial polarization, improve AD pathology, and enhance cognitive function both in vivo and in vitro, while also reducing the expression and activity of CLIC1. Moreover, the study revealed that silencing circAPP led to reduced hippocampal levels of Aβ and phosphorylated tau (p-tau), improved hippocampal synaptic structure, and ameliorated cognitive deficits resulting from neuroinflammation caused by abnormal microglial polarization in APP/PS1 mice [76].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Neuronal loss occurs in AD because of neuroinflammation and oxidative stress. CircRNAs control these processes. The expression of circ_0002945 was shown to be increased in the serum of individuals with Alzheimer’s disease, as well as in Aβ-induced SK-N-SH cells and human primary neurons. The suppression of circ_0002945 reduced Aβ25–35-induced cell apoptosis and endoplasmic reticulum stress. Additionally, circ_0002945 was shown to regulate miR-431-5p. Furthermore, TNFAIP1 was identified as a target of miR-431-5p, and circ_0002945 acted as a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) to modulate the expression of TNFAIP1 through competition with miR-431-5p. Moreover, the suppression of TNFAIP1 mediated by miR-431-5p contributed to the alleviation of Aβ25–35-induced cell apoptosis and endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress [70]. ER stress is all about the accumulation of proteins that haven’t folded properly, and this can happen due to various physiological and pathological reasons. Factors like increased demand for proteins, exposure to toxins, inflammatory cytokines, and the presence of mutant proteins can all throw the endoplasmic reticulum out of balance [77]. Recent research has shown that this ER stress might actually play a significant role in both the onset and progression of AD [78].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text:
In experimental models, overexpression of circ-AXL was associated with increased apoptosis rates and reduced neurite outgrowth, alongside an elevation of inflammatory cytokines in cellular models of Alzheimer’s disease. Furthermore, circ-AXL directly interacted with miR-328, negatively regulating it while positively modulating BACE1. Additionally, miR-328 was found to directly bind to BACE1, exerting a negative regulatory effect on it. Importantly, miR-328 knockdown diminished the impact of circ-AXL knockdown on cellular models of Alzheimer’s disease. Moreover, BACE1 overexpression exacerbated neuronal injury and inflammation, while also reducing the influence of miR-328 overexpression on these functions within cellular models of Alzheimer’s disease [79].

The activation of microglial cells by Aβ and NFTs leads to the continuous production of inflammation-related cytokines and chemokines, ultimately resulting in the death of neighboring neurons [80–82]. Yang et al. have shown that circ_0000950 is associated with decreased expression of miR-103 and increased expression of Ptgs2 (prostaglandin-endoperoxide synthase 2) in AD patients. In cellular models, manipulation of circ_0000950 was associated with increased neuronal death, inhibition of neural processes, and increased expression of inflammatory cytokines, including IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α levels via directly sponging miR-103 [63].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text:
A significant downregulation of circHOMER1 has been observed in patients with AD, and it shows a direct correlation with disease severity. Moreover, the reduced expression of circHOMER1, which normally acts as an inhibitor of miR-651, leads to decreased expression of PSEN1 and PSEN2 as key AD-related genes and predicted targets of this miRNA. This, in turn, may result in reduced activity of the γ-secretase complex, which is essential for amyloid beta processing [48, 48, 83, 84]. In comparison to circHOMER1, circCORO1C is a circular RNA with increased expression in AD patients, and its upregulation is directly associated with clinical traits and AD-related genes, including APP. By acting as a sponge for miR-105, circCORO1C increases APP and SNCA42 expression, which subsequently exacerbates amyloid plaque formation and synaptic dysfunction in AD subjects. In addition, circCORO1C may mitigate the pathological process of the disease by decreasing the accumulation of Aβ and SNCA in neurons, thereby contributing to symptom alleviation [48].

CircPSEN1 is another circular RNA with elevated expression levels in AD patients. It is involved in the regulation of genes associated with the TGF-β1 and Notch signaling pathways [85], which are key contributors to AD progression [86, 87]. Furthermore, dysregulation of circPSEN1 isoforms has been reported in autosomal dominant forms of AD [62].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: In an AD mouse model, overexpression of circRIMS2 in the hippocampus reduces dendritic spine density and impairs memory [88]. It has been shown that amyloid beta enhances the stability of circRIMS2 and its ability to sponge miR-3968, leading to increased expression of UBE2K (ubiquitin-conjugating enzyme E2 K). Elevated UBE2K levels, in turn, promote increased ubiquitination and degradation of the GRIN2B protein by UBE2K, resulting in synaptic dysfunction [88–90]. In a recent study, Zhao et al. developed circMeta2, a computational tool specifically designed to conduct differential expression analysis focused on circRNA clusters. The authors further have demonstrated how circRNA clusters associated with AD function in a coordinated manner both within and across various brain regions, contributing to the etiology of AD by influencing miRNA-mediated gene expression. Additionally, they have identified significant circRNA clusters that correlate with the progression and severity of AD [91]. In another study, Wang et al. have reported circGigyf2 is notably conserved and exhibited the most significant net reduction specifically in the cortex of 7-month-old 5xFAD mice. The level of circGigyf2 in the cortices of AD patients was found to have a negative correlation with the severity of dementia. The authors have identified several splicing factors affected by AD that are crucial for the biogenesis of circGigyf2. They have discovered and experimentally confirmed the conserved roles of circGigyf2 in sponging miRNAs relevant to AD and RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) associated with AD, including the cleavage and polyadenylation factor 6 (CPSF6). Additionally, the downregulation of circGigyf2 in AD facilitated the silencing activities of its sponged miRNAs (miR-17-5p/93-5p) and improved the processing efficiency of polyadenylation sites for CPSF6 targets. Furthermore, the depletion of circGigyf2 in a mouse neuronal cell line led to the dysregulation of the circGigyf2-miRNA and circGigyf2-CPSF6 pathways, which intensified apoptotic responses to various insults, thereby providing strong evidence for the causative roles of circGigyf2 deficiency in neurodegeneration associated with AD [92]. Significantly, circGigyf2 is widely conserved between mouse and human, and its underexpression in the AD mouse cortex is linked with its decrease in human AD brains, indicating the clinical importance of this approach for studying circRNA failure in neurodegeneration [92].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: In another study, Zhang et al. have clarified the patterns of the circRNA-ceRNA network in the hippocampus of APP/PS1 and WT mice through deep RNA-seq analysis. Their results further enhance the existing understanding of the biology of circRNA-ceRNA, including the signaling pathways they are involved in, such as the dysregulated circRNAs in nucleotide metabolism, the cGMP-PKG signaling pathway, the cAMP signaling pathway, platelet activation, and the Hippo signaling pathway, along with their regulatory roles in the pathogenesis of Alzheimer’s disease (AD). Furthermore, their findings preliminarily confirmed that the novel_circ_0003012/mmu-miR-298-3p/Smoc2 signaling axis might influence the cGMP-PKG signaling pathway. The cGMP-PKG signaling pathway plays a crucial role in preventing cell death and promoting the survival of neural cells. In mice with AD, researchers found that the levels of PKG in the hippocampus were significantly lower, indicating that the cGMP-PKG pathway could be essential in the disease’s underlying processes [93]. In addition, mmu_circ_0001125 and mmu_circ_0000672 have been identified as competing endogenous RNAs (ceRNAs) for mmu-miR-351-5p, which specifically targets Cst7. Cst7, which is also referred to as cystatin F, plays a crucial role by encoding a cathepsin inhibitor. This inhibitor works within the endosomal and lysosomal compartments, helping to regulate the activity of cathepsins in the lysosomal pathway [93]. The expression of Cst7 plays a crucial role in microglia, as it helps to diminish the phagocytic capacity of activated microglia. The amount of CST7 is higher in microglia from APP/PS1 mice and in the brains of people with Alzheimer’s disease. This increase in Cst7 expression may inhibit the clearance of Aβ species via microglia and macrophages [94] (Fig. 1).

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Recent findings suggest that dysregulated eRNAs may play a role in Alzheimer’s disease (AD) by affecting synaptic plasticity, neuroinflammation, and amyloid beta mechanisms. eRNA research could uncover novel molecular mechanisms and therapeutic targets for AD [95] (Table 1). Transcripted eRNAs from two enhancers named Bdnf-Enh g1 and Bdnf-Enh g2 interact with brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) in the intergenic region. After BDNF was turned on, the expression of these eRNAs increased, especially in neurons that had completed their mitotic phase. In addition, they modulate the expression of BDNF in relation to the formation of dendrites [96]. BDNF is known as the gene responsible for the production of a neurotrophic factor. BDNF protein plays various significant roles in the development of the brain, including promoting the survival and differentiation of neurons as well as facilitating long-term potentiation and synaptic plasticity in the mature brain [97, 98]. The increased expression of BDNF has been observed to have neuroprotective effects in response to several stimuli, including environmental enrichment [99, 100] and the administration of antidepressant medications [97, 101]. The dysregulation of BDNF signal transduction has been shown in the brains of individuals with AD. However, the overexpression of BDNF has been shown to enhance learning and memory abilities. Aberrant expression of this protein is believed to elevate the risk of neurodegenerative illnesses [96, 102–104].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Watts et al. have identified a noncoding enhancer RNA associated with apolipoprotein E (APOE), which was designated as APOE-activating noncoding RNA, or AANCR. The pausing of RNA Polymerase II dynamically regulates the expression and functionality of this RNA. When this noncoding enhancer RNA is in its full-length form, it promotes the expression of APOE. Conversely, if this RNA is only partially transcribed, it becomes nonfunctional, resulting in the absence of APOE expression. In certain cells, the noncoding AANCR RNA does not undergo complete transcription due to the pausing of RNA Polymerase II elongation, which is caused by R-loops that are stabilized by RNA A basic sites. Upon experiencing hypertonic stress, the R-loops that inhibit transcription are resolved, allowing AANCR RNA to be transcribed into a full-length enhancer that activates APOE expression. Through genetic analysis, it was demonstrated that sequence variants within the enhancer region influence APOE expression across various cell types, including those in the hippocampus, and are linked to Alzheimer’s disease [105]. The discovery of AANCR provides a mechanistic explanation for how non-coding genetic variants influence Alzheimer’s disease risk, independent of the APOE ε4 allele. The discovery highlights enhancer RNAs as critical functional elements in the genome, where sequence changes can modulate the expression of key disease genes such as APOE, thereby opening new avenues for therapeutic interventions that target regulatory elements rather than the protein-coding gene itself.

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Moreover, the sequence variants located in the AANCR region elucidate the individual differences in APOE expression and the levels of APOE in plasma. The variants present in the AANCR region that influence APOE levels also increase the risk of developing Alzheimer’s disease. Considering the degree of linkage disequilibrium between the variants in AANCR and APOE, the impact of polymorphisms in AANCR operates independently of the risk associated with the APOE ϵ4 allele. Through the regulation of APOE expression, AANCR functions as a modifier of Alzheimer’s disease [105]. Furthermore, another study examining expression profiles revealed that the interaction between DSCAML1 enhancers and their target BACE1 promoter results in the excessive production of Aβ peptides, which ultimately contribute to plaque formation. In turn, Aβ peptides promote the hypomethylation of enhancers that influence neurogenesis and cell cycle genes, which are already prepared for activation due to the typical loss of CpH (non-CpG sites) methylation marks associated with aging. The reactivation of cell cycle genes promotes tau hyperphosphorylation, and together with Aβ plaques, this leads to the development and dissemination of tangle pathology, culminating in neuronal death and cognitive symptoms associated with Alzheimer’s disease [95] (Fig. 5).

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: In a recent investigation, the existence of circRNAs in the cerebrospinal fluid of individuals diagnosed with AD was assessed. The study revealed the up-regulation of 112 circRNAs and the down-regulation of 51 circRNAs [56]. In people with AD, circ-LPAR1, circ-AXL, and circGPHN were upregulated, while circ-PCCA, circ-HAUS4, circKIF18B, and circTTC39C were downregulated [56]. Also, circRNA expression in PBMCs can be utilized to discriminate between individuals with AD and non-AD individuals [55]. Researchers found that people with AD exhibited higher levels of hsa_circRNA_405619 and hsa_circRNA_000843 in their PBMCs than those without the condition. On the other hand, the PBMCs of the same AD patients showed lower levels of hsa_circRNA_100861 and hsa_circRNA_102448 [55].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Moreover, circRNAs demonstrate significant therapeutic potential, especially during the preclinical phases of Alzheimer’s disease (AD). Importantly, circRNAs may be regarded as some of the earliest viable pharmacological targets in the context of AD. Several studies have already shown that utilizing specific miRNAs as drug targets can help alleviate or treat AD in mouse models. Consequently, circRNAs are seen as promising candidates in the realm of AD treatment. Due to their direct influence on protein expression, synthetic circRNAs could be engineered to inhibit the production of proteins associated with AD, employing a therapeutic strategy similar to that used with miRNAs. Additionally, circRNAs, functioning as miRNA sponges, represent another fascinating category of molecules that warrant investigation for AD therapy. Their ability to modify the expression of miRNAs that suppress target mRNAs presents exciting opportunities for novel therapeutic approaches in Alzheimer’s disease [74, 106]. For example, this upregulation is associated with cognitive decline in these models, suggesting a potential regulatory Knockdown of circCwc27 affects the nucleocytoplasmic translocation of Pur-α and is associated with reduced APP expression and Aβ production [60]. Therefore, circCwc27 appears to exert a direct regulatory influence on pathological proteins linked to AD and may represent a viable therapeutic target for AD [107]. The circular RNA associated with histone deacetylase (circHDAC9), which is expressed in the mouse hippocampus, contains multiple binding sites for miR-138. By acting as a sponge for this microRNA, circHDAC9 increases the expression of the target gene Sirtuin 1 (Sirt1), leading to elevated levels of Aβ and triggering neuronal dysfunction, characterized by synaptic deficits and impairments in cognitive functions such as learning and memory [59]. Studies have shown a decreased expression level of circHDAC9 in the serum of AD patients and individuals with mild cognitive impairment [59]. Several differentially expressed circRNAs involved in the Hippo, cGMP-PKG, and cAMP signaling pathways have been identified in the hippocampus of the AD model APP/PS1 mice. These circRNAs influence processes such as axon guidance and platelet activation in neuronal synaptic systems [93, 108] (Table 1 and Supplementary Table S1).

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text:
The interplay between transcriptional and post-transcriptional regulation is particularly evident in Alzheimer’s disease. While the AANCR eRNA drives APOE expression at the transcriptional level [105], multiple circRNAs finely regulate amyloidogenic processing post-transcriptionally. This multilayered regulation creates both vulnerability and opportunity: dysregulation at either level can initiate pathological cascades, but coordinated targeting of eRNAs and circRNAs may offer synergistic therapeutic benefits. For example, simultaneous modulation of AANCR to reduce APOE expression and targeting of circCwc27 to enhance Aβ clearance could potentially provide more comprehensive disease modification than either approach alone.

Parkinson’s disease (PD) is the second most prevalent neurodegenerative disorder among adults in the middle-aged and elderly population [109]. PD is observed in a sporadic manner in around 90 to 95% of cases, whereas the remaining instances are familial and linked to certain autosomal genes such as SNCA [110]. PD is additionally associated with mitochondrial dysfunction, increased oxidative stress, impaired apoptosis, and disorders in the ubiquitin-proteasome system [111].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Recent research has demonstrated that circRNAs play the key roles in the development of PD. Table 2 and Supplementary Table S2 has provided a detailed summary of the key circRNAs dysregulated across PD. To find the circRNAs in the substantia nigra (SN), middle temporal gyrus (MTG), and amygdala, Hanan et al. used RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) to compare samples from PD sufferers and healthy people. Based on available evidence, circSLC8A1 exhibits higher expression levels in the SN of individuals affected by Parkinson’s disease. Furthermore, subjection of SH-SY5Y brain cells to an excessive amount of paraquat (PQ) as an indicator of oxidative stress overexpressed circSLC8A in a dose-dependent manner. Administering the neuroprotective drug simvastatin along with the LRRK2 inhibitor PF-06447475 reversed the effect. Moreover, circSLC8A1 has seven binding sites for miR-128 and regulates the levels and/or activity of it. Additionally, miR-128 target genes, including SIRT1, BMI1, and AXIN1, were upregulated in the brains of the Parkinson’s patients and in cultured neurons treated with PQ [112]. One of the miR-128 targets is SIRT1, which has a role in the aging process and exhibits anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective benefits in neuroinflammatory illnesses such as PD [120]. Another target of miR-128 is BMI1. Neuronal BMI1 is a negative regulator of α-synuclein. In Parkinson’s disease, misfolded and aggregated α-synuclein gives rise to Lewy bodies, which are characteristic of the condition. Studies indicate that BMI1 might play a role in regulating α-synuclein levels, with certain findings suggesting that a deficiency in BMI1 could result in heightened aggregation of α-synuclein [121]. Finally, Axin1 exhibited increased expression in the hippocampal tissues and cells of the MPTP-lesioned mouse model of Parkinson’s disease (PD), whereas Hif1a and miR-128-3p showed decreased expression. The rise in HIF-1α/miR-128-3p levels led to the inhibition of apoptosis in hippocampal neurons through the activation of the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway, which was attributed to the suppression of Axin1 in the context of PD [122]. Similarly, circSLC8A1 exhibits binding affinity towards miR-132. This particular miRNA was modified in Parkinson’s Disease (PD) and plays a crucial role in regulating key mechanisms related to PD, including the maturation of dopaminergic neurons [123].Table 2Key circRNAs and eRNAs in Parkinson’s disease and their applications as diagnostic/prognostic biomarkersncRNAExpression ChangeCell line studyAnimal studyClinical sampleTarget/PathwayFunctionClinical PotentialReferencescircSLC8A1UpSH-SY5Y cells-Postmortem human SN (RNA-seq: 42 PD vs 27 controls; qRT-PCR: 24 PD vs 18 controls)miR-128/Ago2 effector complexSponges miR-128 via Ago2 binding; modulates oxidative-stress-induced changes in miR-128 target mRNAs (BMI1, SIRT1, AXIN1)Potential biomarker for PD diagnosis; targetable by oxidative-stress modulators (statins, LRRK2 inhibitors); therapeutic modulation of miR-128 network via circSLC8A1[112]circDLGAP4DownSH-SY5Y & MN9D + MPP+; viabilityC57BL/6 mice + MPTP (25 mg/kg × 7d); expression-miR-134-5p/CREBcircDLGAP4 recruits miR-134-5p, inhibiting it and facilitating the release of the transcription factor CREB. Activated CREB increases the expression of BDNF and anti-apoptotic genes, reduces mitochondrial ROS production, and enhances the flow of autophagy to clear damaged organelles.It can be measured as a non-invasive biomarker in body fluids (serum or exosomes). Also, restoring circDLGAP4 levels or inhibiting miR-134-5p is considered as a novel therapeutic strategy to activate the CREB pathway and slow the progression of neurodegeneration in Parkinson’s.[113]circSAMD4AUpSH-SY5Y, WBC57BL/6J + MPTP-miR-29c-3p/AMPK mTORSponges miR-29c-3p to activate AMPK and inhibit mTOR, thereby promoting dopaminergic neuron apoptosis and autophagyServes as a potential biomarker and therapeutic target, modulating the circSAMD4A/miR-29c-3p/AMPK/mTOR axis may slow PD progression[114]circSNCAUp in MPP+ PD model; Down after PPX treatmentSH-SY5Y, WB--miR-7/SNCA axisSponges miR-7 to upregulate SNCA and pro-apoptotic proteins (CASP3, BAX, PTEN, P53) while inhibiting autophagy (↓ LC3B-II); PPX-mediated downregulation reduces apoptosis and restores autophagyPotential biomarker of PPX response and therapeutic target, modulating the circSNCA/miR-7/SNCA axis may decrease neuron apoptosis and enhance autophagy in PD[115]Has-circ_0070441UpSH-SY5Y--miR-626 / IRS2 axiscirc_0070441 sponges miR-626, relieving its inhibition on IRS2; elevated IRS2 amplifies MPP+ -induced neuronal apoptosis and pro-inflammatory cytokine productionTargeting circ_0070441 (knockdown) or restoring miR-626 to downregulate IRS2 offers a potential neuroprotective strategy in PD models and may guide future therapeutic development[116]circTLK1Updopaminergic cellsC57BL/6 mice-miR-26a-5p/DAPK1circTLK1 binds and sequesters miR-26a-5p, preventing it from repressing DAPK1. As a result, DAPK1 levels rise, triggering apoptotic signaling, increasing cytotoxicity, and driving dopaminergic neuron injury both in vitro and in vivo.Therapeutic silencing of circTLK1 to free miR-26a-5p and downregulate DAPK1 may protect dopaminergic neurons, ameliorate motor deficits, and represent a novel disease-modifying approach in Parkinson’s disease.[117]circzip-2Detected in C. elegans PD model; differential expression not quantified-Transgenic C. elegans-miR-60/zip-2/DAF-16 axiscirczip-2 sponges miR-60, relieving its repression on zip-2; increased zip-2 promotes DAF-16–mediated stress response, reduces α-synuclein aggregation and extends worm lifespan in the PD modelReveals a circRNA–miRNA–FOXO (DAF-16) regulatory module; targeting this axis may inspire miRNA- or circRNA-based therapies to limit α-syn toxicity and bolster neuronal resilience in Parkinson’s disease[111]PD-associated eRNAsUpLUHMES---Genetic variants associated with PD are enriched in active enhancer regions in differentiating neurons, suggesting that dysregulation of these elements may contribute to neuronal vulnerability in PD.Identifying the gene targets of these enhancers could reveal new therapeutic avenues.[118]SNCA risk enhancer (intron 2)CRISPR/Cas9 deletion of the 439-bp enhancer in iPSC-derived microglia reduces SNCA and MMRN1 expression at Day 40iPSC-derived microglia--SNCA & MMRN1Two PD risk SNPs are located in a microglial enhancer in the SNCA gene. Deletion of this enhancer results in reduced expression of SNCA and MMRN1 and alterations in glucose metabolism genes.This finding provides a mechanistic link between genetic variants, microglial function, and PD pathogenesis.[119]

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text:
Key circRNAs and eRNAs in Parkinson’s disease and their applications as diagnostic/prognostic biomarkers

The SNCA gene, in addition to producing SNCA mRNA, can also generate circular RNAs. One such circRNA, known as circSNCA, originates from the proximal 3′ untranslated region (3′UTR) of SNCA mRNA, which contains a high-affinity binding site for miR-7. Also, when SH-SH5Y cells were subjected to pramipexole, as a dopamine agonist, the circSNCA levels were reduced, resulting in elevated miR-7 and decreased SNCA protein [115]. Elevated levels of intracellular α-synuclein, which is primarily encoded by the SNCA gene in the nucleus, have been associated with the etiology of PD and play a significant role in neurodegenerative disorders [124, 125]. Moreover, there is a correlation between the expression of this gene and the levels of miR-7 in neurons [126]. Additionally, it has been observed that miR-7 expression is decreased in the SN of individuals with PD. The reduction in miR-7 levels is also associated with the loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra and a consequent decrease in striatal dopamine content [127].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Similar to circSNCA, circPANK1 upregulation has been reported in mouse and cellular models of Parkinson’s disease. This circRNA also acts specifically as a sponge for miR-7. Therefore, its increased expression reduces the availability of miR-7, leading to elevated expression of alpha-synuclein (SNCA) protein and, ultimately, enhanced neurodegeneration of dopaminergic neurons [128].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Hsa_circ_0070441 is a circRNA molecule that is derived from the coding area of the SNCA gene, and its expression is elevated in SHSY5Y cells under the influence of 1-methyl-4-phenylpyridinium ions (MPP+), a neural metabolite that leads to impaired cellular respiration and dopaminergic neuron death [116]. Moreover, Hsa_circ_0070441 acts as a sponge for a microRNA named miR-626, and its knockdown has led to a significant reduction in inflammation and apoptosis following MPP+ exposure in these cells. Furthermore, it has been observed that miR-626 modulates the expression of insulin receptor substrate 2. The miR-626/insulin receptor substrate 2 axis has been proposed as a potential diagnostic biomarker for Parkinson’s disease [116]. The circular RNA known as circzip-2, derived from the zip-2 gene (Zinc-regulated transporters and iron-regulated transporter-like protein-2), shows significantly reduced expression in the PD model of C. elegans. It has also been demonstrated that circzip-2 established a competitive inhibitory interaction at the cleavage site with its parent gene, zip-2. The protein encoded by zip-2 directly affects the expression level of the SNCA gene and reactive oxygen species (ROS) concentrations [111]. Moreover, circzip-2 can bind to miR-60, thereby inhibiting the progression of PD. This neuroprotective property of circzip-2 may make it a promising candidate for the development of targeted therapeutics and precise diagnostic tools for neurodegenerative diseases such as PD [111]. Despite C. elegans missing direct orthologs of several human Parkinson’s disorder genes, the common miRNA pathways (like the miR-60 family) and stress response processes make it a model for showing important principles of circRNA-based neuroprotection that may increase investigation in higher organisms.

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text:
The circular RNA derived from the SAMD4A gene, known as circSAMD4A, plays an active role in promoting both apoptosis and autophagy. Moreover, it has been shown that circSAMD4A contributes to dopaminergic neuronal apoptosis and autophagy via the AMPK/mTOR signaling pathway in human neuroblastoma cells (SH-SY5Y) treated with the neurotoxins MPTP and MPP+ [114, 129]. Additionally, the binding of microRNA-29c-3p to circSAMD4A was associated with reduced neurotoxin-induced cytotoxicity in neuronal cells. Therefore, circSAMD4A may serve as a valuable biomarker and a potential therapeutic target for PD [107].

The circular RNA associated with DLG protein (circDLGAP4), which has been reported to be downregulated in MPTP-induced mouse models of PD and in PD cellular models treated with MPP+, may contribute to the onset and progression of PD by affecting the viability of neuronal cells (SH-SY5Y and MN9D), promoting apoptosis, impairing mitochondrial function, and enhancing autophagy [113].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: The circular RNA circTLK1, which is derived from the TLK1 (Tousled-like kinase 1) gene, is a highly upregulated circRNA in MPTP-induced PD mouse models, and its knockdown in these models is associated with reduced apoptosis and cellular toxicity, along with increased cell viability. Moreover, by sequestering miR-26a-5p, circTLK1 regulates the expression of its target gene DAPK1 (Death-associated protein kinase 1), which is involved in apoptotic processes. Through this mechanism, circTLK1 contributes to the improvement of neurological deficits caused by middle cerebral artery occlusion and reperfusion in in vivo models [117]. Therefore, the circTLK1/miR-26a-5p/DAPK1 regulatory axis is considered a prominent mechanism in PD pathogenesis and may provide a foundation for the development of innovative and targeted therapeutic approaches for this disease [107].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text:
In a study involving 300 PD cases and 100 healthy controls, it was found that the circRNAs circARID1B and circTCONS_l2_00002816 exhibit high sensitivity for the early diagnosis of PD. Whereas, circFAM83H, circHUWE1, circARID1B, and circTCONS_l2_00002816 were identified as potential biomarkers capable of distinguishing between early and advanced stages of PD [130]. CircEps15 has also been identified in both human and animal studies [131]. Its reduced expression in the plasma of PD patients is associated with disease progression. circEps15 functions by sponging miR-24, thereby maintaining PINK1 gene expression and promoting the PINK1-PRKN–dependent mitophagy pathway, which is essential for the clearance of damaged mitochondria and maintaining mitochondrial homeostasis in neurons [131]. Top circRNAs in PD development is indicated in Fig. 2.Fig. 2Illustration depicting the roles of circRNA in Parkinson’s disease. It highlights how circRNAs by sponging various miRNAs and interacting with and α–synuclein for regulating PD

Illustration depicting the roles of circRNA in Parkinson’s disease. It highlights how circRNAs by sponging various miRNAs and interacting with and α–synuclein for regulating PD

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Dysregulation of eRNAs in PD can disrupt dopaminergic neuron function and be a cause of neurodegeneration. The study of eRNAs may reveal new insights into the mechanisms of PD and its therapeutic targets (Table 2). The production of an eRNA from intron 2 of the KANSL1 gene has been linked to Polymorphisms on chromosome 17q21 in dopamine neurons that are associated with PD. The dysregulation of KANSL1 hinders the process of autophagy, leading to the promotion of memory impairment and neurodegeneration [132, 133]. Studies have shown that the intronic enhancers of the SNCA gene form clusters of enhancers in the human brain, where single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) highly associated with PD risk are enriched. These enhancer cluster regions have the potential to liberate RNA polymerase II from the promoter region of the SNCA gene, thereby exerting regulatory control over its expression [134]. One proposed idea posits that the eRNAs generated from these enhancers are capable of promoting transcription elongation by dissociating the negative elongation factor (NELF) complex. On the other hand, deletion of the intronic enhancer clusters of the SNCA gene has been shown to significantly downregulate SNCA expression in the brain by inhibiting its transcription elongation [135]. This finding highlights the potential of targeting these enhancer regions as a novel therapeutic strategy for treating neurodegenerative disorders associated with SNCA accumulation in the brain (Fig. 5).

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Zhong et al. [130] investigated the prognostic and diagnostic applicability of circRNAs during the early and progressive stages of PD. The analysis conducted on the plasmatic circRNAs of three individuals diagnosed with PD, compared to a control group, used microarray technology. The results of this analysis revealed that two specific circRNAs, namely circ_0004381 and circ_0017204 (referred to as circARID1B and circTCONS-l200002816, respectively), exhibited the potential to accurately predict PD at an early stage of diagnosis. This predictive ability was achieved by employing risk score formulas and conducting receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve analysis, which demonstrated relatively high sensitivity and specificity. In addition, the circRNA molecules circ_0085869, circ_0004381, circ_0017204, and circ_0090668, which are respectively referred to as circFAM83H, circARID1B, circTCONS-l2-00002816, and circHUWE1, have potential discriminatory capabilities between late-stage and early-stage PD.

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text:
In conclusion, these findings suggest that a cell-free circRNA panel holds promise as a non-invasive biomarker for the early detection or continuous monitoring of PD progression [130]. Ravanidis et al. conducted a study to further explore the potential of circRNAs as biomarkers in a larger group of individuals with PD. The outcome of this study identified six circRNAs (MAPK9_circ_0001566, HOMER1_circ_0006916, SLAIN1_circ_0000497, DOP1B_circ_0001187, RESP1_circ_0004368, and PSEN1_circ_0003848) that are enriched in the brain and are significantly downregulated in PBMCs of PD subjects compared to healthy ones [136]. A different study suggested that circzip-2 can bind with miR-60, consequently hindering the advancement of PD. This neuroprotective characteristic of circzip-2 could position it as a potential candidate for creating targeted therapies and accurate diagnostic instruments for neurodegenerative disorders like PD [111] (Table 2 and Supplementary Table S2).

Huntington’s Disease (HD) is an atypical autosomal dominant neurodegenerative condition characterized by the progressive loss of cortical and striatal neurons. Individuals with Huntington’s disease (HD) have expressed motor skills, memory function, and overall cognitive abilities [137]. HD is the most prevalent trinucleotide repeat disorder. The expansion of CAG repeats within exon 1 of the Huntingtin (HTT) gene has been identified as the underlying etiology of HD [138].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: As a result of CAG expansion within the first exon, a polyglutamine mutant HTT (mHTT) protein is produced, which is prone to misfolding and aggregation and has a higher tendency to interact with proteins in ways that aren’t normal. The role of HTT encompasses various cellular processes, including axonal trafficking, gene regulation, disruption of the ubiquitin-proteasome system, and autophagy [139]. Dysregulation of autophagy has been implicated in the early cognitive changes observed in Huntington’s disease. Therefore, it undergoes alterations and qualifies as a potential candidate for Huntington’s disease treatment [140].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: circRNAs have been shown that differentially expressed in HD. Table 3 summarizes the expression patterns of key circRNAs in HD. In addition, Several circRNAs have been shown to play a role in regulating autophagy in HD; examples include circLRP1B, circDLGAP4, and circHIPK2, which inhibit miR-27a-3p, miR-134-5p, and miR-124, respectively. Emerging evidence suggests that circRNAs may influence the pathogenesis of HD through potential modulation of the autophagy process [144]. Nonetheless, to validate this hypothesis, a rigorous investigation of the role of autophagy-related circRNAs in the progression of HD is necessary. In addition, when circ-016719 is overexpressed in neurons, it acts as a sponge for miR-29c, which starts the process of autophagy. Circ-016719 stimulates the expression of MAP2K6 and BECN1 and, as a consequence, increases the rate of autophagy within the neuronal cells. The function of circ-016719 was confirmed using knockdown experiments, which effectively inhibited the process of autophagy [145].Table 3Key circRNAs and eRNAs in Huntington’s disease and their applications as diagnostic/prognostic biomarkersncRNAExpression ChangeCell line studyAnimal studyClinical sampleTarget/PathwayFunctionClinical PotentialReferencescircHTT(2–6) (exons 2–6)UpSH-SY5Y, HEK293-7 HD patients, 4 controls (frontal cortex tissue)HTTBy modulating HTT nuclear localization and influencing nuclear morphology and proliferation, circHTT(2–6) recapitulates HD-like cellular phenotypes without altering repeat lengthUpregulation correlates with CAG burden and nuclear pathologies, suggesting utility as a biomarker of disease severity and a target to restore HTT localization[141]circHTT(2,3,4,5,6)UpST Hdh Q111/Q111 striatal cells, HEK293T and SH-SY5YzQ175 and Htt Q111/20 knock-in miceiPSC-derived terminally differentiated neurons (hTDNs) from HD patients (CAG 43–180) vs controls ( < 36)40S ribosomal subunitAlthough this circRNA is not translated in the adult mouse brain, its binding to ribosomes suggests that it may regulate the translation of other proteins. This could contribute to the imbalance of proteostasis in HD.Potential molecular modulator in HD pathogenesis.[142]Overall circRNA populationdysregulationPC12 neuron-like cellsmESCs and mNPCs derived from Htt CAG-expansion knock-in mice-Dopaminergic synapse; MAPK; long-term depressionInferred involvement in synaptic and signaling pathways; specific mechanisms unvalidatedNot yet assessed in patient samples; stability and blood detectability suggest potential as HD biomarkers[143]

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text:
Key circRNAs and eRNAs in Huntington’s disease and their applications as diagnostic/prognostic biomarkers

The association between miR-142 and HD has been established through the circHectd1/miR-142/autophagy axis. In astrocytes, upregulated circHectd1 has been shown to enhance TIPARP expression level via the miR-142-TIPARP axis. Subsequently, elevated TIPARP levels trigger the production of LC3-II, hence promoting the activation of autophagy [146].

Based on evidence, the circHTT (2–6), which encompasses exons 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 of the HTT gene, has been upregulated in the brains of HD individuals. The upregulation of circHTT (2–6) in HEK293 and SH-SY5Y cells demonstrated no modification to the CAG repeat region, which is considered the primary cause of HD. However, this upregulation lead to a decrease in cell proliferation, nuclear size, and nuclear localization of the HTT protein. These effects were consistent with the pathophysiological abnormalities observed in HD [141].

It was demonstrated in a study that circAKAP7 specifically interacted with miR-155-5p, hence activating ATG12 as an autophagy-related gene and consequently stimulating autophagy [147]. Growing evidence have indicated the importance of autophagy in maintenance of the synapse in HD [140].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text:
In a study conducted by Ayyildiz and colleagues using HD mouse models and RNA sequencing (RNA-seq), it was demonstrated that although neural progenitor cells (NPCs) predominantly express more circRNAs than embryonic stem cells (ESCs), cells harboring the mutant HTT gene exhibit an aberrant set of linear and back-splicing-generated circular RNA isoforms. Notably, circRNA expression levels in NPCs were inversely correlated with the length of the CAG repeat expansion in the HTT gene, indicating HTT mutation impairs RNA circularization. Furthermore, approximately 32.7% of the differentially expressed circRNAs showed N6-methyladenosine (m6A) modifications. This suggest that mutant huntingtin may disrupt circRNA biogenesis via m6A-mediated regulatory mechanisms, particularly in NPCs [148] (Fig. 3).Fig. 3The schematic illustration demonstrates the roles of different circRNAs in Huntington’s disease. circRNAs by sponging some miRNAs and influencing various genes, modulating HD

The schematic illustration demonstrates the roles of different circRNAs in Huntington’s disease. circRNAs by sponging some miRNAs and influencing various genes, modulating HD

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text:
In HD, eRNA dysregulation can abolish transcriptional networks to lead to mutant HTT-induced neurodegeneration. eRNA dysregulation could offer new insights into HD pathogenesis and therapeutic targeting [149]. A brain-specific ultraconservative eRNA called Evf2 can help DLX and MECP2 control the homeodomain transcription factor Dlx5/6 in their intergenic enhancer regions. Evf2 mutations during the early postnatal period decrease the number of GABAergic interneurons in the hippocampus and dentate gyrus. Additionally, these mutations have been found to diminish synaptic blockage in the brains of adult individuals [150].

GABA-regulated circuit dysfunction, which disrupts the balance between excitatory and inhibitory signaling, has been strongly implicated in a lot of neurological conditions, including neurodegenerative disorders such as Huntington’s disease and epilepsy, neurodevelopmental disorders like autism and Tourette’s syndrome, as well as other disorders, including dystonia and hepatic encephalopathy [151–153] (Table 3 and Fig. 5).

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: The study of circRNAs and eRNAs in Huntington’s disease, while still in its early stages, reveals unique diagnostic and therapeutic opportunities shaped by the monogenic nature of the disease. In contrast to sporadic neurological disorders, the clear genetic etiology of HD provides a clear context for interpreting ncRNA dysregulation. The inverse correlation between CAG repeat length and expression of specific circRNAs in neural progenitor cells suggests that circRNA signatures can serve as sensitive molecular readouts of “genetic burden,” potentially reflecting disease susceptibility and time to progression even before the onset of symptoms [148]. Furthermore, the accumulation of circHTT variants, which mimic cellular pathologies such as aberrant nuclear localization of HTT without themselves containing expanded CAG repeats, positions them as novel biomarkers that report cellular stress responses downstream of mutant HTT protein [142].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Regulatory networks formed by circRNAs, such as the circHectd1/miR-142/TIPARP and circAKAP7/miR-155-5p/ATG12 axes, highlight autophagy as a central pathway that is susceptible to targeted circRNA intervention [147, 154]. Modulation of these circuits could enhance clearance of mutant huntingtin protein. From a transcriptional perspective, dysfunction of GABAergic circuits, which are regulated in part by eRNAs such as Evf2, suggests a compelling strategy [150]. Several studies have conducted additional research to explore the clinical relevance of eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders. For instance, evidence indicates that eRNA affects neuronal genes related to HD within the striatum. This suggests targeting striatal enhancers to boost eRNA expressions and improving the neural gene repression observed in HD patients [149]. In the context of neurodegenerative disorders, most of the research on how eRNAs can be used in the clinic, such as for diagnosis or targeted therapy, is still in its early stages [155] (Tables 3). Targeted epigenetic therapies or engineered transcription factors designed to stabilize the expression of key eRNAs could restore inhibitory balance and counteract the excitotoxicity observed in HD, offering a novel approach to correct the underlying transcriptional defects caused by mutant HTT.

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: While MS is classically considered a demyelinating autoimmune disease, progressive neurodegeneration is recognized as the main substrate for irreversible neurological disability in its chronic stages. The pathological continuum of MS includes inflammatory demyelination and subsequent axonal scission, neuronal loss, and synaptic damage, hallmarks shared with conventional neurodegenerative disorders. This neurodegenerative component is particularly prevalent in progressive forms of MS, where clinical deterioration continues independent of overt inflammatory activity [156]. Therefore, examining circRNAs and eRNAs in the context of MS offers a unique opportunity to investigate how ncRNA networks associated with neuroinflammatory stimuli are linked to downstream neurodegenerative processes. Specifically, these regulatory RNAs may modulate: (1) glial activation states and neuroinflammatory cascades; (2) mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress in neurons; (3) remyelination failure and oligodendrocyte pathology; and (4) direct mechanisms of axonal degeneration. Therefore, the investigation of circRNAs and eRNAs in MS provides important insights into the regulatory mechanisms that drive neurodegeneration in both autoimmune and proteinopathies [157].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: The clinical subgroups encompass clinically isolated syndrome (CIS), relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (RRMS), primary progressive multiple sclerosis (PPMS), and secondary progressive multiple sclerosis (SPMS) [158]. The predominant manifestation of the disease is RRMS, which represents approximately 80% of cases and is characterized by recurring episodes of disease exacerbation. PPMS manifests in around 5–15% of cases, displaying a distinct clinical profile characterized by a gradual deterioration of neurological function without the occurrence of relapses. Following a period of 10–15 years, RRMS undergoes a transition to a secondary progressive form characterized by a slow advancement of the disease [159].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: CircRNAs have been implicated in the development and progression of autoimmune diseases, such as MS. A large number of circular RNAs in peripheral blood exhibited differential expression in the MS cohort [160, 161].
