chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text:
Key circRNAs and eRNAs in multiple sclerosis and their applications as diagnostic/prognostic biomarkers

It has been observed that hsa_circ_0106803, originating from the GSDMB gene, exhibited a 2.8-fold increase in expression levels among individuals diagnosed with RRMS [167]. Multiple miRNAs were identified as having multiple target sites in hsa_circ_0106803, notably including miR-1275 and miR-149, both of which exhibit differential expression in the blood of individuals with MS [170].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: miR-149 has been observed to interact with the protein ASIC1a, resulting in ASIC1a downregulation [171]. The ASIC1a gene is responsible for encoding a specific subunit of the acid-sensing ion channel. This particular subunit is found to be highly expressed in acute MS lesions, suggesting its potential involvement in neurological disease mechanisms underlying the development of this illness [112, 120, 123, 172]. Based on the available evidence, researchers have put forth the concept of a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) network, suggesting that hsa_circ_0106803 may have a role in influencing the development of MS via controlling the expression of ASIC1a mRNA through miR-149 [173]. miR-1275 is a microRNA coding gene that is functional and has been directly associated with multiple sclerosis. This microRNA is involved in the TGF-beta signaling pathway and has been confirmed to exhibit varying methylation statuses along with distinct expression patterns across different IDH expression glioma subtypes [174].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Studies have shown a significant association between the expression level of the circular RNA circINPP4B and the clinical prognosis of MS. In the experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE) mice, circINPP4B expression was markedly elevated, and this increase was directly correlated with the severity of clinical symptoms. Furthermore, circINPP4B acts as a sponge for miR-30a, a microRNA whose overexpression inhibits excessive Th17 cell differentiation, a key pathogenic factor in MS and EAE. By sequestering miR-30a, circINPP4B suppresses its anti-inflammatory effects, leading to enhanced Th17 differentiation and progression of EAE [175].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Recent evidence indicates that the circular RNA HECW2 (circ_HECW2) plays a pivotal role in the pathogenesis of MS. Elevated expression of circ_HECW2 has been observed in both in vitro and in vivo MS models and is associated with the induction of endothelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EndoMT), a process recognized as a critical contributor to blood-brain barrier (BBB) impairment and increased permeability. Yang et al. reported that circ_HECW2 functions as a sponge for microRNA-30D (miR-30D), thereby resulting in upregulation of ATG5, a core component of the autophagy pathway, activating the NOTCH signaling pathway, and ultimately promoting LPS-induced EndoMT [176]. In addition, Dong and his team revealed that circ_HECW2 also interacts with miR-30e-5p and, through modulation of neural growth regulator 1 (NGR1) levels, inhibits endothelial cell proliferation, increases apoptosis, and enhances LPS-induced EndoMT [177].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text:
For the first time, a study based on transcriptomic data classified MS into three distinct molecular subtypes: MS-FCRL1 (C1), MS-BTG1 (C2), and MS-RPL38 (C3). Each of these subtypes is linked to various immune- and infection-related signaling pathways. In particular, the investigation of circRNA/miRNA regulatory networks in this study identified 18 differentially expressed circRNAs and 22 differentially expressed miRNAs implicated in subtype C3. Furthermore, EEF1D and TUBA1A were identified as central hub genes involved in neural and immune functions within this subtype, and finally, the circ_0045537/miR-196a-5p/TUBA1A axis was proposed as a potential regulatory pathway modulating disease severity in the C3 subtype [178]. Also, Lodde et al. reported a significant decrease in the expression level of hsa_circ_0018905 in patients with higher levels of disability and in progressive forms of multiple sclerosis (MS), suggesting its potential as a biomarker for MS [179] (Fig. 4).Fig. 4The schematic diagram represents the functional importance of circRNAs in multiple sclerosis and their related roles as a inhibitor, activator, and sponge by binding to miRNA

The schematic diagram represents the functional importance of circRNAs in multiple sclerosis and their related roles as a inhibitor, activator, and sponge by binding to miRNA

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: In another study, circ_0005402 and circ_0035560 from the ANXA2 gene exhibit reduced expression levels in patients with multiple sclerosis, indicating their potential as biomarkers for the disease. They discovered that circ_0005402 contains binding sites for several RNA-binding proteins, including AGO2, EIF4A3, LIN28A, IGF2BP1, and PTB. Additionally, it features two binding sites for miR-1248 and miR-766. This suggests the possibility of a collaborative regulation involving a circRNA-miRNA-mRNA axis in the pathophysiology of MS [166]. In a study by Mohammed, it was reported that ANXA2 is recognized for its involvement in the passage through the blood-brain barrier (BBB) and for enhancing miR-155 expression through the phosphorylation of STAT3 (Signal Transducer and Activator of Transcription 3). Notably, miR-155 serves as a biomarker for multiple sclerosis (MS), detectable in the bodily fluids of patients prior to the disease’s onset, and has been reported to rise in expression during relapse phases [157].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Bioinformatics research of circRNAs derived from non-coding regions of the genome linked to MS showed that these circRNAs might make people more likely to get MS [180]. Another study revealed an association between immunoglobulin (IgG) levels in the brain fluid and the presence of exosomal circRNAs in MS [181]. In addition, Zurawska et al. have recently provided a study on circRNAs that 3 circRNAs, hsa_circRNA_101348, hsa_circRNA_102611, and hsa_circRNA104361, are differentially overexpressed in individuals with RRMS in relapse and have the ability to influence B-cell activity [160]. Furthermore, these three circRNAs exhibited a significant correlation with MRI Gd-enhancing lesions, thereby suggesting their potential involvement in the activity of inflammatory diseases. Given the critical role of B cells in the pathogenesis of multiple sclerosis (MS), they have uncovered two transcripts related to B cells, namely AK2 and IKZF3 [160]. These observations were corroborated by the direct evidence of an upregulation of AK2 and IKZF3 in the peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) of patients experiencing a relapse of MS. The AK2 gene is recognized for encoding phosphotransferase adenylate kinase 2, fulfilling the cell-specific requirements necessary for mitochondrial functions, especially in the activation of B cells and the production of antibodies. The significance of AK2 has been particularly highlighted in B cells infected with the Epstein-Barr virus, which has been extensively discussed in relation to MS as a viral infection associated with the disease. IKZF3 regulates the development of lymphoid and myeloid cells as well as immune homeostasis [160].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text:
While the bulk of circRNAs that were differentially expressed exhibited upregulation, it is worth noting that a subset of circRNAs showed downregulation in those affected by MS.

The results of the global hybridization experiment, which was then confirmed by PCR in a separate group of people, showed that there were two circRNAs, hsa_circRNA_101145 and hsa_circRNA_001896, with expression levels that were significantly lower in MS patients during remission and significantly associated with alleviation in disease-induced disability [165]. In people with RRMS who are in remission, reducing the amount of hsa_circRNA_101145 and hsa_circRNA_001896 could increase the function of all miRNAs that are vulnerable to being targeted by these two circRNAs. One example of the mentioned miRNAs is miR-181c, whose increased level in the cerebrospinal fluid has been linked to a higher chance that clinically isolated syndrome (CIS) will turn into confirmed MS [182].

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: There is new evidence suggesting that eRNA dysregulation may be involved in MS pathogenesis via alterations of immune response and neuronal function. Understanding of eRNAs has the potential to provide new insights into MS mechanisms and therapeutic targets [183]. Several study investigated the role of super-enhancers (SEs) and enhancer RNAs (eRNAs) in autoimmune diseases. Their findings indicated that increased activity or dysfunction of individual SEs could lead to the development of various autoimmune disorders [184, 185, 185–191]. However, despite growing interest in the regulatory roles of eRNAs in autoimmune diseases and neurodegenerative disorders, there is currently no direct evidence supporting the involvement of eRNAs in the pathogenesis of MS as a disease considered both autoimmune and neurodegenerative. Nevertheless, one study demonstrated that MS-associated single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) are located within vitamin D receptor (VDR) binding sites and in activated super-enhancers (SEs), especially in VDR-associated SEs (VSEs), in CD4+ T cells and monocytes [184, 188] (Table 4 and Supplementary Table S3). These disease-linked SNPs are believed to influence the regulatory function of these SEs. This spatial correspondence between MS risk SNPs and active enhancer markers strongly suggests that dysregulation of SEs and their associated eRNAs is a key component of MS pathophysiology. This hypothesis proposes that genetic risk is exerted in part by altering the transcriptional output of immune cells via enhancer elements, and provides a new perspective linking genetic susceptibility to functional dysregulation in MS, and identifies eRNAs generated from these SEs as potential biomarkers or therapeutic targets. However, it remains unclear how the presence of these SNPs affects the expression of these SE-derived eRNAs and what impact these eRNAs might have on MS pathogenesis. Thus, this area of research remains largely unexplored and requires further investigation in the future (Fig. 5).Fig. 5Mechanistic roles of eRNAs in neurodegenerative diseases

chunks.section: Functional roles of circRNAs and eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders
chunks.text: Mechanistic roles of eRNAs in neurodegenerative diseases

Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a neurodegenerative disrder characterized by an uncertain onset and a steady progression that results in cognitive impairments in older individuals. This condition is associated with the exacerbation of amyloid β (Aβ) and the hyperphosphorylation of tau, which leads to the formation of neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs) and the failure of neurons [42–45]. Aβ is the primary component in AD. Excessive accumulation of Aβ has been implicated in the induction of neuroinflammation, oxidative stress, and ionic calcium overload, which are believed to be underlying factors contributing to neuronal malfunction and subsequent cognitive disorders [46, 47].

Circular RNAs exhibit an accumulation phenomenon in the brains of older individuals. However, it is important to note that the changes observed in circRNAs due to aging are distinct from those linked to the progression of AD [48, 48–53]. They also exhibit abnormal expression patterns in the brain, cerebrospinal fluid, and serum of individuals affected by AD. It has been identified that a large number of circRNAs are involved in Aβ clearance, neurotrophic signaling, neuroinflammation, and oxidative stress pathways [54–56]. The excessive deposition of Aβ in the cerebral cortex expedites the progression of AD [57]. The process of cleavage by β-secretase and γ-secretase in the APP (amyloid precursor protein) produces Aβ protein, which is primarily eliminated through autophagy in brain cells [54–56]. We summarize the circRNAs that can be effective in AD in Table 1, Supplementary Table S1, and Fig. 1. GO enrichment analysis suggested that a significant number of circRNAs differentially expressed in AD are associated with pathways involved in Aβ turnover. For example, ciRS-7 represents a candidate circRNA that shows high expression levels in healthy human brain but is downregulated in brains of individuals with AD, potentially implicating it in Aβ-related pathways [72]. In a study conducted by Shi et al., it was found that expression of ciRS-7 inhibited the translation of NF-κB and promoted its localization in the cytoplasm, thereby allowing for the derepression of UCHL1 expression, which facilitates the degradation of APP and BACE1. Furthermore, APP decreased the levels of ciRS-7, indicating a reciprocal regulatory relationship between ciRS-7 and APP. These findings elucidated a molecular mechanism that associates reduced ciRS-7 expression with AD, implying that ciRS-7 could serve as a valuable target in the formulation of therapeutic approaches for AD and suggesting a possible neuroprotective function of ciRS-7 [73]. Zhao et al. reported that a deficiency in the sponging activity of ciRS-7 leads to an excess of ambient miRNA-7, which appears to drive the selective down-regulation of miRNA-7-sensitive mRNA targets, including the one that encodes the ubiquitin conjugating enzyme E2A (UBE2A). UBE2A typically functions as a key effector in the ubiquitin-26S proteasome system, facilitating the clearance of amyloid peptides through proteolysis. ciRS-7 is reduced in the brains of individuals with sporadic Alzheimer’s disease (AD), thereby contributing to the accumulation of amyloid and the development of senile plaque deposits [74]. Similarly, CircCwc27, a newly identified circRNA derived from exons, is highly conserved and expressed in abundance within neurons. It diminished the recruitment of Pur-α (purine-rich element-binding protein A or PURA) to the promoters of genes associated with Alzheimer’s disease (AD) by binding to Pur-α. This interaction led to a reduction in Aβ load, helped prevent cognitive decline in AD, and resulted in the downregulation of several genes linked to AD, including APP, Mme, dopamine receptor D1 (Drd1), protein phosphatase 1 regulatory inhibitor subunit 1B (Ppp1r1b), neurotrophic tyrosine kinase receptor type 1 (Ntrk1), and LIM homeobox 8 (Lhx8) [60]. Furthermore, it is worth noting that there is another circRNA, circAβ-a, that acted as a template for the biosynthesis of an Aβ-related protein (Aβ175) in HEK293 cells. Moreover, the distinctive C-terminal peptide of Aβ175 was detected in human brain samples. Additionally, Aβ175 underwent processing into Aβ peptides, indicating a novel pathway for Aβ generation that may provide new insights into the molecular mechanisms contributing to the development of Alzheimer’s disease. This mechanism significantly differs from the traditional Aβ accumulation resulting from the pathophysiological dysregulation of full-length APP proteolytic processing [75].Table 1Key circRNAs and eRNAs in Alzheimer’s disease and their applications as diagnostic/prognostic biomarkersncRNAExpression ChangeCell line studyAnimal studyClinical sampleTarget/PathwayFunctionClinical PotentialReferencesciRS-7Down--Brain and retinal tissue from sporadic Alzheimer’s disease patients and healthy controlsmiR-7ceRNA sponge miR-7; translational repression of UBE2A and EGFR; impaired amyloid clearance and cell signalingA reduced biomarker for AD diagnosis and a therapeutic target to restore UBE2A-dependent amyloid clearance and restore EGFR signaling to reduce β-amyloid accumulation.[58]circHDAC9DownN2a cellsAPP/PS1 model miceSerum from individuals with MCI and ADmiR-138Reduced circHDAC9 expression allows miR-138 to repress Sirt1. This shematic highlights some key circRNAs and how they function in the context of AD. For instance, ciRS-7 plays a role in regulating Aβ production and microglial activation through the miR-7/UBE2A pathway. Then there’s circ-AXL, which modulates neuroinflammation and apoptosis via miR-328/BACE1. Additionally, circHOMER1 and circCORO1C are known to influence γ-secretase activity and app expression. Other circRNAs, like circRIMS2, are also implicated in synaptic dysfunction, signaling pathways, and neuronal death. Together, these circRNAs form a crucial network that contributes to the pathogenesis of Alzheimer’s disease

Key circRNAs and eRNAs in Alzheimer’s disease and their applications as diagnostic/prognostic biomarkers

The regulatory roles of circRNAs in Alzheimer’s disease. This shematic highlights some key circRNAs and how they function in the context of AD. For instance, ciRS-7 plays a role in regulating Aβ production and microglial activation through the miR-7/UBE2A pathway. Then there’s circ-AXL, which modulates neuroinflammation and apoptosis via miR-328/BACE1. Additionally, circHOMER1 and circCORO1C are known to influence γ-secretase activity and app expression. Other circRNAs, like circRIMS2, are also implicated in synaptic dysfunction, signaling pathways, and neuronal death. Together, these circRNAs form a crucial network that contributes to the pathogenesis of Alzheimer’s disease

A study conducted by Wu et al. [76] identified a novel circRNA named circAPP, which was found to be upregulated in Aβ-treated microglia and in the hippocampus of APP/PS1 mice. This research demonstrated the regulatory role of circAPP in microglial polarization in AD through the miR-1906/CLIC1 axis. Specifically, circAPP interacts with miR-1906, modulating its expression and consequently increasing the expression and activity of the ion channel CLIC1. In contrast, overexpression of miR-1906 was shown to modulate microglial polarization, improve AD pathology, and enhance cognitive function both in vivo and in vitro, while also reducing the expression and activity of CLIC1. Moreover, the study revealed that silencing circAPP led to reduced hippocampal levels of Aβ and phosphorylated tau (p-tau), improved hippocampal synaptic structure, and ameliorated cognitive deficits resulting from neuroinflammation caused by abnormal microglial polarization in APP/PS1 mice [76].

Neuronal loss occurs in AD because of neuroinflammation and oxidative stress. CircRNAs control these processes. The expression of circ_0002945 was shown to be increased in the serum of individuals with Alzheimer’s disease, as well as in Aβ-induced SK-N-SH cells and human primary neurons. The suppression of circ_0002945 reduced Aβ25–35-induced cell apoptosis and endoplasmic reticulum stress. Additionally, circ_0002945 was shown to regulate miR-431-5p. Furthermore, TNFAIP1 was identified as a target of miR-431-5p, and circ_0002945 acted as a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) to modulate the expression of TNFAIP1 through competition with miR-431-5p. Moreover, the suppression of TNFAIP1 mediated by miR-431-5p contributed to the alleviation of Aβ25–35-induced cell apoptosis and endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress [70]. ER stress is all about the accumulation of proteins that haven’t folded properly, and this can happen due to various physiological and pathological reasons. Factors like increased demand for proteins, exposure to toxins, inflammatory cytokines, and the presence of mutant proteins can all throw the endoplasmic reticulum out of balance [77]. Recent research has shown that this ER stress might actually play a significant role in both the onset and progression of AD [78].

In experimental models, overexpression of circ-AXL was associated with increased apoptosis rates and reduced neurite outgrowth, alongside an elevation of inflammatory cytokines in cellular models of Alzheimer’s disease. Furthermore, circ-AXL directly interacted with miR-328, negatively regulating it while positively modulating BACE1. Additionally, miR-328 was found to directly bind to BACE1, exerting a negative regulatory effect on it. Importantly, miR-328 knockdown diminished the impact of circ-AXL knockdown on cellular models of Alzheimer’s disease. Moreover, BACE1 overexpression exacerbated neuronal injury and inflammation, while also reducing the influence of miR-328 overexpression on these functions within cellular models of Alzheimer’s disease [79].

The activation of microglial cells by Aβ and NFTs leads to the continuous production of inflammation-related cytokines and chemokines, ultimately resulting in the death of neighboring neurons [80–82]. Yang et al. have shown that circ_0000950 is associated with decreased expression of miR-103 and increased expression of Ptgs2 (prostaglandin-endoperoxide synthase 2) in AD patients. In cellular models, manipulation of circ_0000950 was associated with increased neuronal death, inhibition of neural processes, and increased expression of inflammatory cytokines, including IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α levels via directly sponging miR-103 [63].

A significant downregulation of circHOMER1 has been observed in patients with AD, and it shows a direct correlation with disease severity. Moreover, the reduced expression of circHOMER1, which normally acts as an inhibitor of miR-651, leads to decreased expression of PSEN1 and PSEN2 as key AD-related genes and predicted targets of this miRNA. This, in turn, may result in reduced activity of the γ-secretase complex, which is essential for amyloid beta processing [48, 48, 83, 84]. In comparison to circHOMER1, circCORO1C is a circular RNA with increased expression in AD patients, and its upregulation is directly associated with clinical traits and AD-related genes, including APP. By acting as a sponge for miR-105, circCORO1C increases APP and SNCA42 expression, which subsequently exacerbates amyloid plaque formation and synaptic dysfunction in AD subjects. In addition, circCORO1C may mitigate the pathological process of the disease by decreasing the accumulation of Aβ and SNCA in neurons, thereby contributing to symptom alleviation [48].

CircPSEN1 is another circular RNA with elevated expression levels in AD patients. It is involved in the regulation of genes associated with the TGF-β1 and Notch signaling pathways [85], which are key contributors to AD progression [86, 87]. Furthermore, dysregulation of circPSEN1 isoforms has been reported in autosomal dominant forms of AD [62].

In an AD mouse model, overexpression of circRIMS2 in the hippocampus reduces dendritic spine density and impairs memory [88]. It has been shown that amyloid beta enhances the stability of circRIMS2 and its ability to sponge miR-3968, leading to increased expression of UBE2K (ubiquitin-conjugating enzyme E2 K). Elevated UBE2K levels, in turn, promote increased ubiquitination and degradation of the GRIN2B protein by UBE2K, resulting in synaptic dysfunction [88–90]. In a recent study, Zhao et al. developed circMeta2, a computational tool specifically designed to conduct differential expression analysis focused on circRNA clusters. The authors further have demonstrated how circRNA clusters associated with AD function in a coordinated manner both within and across various brain regions, contributing to the etiology of AD by influencing miRNA-mediated gene expression. Additionally, they have identified significant circRNA clusters that correlate with the progression and severity of AD [91]. In another study, Wang et al. have reported circGigyf2 is notably conserved and exhibited the most significant net reduction specifically in the cortex of 7-month-old 5xFAD mice. The level of circGigyf2 in the cortices of AD patients was found to have a negative correlation with the severity of dementia. The authors have identified several splicing factors affected by AD that are crucial for the biogenesis of circGigyf2. They have discovered and experimentally confirmed the conserved roles of circGigyf2 in sponging miRNAs relevant to AD and RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) associated with AD, including the cleavage and polyadenylation factor 6 (CPSF6). Additionally, the downregulation of circGigyf2 in AD facilitated the silencing activities of its sponged miRNAs (miR-17-5p/93-5p) and improved the processing efficiency of polyadenylation sites for CPSF6 targets. Furthermore, the depletion of circGigyf2 in a mouse neuronal cell line led to the dysregulation of the circGigyf2-miRNA and circGigyf2-CPSF6 pathways, which intensified apoptotic responses to various insults, thereby providing strong evidence for the causative roles of circGigyf2 deficiency in neurodegeneration associated with AD [92]. Significantly, circGigyf2 is widely conserved between mouse and human, and its underexpression in the AD mouse cortex is linked with its decrease in human AD brains, indicating the clinical importance of this approach for studying circRNA failure in neurodegeneration [92].

In another study, Zhang et al. have clarified the patterns of the circRNA-ceRNA network in the hippocampus of APP/PS1 and WT mice through deep RNA-seq analysis. Their results further enhance the existing understanding of the biology of circRNA-ceRNA, including the signaling pathways they are involved in, such as the dysregulated circRNAs in nucleotide metabolism, the cGMP-PKG signaling pathway, the cAMP signaling pathway, platelet activation, and the Hippo signaling pathway, along with their regulatory roles in the pathogenesis of Alzheimer’s disease (AD). Furthermore, their findings preliminarily confirmed that the novel_circ_0003012/mmu-miR-298-3p/Smoc2 signaling axis might influence the cGMP-PKG signaling pathway. The cGMP-PKG signaling pathway plays a crucial role in preventing cell death and promoting the survival of neural cells. In mice with AD, researchers found that the levels of PKG in the hippocampus were significantly lower, indicating that the cGMP-PKG pathway could be essential in the disease’s underlying processes [93]. In addition, mmu_circ_0001125 and mmu_circ_0000672 have been identified as competing endogenous RNAs (ceRNAs) for mmu-miR-351-5p, which specifically targets Cst7. Cst7, which is also referred to as cystatin F, plays a crucial role by encoding a cathepsin inhibitor. This inhibitor works within the endosomal and lysosomal compartments, helping to regulate the activity of cathepsins in the lysosomal pathway [93]. The expression of Cst7 plays a crucial role in microglia, as it helps to diminish the phagocytic capacity of activated microglia. The amount of CST7 is higher in microglia from APP/PS1 mice and in the brains of people with Alzheimer’s disease. This increase in Cst7 expression may inhibit the clearance of Aβ species via microglia and macrophages [94] (Fig. 1).

Recent findings suggest that dysregulated eRNAs may play a role in Alzheimer’s disease (AD) by affecting synaptic plasticity, neuroinflammation, and amyloid beta mechanisms. eRNA research could uncover novel molecular mechanisms and therapeutic targets for AD [95] (Table 1). Transcripted eRNAs from two enhancers named Bdnf-Enh g1 and Bdnf-Enh g2 interact with brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) in the intergenic region. After BDNF was turned on, the expression of these eRNAs increased, especially in neurons that had completed their mitotic phase. In addition, they modulate the expression of BDNF in relation to the formation of dendrites [96]. BDNF is known as the gene responsible for the production of a neurotrophic factor. BDNF protein plays various significant roles in the development of the brain, including promoting the survival and differentiation of neurons as well as facilitating long-term potentiation and synaptic plasticity in the mature brain [97, 98]. The increased expression of BDNF has been observed to have neuroprotective effects in response to several stimuli, including environmental enrichment [99, 100] and the administration of antidepressant medications [97, 101]. The dysregulation of BDNF signal transduction has been shown in the brains of individuals with AD. However, the overexpression of BDNF has been shown to enhance learning and memory abilities. Aberrant expression of this protein is believed to elevate the risk of neurodegenerative illnesses [96, 102–104].

Watts et al. have identified a noncoding enhancer RNA associated with apolipoprotein E (APOE), which was designated as APOE-activating noncoding RNA, or AANCR. The pausing of RNA Polymerase II dynamically regulates the expression and functionality of this RNA. When this noncoding enhancer RNA is in its full-length form, it promotes the expression of APOE. Conversely, if this RNA is only partially transcribed, it becomes nonfunctional, resulting in the absence of APOE expression. In certain cells, the noncoding AANCR RNA does not undergo complete transcription due to the pausing of RNA Polymerase II elongation, which is caused by R-loops that are stabilized by RNA A basic sites. Upon experiencing hypertonic stress, the R-loops that inhibit transcription are resolved, allowing AANCR RNA to be transcribed into a full-length enhancer that activates APOE expression. Through genetic analysis, it was demonstrated that sequence variants within the enhancer region influence APOE expression across various cell types, including those in the hippocampus, and are linked to Alzheimer’s disease [105]. The discovery of AANCR provides a mechanistic explanation for how non-coding genetic variants influence Alzheimer’s disease risk, independent of the APOE ε4 allele. The discovery highlights enhancer RNAs as critical functional elements in the genome, where sequence changes can modulate the expression of key disease genes such as APOE, thereby opening new avenues for therapeutic interventions that target regulatory elements rather than the protein-coding gene itself.

Moreover, the sequence variants located in the AANCR region elucidate the individual differences in APOE expression and the levels of APOE in plasma. The variants present in the AANCR region that influence APOE levels also increase the risk of developing Alzheimer’s disease. Considering the degree of linkage disequilibrium between the variants in AANCR and APOE, the impact of polymorphisms in AANCR operates independently of the risk associated with the APOE ϵ4 allele. Through the regulation of APOE expression, AANCR functions as a modifier of Alzheimer’s disease [105]. Furthermore, another study examining expression profiles revealed that the interaction between DSCAML1 enhancers and their target BACE1 promoter results in the excessive production of Aβ peptides, which ultimately contribute to plaque formation. In turn, Aβ peptides promote the hypomethylation of enhancers that influence neurogenesis and cell cycle genes, which are already prepared for activation due to the typical loss of CpH (non-CpG sites) methylation marks associated with aging. The reactivation of cell cycle genes promotes tau hyperphosphorylation, and together with Aβ plaques, this leads to the development and dissemination of tangle pathology, culminating in neuronal death and cognitive symptoms associated with Alzheimer’s disease [95] (Fig. 5).

In a recent investigation, the existence of circRNAs in the cerebrospinal fluid of individuals diagnosed with AD was assessed. The study revealed the up-regulation of 112 circRNAs and the down-regulation of 51 circRNAs [56]. In people with AD, circ-LPAR1, circ-AXL, and circGPHN were upregulated, while circ-PCCA, circ-HAUS4, circKIF18B, and circTTC39C were downregulated [56]. Also, circRNA expression in PBMCs can be utilized to discriminate between individuals with AD and non-AD individuals [55]. Researchers found that people with AD exhibited higher levels of hsa_circRNA_405619 and hsa_circRNA_000843 in their PBMCs than those without the condition. On the other hand, the PBMCs of the same AD patients showed lower levels of hsa_circRNA_100861 and hsa_circRNA_102448 [55].

Moreover, circRNAs demonstrate significant therapeutic potential, especially during the preclinical phases of Alzheimer’s disease (AD). Importantly, circRNAs may be regarded as some of the earliest viable pharmacological targets in the context of AD. Several studies have already shown that utilizing specific miRNAs as drug targets can help alleviate or treat AD in mouse models. Consequently, circRNAs are seen as promising candidates in the realm of AD treatment. Due to their direct influence on protein expression, synthetic circRNAs could be engineered to inhibit the production of proteins associated with AD, employing a therapeutic strategy similar to that used with miRNAs. Additionally, circRNAs, functioning as miRNA sponges, represent another fascinating category of molecules that warrant investigation for AD therapy. Their ability to modify the expression of miRNAs that suppress target mRNAs presents exciting opportunities for novel therapeutic approaches in Alzheimer’s disease [74, 106]. For example, this upregulation is associated with cognitive decline in these models, suggesting a potential regulatory Knockdown of circCwc27 affects the nucleocytoplasmic translocation of Pur-α and is associated with reduced APP expression and Aβ production [60]. Therefore, circCwc27 appears to exert a direct regulatory influence on pathological proteins linked to AD and may represent a viable therapeutic target for AD [107]. The circular RNA associated with histone deacetylase (circHDAC9), which is expressed in the mouse hippocampus, contains multiple binding sites for miR-138. By acting as a sponge for this microRNA, circHDAC9 increases the expression of the target gene Sirtuin 1 (Sirt1), leading to elevated levels of Aβ and triggering neuronal dysfunction, characterized by synaptic deficits and impairments in cognitive functions such as learning and memory [59]. Studies have shown a decreased expression level of circHDAC9 in the serum of AD patients and individuals with mild cognitive impairment [59]. Several differentially expressed circRNAs involved in the Hippo, cGMP-PKG, and cAMP signaling pathways have been identified in the hippocampus of the AD model APP/PS1 mice. These circRNAs influence processes such as axon guidance and platelet activation in neuronal synaptic systems [93, 108] (Table 1 and Supplementary Table S1).

The interplay between transcriptional and post-transcriptional regulation is particularly evident in Alzheimer’s disease. While the AANCR eRNA drives APOE expression at the transcriptional level [105], multiple circRNAs finely regulate amyloidogenic processing post-transcriptionally. This multilayered regulation creates both vulnerability and opportunity: dysregulation at either level can initiate pathological cascades, but coordinated targeting of eRNAs and circRNAs may offer synergistic therapeutic benefits. For example, simultaneous modulation of AANCR to reduce APOE expression and targeting of circCwc27 to enhance Aβ clearance could potentially provide more comprehensive disease modification than either approach alone.

Circular RNAs exhibit an accumulation phenomenon in the brains of older individuals. However, it is important to note that the changes observed in circRNAs due to aging are distinct from those linked to the progression of AD [48, 48–53]. They also exhibit abnormal expression patterns in the brain, cerebrospinal fluid, and serum of individuals affected by AD. It has been identified that a large number of circRNAs are involved in Aβ clearance, neurotrophic signaling, neuroinflammation, and oxidative stress pathways [54–56]. The excessive deposition of Aβ in the cerebral cortex expedites the progression of AD [57]. The process of cleavage by β-secretase and γ-secretase in the APP (amyloid precursor protein) produces Aβ protein, which is primarily eliminated through autophagy in brain cells [54–56]. We summarize the circRNAs that can be effective in AD in Table 1, Supplementary Table S1, and Fig. 1. GO enrichment analysis suggested that a significant number of circRNAs differentially expressed in AD are associated with pathways involved in Aβ turnover. For example, ciRS-7 represents a candidate circRNA that shows high expression levels in healthy human brain but is downregulated in brains of individuals with AD, potentially implicating it in Aβ-related pathways [72]. In a study conducted by Shi et al., it was found that expression of ciRS-7 inhibited the translation of NF-κB and promoted its localization in the cytoplasm, thereby allowing for the derepression of UCHL1 expression, which facilitates the degradation of APP and BACE1. Furthermore, APP decreased the levels of ciRS-7, indicating a reciprocal regulatory relationship between ciRS-7 and APP. These findings elucidated a molecular mechanism that associates reduced ciRS-7 expression with AD, implying that ciRS-7 could serve as a valuable target in the formulation of therapeutic approaches for AD and suggesting a possible neuroprotective function of ciRS-7 [73]. Zhao et al. reported that a deficiency in the sponging activity of ciRS-7 leads to an excess of ambient miRNA-7, which appears to drive the selective down-regulation of miRNA-7-sensitive mRNA targets, including the one that encodes the ubiquitin conjugating enzyme E2A (UBE2A). UBE2A typically functions as a key effector in the ubiquitin-26S proteasome system, facilitating the clearance of amyloid peptides through proteolysis. ciRS-7 is reduced in the brains of individuals with sporadic Alzheimer’s disease (AD), thereby contributing to the accumulation of amyloid and the development of senile plaque deposits [74]. Similarly, CircCwc27, a newly identified circRNA derived from exons, is highly conserved and expressed in abundance within neurons. It diminished the recruitment of Pur-α (purine-rich element-binding protein A or PURA) to the promoters of genes associated with Alzheimer’s disease (AD) by binding to Pur-α. This interaction led to a reduction in Aβ load, helped prevent cognitive decline in AD, and resulted in the downregulation of several genes linked to AD, including APP, Mme, dopamine receptor D1 (Drd1), protein phosphatase 1 regulatory inhibitor subunit 1B (Ppp1r1b), neurotrophic tyrosine kinase receptor type 1 (Ntrk1), and LIM homeobox 8 (Lhx8) [60]. Furthermore, it is worth noting that there is another circRNA, circAβ-a, that acted as a template for the biosynthesis of an Aβ-related protein (Aβ175) in HEK293 cells. Moreover, the distinctive C-terminal peptide of Aβ175 was detected in human brain samples. Additionally, Aβ175 underwent processing into Aβ peptides, indicating a novel pathway for Aβ generation that may provide new insights into the molecular mechanisms contributing to the development of Alzheimer’s disease. This shematic highlights some key circRNAs and how they function in the context of AD. For instance, ciRS-7 plays a role in regulating Aβ production and microglial activation through the miR-7/UBE2A pathway. Then there’s circ-AXL, which modulates neuroinflammation and apoptosis via miR-328/BACE1. Additionally, circHOMER1 and circCORO1C are known to influence γ-secretase activity and app expression. Key circRNAs and eRNAs in Alzheimer’s disease and their applications as diagnostic/prognostic biomarkers

The regulatory roles of circRNAs in Alzheimer’s disease. This shematic highlights some key circRNAs and how they function in the context of AD. For instance, ciRS-7 plays a role in regulating Aβ production and microglial activation through the miR-7/UBE2A pathway. Then there’s circ-AXL, which modulates neuroinflammation and apoptosis via miR-328/BACE1. Additionally, circHOMER1 and circCORO1C are known to influence γ-secretase activity and app expression. Other circRNAs, like circRIMS2, are also implicated in synaptic dysfunction, signaling pathways, and neuronal death. Together, these circRNAs form a crucial network that contributes to the pathogenesis of Alzheimer’s disease

A study conducted by Wu et al. [76] identified a novel circRNA named circAPP, which was found to be upregulated in Aβ-treated microglia and in the hippocampus of APP/PS1 mice. This research demonstrated the regulatory role of circAPP in microglial polarization in AD through the miR-1906/CLIC1 axis. Specifically, circAPP interacts with miR-1906, modulating its expression and consequently increasing the expression and activity of the ion channel CLIC1. In contrast, overexpression of miR-1906 was shown to modulate microglial polarization, improve AD pathology, and enhance cognitive function both in vivo and in vitro, while also reducing the expression and activity of CLIC1. Moreover, the study revealed that silencing circAPP led to reduced hippocampal levels of Aβ and phosphorylated tau (p-tau), improved hippocampal synaptic structure, and ameliorated cognitive deficits resulting from neuroinflammation caused by abnormal microglial polarization in APP/PS1 mice [76].

Neuronal loss occurs in AD because of neuroinflammation and oxidative stress. CircRNAs control these processes. The expression of circ_0002945 was shown to be increased in the serum of individuals with Alzheimer’s disease, as well as in Aβ-induced SK-N-SH cells and human primary neurons. The suppression of circ_0002945 reduced Aβ25–35-induced cell apoptosis and endoplasmic reticulum stress. Additionally, circ_0002945 was shown to regulate miR-431-5p. Furthermore, TNFAIP1 was identified as a target of miR-431-5p, and circ_0002945 acted as a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) to modulate the expression of TNFAIP1 through competition with miR-431-5p. Moreover, the suppression of TNFAIP1 mediated by miR-431-5p contributed to the alleviation of Aβ25–35-induced cell apoptosis and endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress [70]. ER stress is all about the accumulation of proteins that haven’t folded properly, and this can happen due to various physiological and pathological reasons. Factors like increased demand for proteins, exposure to toxins, inflammatory cytokines, and the presence of mutant proteins can all throw the endoplasmic reticulum out of balance [77]. Recent research has shown that this ER stress might actually play a significant role in both the onset and progression of AD [78].

In experimental models, overexpression of circ-AXL was associated with increased apoptosis rates and reduced neurite outgrowth, alongside an elevation of inflammatory cytokines in cellular models of Alzheimer’s disease. Furthermore, circ-AXL directly interacted with miR-328, negatively regulating it while positively modulating BACE1. Additionally, miR-328 was found to directly bind to BACE1, exerting a negative regulatory effect on it. Importantly, miR-328 knockdown diminished the impact of circ-AXL knockdown on cellular models of Alzheimer’s disease. Moreover, BACE1 overexpression exacerbated neuronal injury and inflammation, while also reducing the influence of miR-328 overexpression on these functions within cellular models of Alzheimer’s disease [79].

The activation of microglial cells by Aβ and NFTs leads to the continuous production of inflammation-related cytokines and chemokines, ultimately resulting in the death of neighboring neurons [80–82]. Yang et al. have shown that circ_0000950 is associated with decreased expression of miR-103 and increased expression of Ptgs2 (prostaglandin-endoperoxide synthase 2) in AD patients. In cellular models, manipulation of circ_0000950 was associated with increased neuronal death, inhibition of neural processes, and increased expression of inflammatory cytokines, including IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α levels via directly sponging miR-103 [63].

A significant downregulation of circHOMER1 has been observed in patients with AD, and it shows a direct correlation with disease severity. Moreover, the reduced expression of circHOMER1, which normally acts as an inhibitor of miR-651, leads to decreased expression of PSEN1 and PSEN2 as key AD-related genes and predicted targets of this miRNA. This, in turn, may result in reduced activity of the γ-secretase complex, which is essential for amyloid beta processing [48, 48, 83, 84]. In comparison to circHOMER1, circCORO1C is a circular RNA with increased expression in AD patients, and its upregulation is directly associated with clinical traits and AD-related genes, including APP. By acting as a sponge for miR-105, circCORO1C increases APP and SNCA42 expression, which subsequently exacerbates amyloid plaque formation and synaptic dysfunction in AD subjects. In addition, circCORO1C may mitigate the pathological process of the disease by decreasing the accumulation of Aβ and SNCA in neurons, thereby contributing to symptom alleviation [48].

CircPSEN1 is another circular RNA with elevated expression levels in AD patients. It is involved in the regulation of genes associated with the TGF-β1 and Notch signaling pathways [85], which are key contributors to AD progression [86, 87]. Furthermore, dysregulation of circPSEN1 isoforms has been reported in autosomal dominant forms of AD [62].

In an AD mouse model, overexpression of circRIMS2 in the hippocampus reduces dendritic spine density and impairs memory [88]. It has been shown that amyloid beta enhances the stability of circRIMS2 and its ability to sponge miR-3968, leading to increased expression of UBE2K (ubiquitin-conjugating enzyme E2 K). Elevated UBE2K levels, in turn, promote increased ubiquitination and degradation of the GRIN2B protein by UBE2K, resulting in synaptic dysfunction [88–90]. In a recent study, Zhao et al. developed circMeta2, a computational tool specifically designed to conduct differential expression analysis focused on circRNA clusters. The authors further have demonstrated how circRNA clusters associated with AD function in a coordinated manner both within and across various brain regions, contributing to the etiology of AD by influencing miRNA-mediated gene expression. Additionally, they have identified significant circRNA clusters that correlate with the progression and severity of AD [91]. In another study, Wang et al. have reported circGigyf2 is notably conserved and exhibited the most significant net reduction specifically in the cortex of 7-month-old 5xFAD mice. The level of circGigyf2 in the cortices of AD patients was found to have a negative correlation with the severity of dementia. The authors have identified several splicing factors affected by AD that are crucial for the biogenesis of circGigyf2. They have discovered and experimentally confirmed the conserved roles of circGigyf2 in sponging miRNAs relevant to AD and RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) associated with AD, including the cleavage and polyadenylation factor 6 (CPSF6). Additionally, the downregulation of circGigyf2 in AD facilitated the silencing activities of its sponged miRNAs (miR-17-5p/93-5p) and improved the processing efficiency of polyadenylation sites for CPSF6 targets. Furthermore, the depletion of circGigyf2 in a mouse neuronal cell line led to the dysregulation of the circGigyf2-miRNA and circGigyf2-CPSF6 pathways, which intensified apoptotic responses to various insults, thereby providing strong evidence for the causative roles of circGigyf2 deficiency in neurodegeneration associated with AD [92]. Significantly, circGigyf2 is widely conserved between mouse and human, and its underexpression in the AD mouse cortex is linked with its decrease in human AD brains, indicating the clinical importance of this approach for studying circRNA failure in neurodegeneration [92].

In another study, Zhang et al. have clarified the patterns of the circRNA-ceRNA network in the hippocampus of APP/PS1 and WT mice through deep RNA-seq analysis. Their results further enhance the existing understanding of the biology of circRNA-ceRNA, including the signaling pathways they are involved in, such as the dysregulated circRNAs in nucleotide metabolism, the cGMP-PKG signaling pathway, the cAMP signaling pathway, platelet activation, and the Hippo signaling pathway, along with their regulatory roles in the pathogenesis of Alzheimer’s disease (AD). Furthermore, their findings preliminarily confirmed that the novel_circ_0003012/mmu-miR-298-3p/Smoc2 signaling axis might influence the cGMP-PKG signaling pathway. The cGMP-PKG signaling pathway plays a crucial role in preventing cell death and promoting the survival of neural cells. In mice with AD, researchers found that the levels of PKG in the hippocampus were significantly lower, indicating that the cGMP-PKG pathway could be essential in the disease’s underlying processes [93]. In addition, mmu_circ_0001125 and mmu_circ_0000672 have been identified as competing endogenous RNAs (ceRNAs) for mmu-miR-351-5p, which specifically targets Cst7. Cst7, which is also referred to as cystatin F, plays a crucial role by encoding a cathepsin inhibitor. This inhibitor works within the endosomal and lysosomal compartments, helping to regulate the activity of cathepsins in the lysosomal pathway [93]. The expression of Cst7 plays a crucial role in microglia, as it helps to diminish the phagocytic capacity of activated microglia. The amount of CST7 is higher in microglia from APP/PS1 mice and in the brains of people with Alzheimer’s disease. This increase in Cst7 expression may inhibit the clearance of Aβ species via microglia and macrophages [94] (Fig. 1).

After BDNF was turned on, the expression of these eRNAs increased, especially in neurons that had completed their mitotic phase. In addition, they modulate the expression of BDNF in relation to the formation of dendrites [96]. BDNF is known as the gene responsible for the production of a neurotrophic factor. BDNF protein plays various significant roles in the development of the brain, including promoting the survival and differentiation of neurons as well as facilitating long-term potentiation and synaptic plasticity in the mature brain [97, 98]. The increased expression of BDNF has been observed to have neuroprotective effects in response to several stimuli, including environmental enrichment [99, 100] and the administration of antidepressant medications [97, 101]. The dysregulation of BDNF signal transduction has been shown in the brains of individuals with AD. However, the overexpression of BDNF has been shown to enhance learning and memory abilities. Aberrant expression of this protein is believed to elevate the risk of neurodegenerative illnesses [96, 102–104].

chunks.section: Clinical implications of CircRNAs and eRNAS in AD
chunks.text: In a recent investigation, the existence of circRNAs in the cerebrospinal fluid of individuals diagnosed with AD was assessed. The study revealed the up-regulation of 112 circRNAs and the down-regulation of 51 circRNAs [56]. In people with AD, circ-LPAR1, circ-AXL, and circGPHN were upregulated, while circ-PCCA, circ-HAUS4, circKIF18B, and circTTC39C were downregulated [56]. Also, circRNA expression in PBMCs can be utilized to discriminate between individuals with AD and non-AD individuals [55]. Researchers found that people with AD exhibited higher levels of hsa_circRNA_405619 and hsa_circRNA_000843 in their PBMCs than those without the condition. On the other hand, the PBMCs of the same AD patients showed lower levels of hsa_circRNA_100861 and hsa_circRNA_102448 [55].

chunks.section: Clinical implications of CircRNAs and eRNAS in AD
chunks.text: Moreover, circRNAs demonstrate significant therapeutic potential, especially during the preclinical phases of Alzheimer’s disease (AD). Importantly, circRNAs may be regarded as some of the earliest viable pharmacological targets in the context of AD. Several studies have already shown that utilizing specific miRNAs as drug targets can help alleviate or treat AD in mouse models. Consequently, circRNAs are seen as promising candidates in the realm of AD treatment. Due to their direct influence on protein expression, synthetic circRNAs could be engineered to inhibit the production of proteins associated with AD, employing a therapeutic strategy similar to that used with miRNAs. Additionally, circRNAs, functioning as miRNA sponges, represent another fascinating category of molecules that warrant investigation for AD therapy. Their ability to modify the expression of miRNAs that suppress target mRNAs presents exciting opportunities for novel therapeutic approaches in Alzheimer’s disease [74, 106]. For example, this upregulation is associated with cognitive decline in these models, suggesting a potential regulatory Knockdown of circCwc27 affects the nucleocytoplasmic translocation of Pur-α and is associated with reduced APP expression and Aβ production [60]. Therefore, circCwc27 appears to exert a direct regulatory influence on pathological proteins linked to AD and may represent a viable therapeutic target for AD [107]. The circular RNA associated with histone deacetylase (circHDAC9), which is expressed in the mouse hippocampus, contains multiple binding sites for miR-138. By acting as a sponge for this microRNA, circHDAC9 increases the expression of the target gene Sirtuin 1 (Sirt1), leading to elevated levels of Aβ and triggering neuronal dysfunction, characterized by synaptic deficits and impairments in cognitive functions such as learning and memory [59]. Studies have shown a decreased expression level of circHDAC9 in the serum of AD patients and individuals with mild cognitive impairment [59]. Several differentially expressed circRNAs involved in the Hippo, cGMP-PKG, and cAMP signaling pathways have been identified in the hippocampus of the AD model APP/PS1 mice. These circRNAs influence processes such as axon guidance and platelet activation in neuronal synaptic systems [93, 108] (Table 1 and Supplementary Table S1).

chunks.section: Clinical implications of CircRNAs and eRNAS in AD
chunks.text: The interplay between transcriptional and post-transcriptional regulation is particularly evident in Alzheimer’s disease. While the AANCR eRNA drives APOE expression at the transcriptional level [105], multiple circRNAs finely regulate amyloidogenic processing post-transcriptionally. This multilayered regulation creates both vulnerability and opportunity: dysregulation at either level can initiate pathological cascades, but coordinated targeting of eRNAs and circRNAs may offer synergistic therapeutic benefits. For example, simultaneous modulation of AANCR to reduce APOE expression and targeting of circCwc27 to enhance Aβ clearance could potentially provide more comprehensive disease modification than either approach alone.

Parkinson’s disease (PD) is the second most prevalent neurodegenerative disorder among adults in the middle-aged and elderly population [109]. PD is observed in a sporadic manner in around 90 to 95% of cases, whereas the remaining instances are familial and linked to certain autosomal genes such as SNCA [110]. PD is additionally associated with mitochondrial dysfunction, increased oxidative stress, impaired apoptosis, and disorders in the ubiquitin-proteasome system [111].

Recent research has demonstrated that circRNAs play the key roles in the development of PD. Table 2 and Supplementary Table S2 has provided a detailed summary of the key circRNAs dysregulated across PD. To find the circRNAs in the substantia nigra (SN), middle temporal gyrus (MTG), and amygdala, Hanan et al. used RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) to compare samples from PD sufferers and healthy people. Based on available evidence, circSLC8A1 exhibits higher expression levels in the SN of individuals affected by Parkinson’s disease. Furthermore, subjection of SH-SY5Y brain cells to an excessive amount of paraquat (PQ) as an indicator of oxidative stress overexpressed circSLC8A in a dose-dependent manner. Administering the neuroprotective drug simvastatin along with the LRRK2 inhibitor PF-06447475 reversed the effect. Moreover, circSLC8A1 has seven binding sites for miR-128 and regulates the levels and/or activity of it. Additionally, miR-128 target genes, including SIRT1, BMI1, and AXIN1, were upregulated in the brains of the Parkinson’s patients and in cultured neurons treated with PQ [112]. One of the miR-128 targets is SIRT1, which has a role in the aging process and exhibits anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective benefits in neuroinflammatory illnesses such as PD [120]. Another target of miR-128 is BMI1. Neuronal BMI1 is a negative regulator of α-synuclein. In Parkinson’s disease, misfolded and aggregated α-synuclein gives rise to Lewy bodies, which are characteristic of the condition. Studies indicate that BMI1 might play a role in regulating α-synuclein levels, with certain findings suggesting that a deficiency in BMI1 could result in heightened aggregation of α-synuclein [121]. Finally, Axin1 exhibited increased expression in the hippocampal tissues and cells of the MPTP-lesioned mouse model of Parkinson’s disease (PD), whereas Hif1a and miR-128-3p showed decreased expression. The rise in HIF-1α/miR-128-3p levels led to the inhibition of apoptosis in hippocampal neurons through the activation of the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway, which was attributed to the suppression of Axin1 in the context of PD [122]. Similarly, circSLC8A1 exhibits binding affinity towards miR-132. Key circRNAs and eRNAs in Parkinson’s disease and their applications as diagnostic/prognostic biomarkers

The SNCA gene, in addition to producing SNCA mRNA, can also generate circular RNAs. One such circRNA, known as circSNCA, originates from the proximal 3′ untranslated region (3′UTR) of SNCA mRNA, which contains a high-affinity binding site for miR-7. Also, when SH-SH5Y cells were subjected to pramipexole, as a dopamine agonist, the circSNCA levels were reduced, resulting in elevated miR-7 and decreased SNCA protein [115]. Elevated levels of intracellular α-synuclein, which is primarily encoded by the SNCA gene in the nucleus, have been associated with the etiology of PD and play a significant role in neurodegenerative disorders [124, 125]. Moreover, there is a correlation between the expression of this gene and the levels of miR-7 in neurons [126]. Additionally, it has been observed that miR-7 expression is decreased in the SN of individuals with PD. The reduction in miR-7 levels is also associated with the loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra and a consequent decrease in striatal dopamine content [127].

Similar to circSNCA, circPANK1 upregulation has been reported in mouse and cellular models of Parkinson’s disease. This circRNA also acts specifically as a sponge for miR-7. Therefore, its increased expression reduces the availability of miR-7, leading to elevated expression of alpha-synuclein (SNCA) protein and, ultimately, enhanced neurodegeneration of dopaminergic neurons [128].

Hsa_circ_0070441 is a circRNA molecule that is derived from the coding area of the SNCA gene, and its expression is elevated in SHSY5Y cells under the influence of 1-methyl-4-phenylpyridinium ions (MPP+), a neural metabolite that leads to impaired cellular respiration and dopaminergic neuron death [116]. Moreover, Hsa_circ_0070441 acts as a sponge for a microRNA named miR-626, and its knockdown has led to a significant reduction in inflammation and apoptosis following MPP+ exposure in these cells. Furthermore, it has been observed that miR-626 modulates the expression of insulin receptor substrate 2. The miR-626/insulin receptor substrate 2 axis has been proposed as a potential diagnostic biomarker for Parkinson’s disease [116]. The circular RNA known as circzip-2, derived from the zip-2 gene (Zinc-regulated transporters and iron-regulated transporter-like protein-2), shows significantly reduced expression in the PD model of C. elegans. It has also been demonstrated that circzip-2 established a competitive inhibitory interaction at the cleavage site with its parent gene, zip-2. The protein encoded by zip-2 directly affects the expression level of the SNCA gene and reactive oxygen species (ROS) concentrations [111]. Moreover, circzip-2 can bind to miR-60, thereby inhibiting the progression of PD. This neuroprotective property of circzip-2 may make it a promising candidate for the development of targeted therapeutics and precise diagnostic tools for neurodegenerative diseases such as PD [111]. Despite C. elegans missing direct orthologs of several human Parkinson’s disorder genes, the common miRNA pathways (like the miR-60 family) and stress response processes make it a model for showing important principles of circRNA-based neuroprotection that may increase investigation in higher organisms.

The circular RNA derived from the SAMD4A gene, known as circSAMD4A, plays an active role in promoting both apoptosis and autophagy. Moreover, it has been shown that circSAMD4A contributes to dopaminergic neuronal apoptosis and autophagy via the AMPK/mTOR signaling pathway in human neuroblastoma cells (SH-SY5Y) treated with the neurotoxins MPTP and MPP+ [114, 129]. Additionally, the binding of microRNA-29c-3p to circSAMD4A was associated with reduced neurotoxin-induced cytotoxicity in neuronal cells. Therefore, circSAMD4A may serve as a valuable biomarker and a potential therapeutic target for PD [107].

The circular RNA associated with DLG protein (circDLGAP4), which has been reported to be downregulated in MPTP-induced mouse models of PD and in PD cellular models treated with MPP+, may contribute to the onset and progression of PD by affecting the viability of neuronal cells (SH-SY5Y and MN9D), promoting apoptosis, impairing mitochondrial function, and enhancing autophagy [113].

The circular RNA circTLK1, which is derived from the TLK1 (Tousled-like kinase 1) gene, is a highly upregulated circRNA in MPTP-induced PD mouse models, and its knockdown in these models is associated with reduced apoptosis and cellular toxicity, along with increased cell viability. Moreover, by sequestering miR-26a-5p, circTLK1 regulates the expression of its target gene DAPK1 (Death-associated protein kinase 1), which is involved in apoptotic processes. Through this mechanism, circTLK1 contributes to the improvement of neurological deficits caused by middle cerebral artery occlusion and reperfusion in in vivo models [117]. Therefore, the circTLK1/miR-26a-5p/DAPK1 regulatory axis is considered a prominent mechanism in PD pathogenesis and may provide a foundation for the development of innovative and targeted therapeutic approaches for this disease [107].

In a study involving 300 PD cases and 100 healthy controls, it was found that the circRNAs circARID1B and circTCONS_l2_00002816 exhibit high sensitivity for the early diagnosis of PD. Whereas, circFAM83H, circHUWE1, circARID1B, and circTCONS_l2_00002816 were identified as potential biomarkers capable of distinguishing between early and advanced stages of PD [130]. CircEps15 has also been identified in both human and animal studies [131]. Its reduced expression in the plasma of PD patients is associated with disease progression. circEps15 functions by sponging miR-24, thereby maintaining PINK1 gene expression and promoting the PINK1-PRKN–dependent mitophagy pathway, which is essential for the clearance of damaged mitochondria and maintaining mitochondrial homeostasis in neurons [131]. Top circRNAs in PD development is indicated in Fig. 2.Fig. 2Illustration depicting the roles of circRNA in Parkinson’s disease. It highlights how circRNAs by sponging various miRNAs and interacting with and α–synuclein for regulating PD

Illustration depicting the roles of circRNA in Parkinson’s disease. It highlights how circRNAs by sponging various miRNAs and interacting with and α–synuclein for regulating PD

Dysregulation of eRNAs in PD can disrupt dopaminergic neuron function and be a cause of neurodegeneration. The study of eRNAs may reveal new insights into the mechanisms of PD and its therapeutic targets (Table 2). The production of an eRNA from intron 2 of the KANSL1 gene has been linked to Polymorphisms on chromosome 17q21 in dopamine neurons that are associated with PD. The dysregulation of KANSL1 hinders the process of autophagy, leading to the promotion of memory impairment and neurodegeneration [132, 133]. Studies have shown that the intronic enhancers of the SNCA gene form clusters of enhancers in the human brain, where single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) highly associated with PD risk are enriched. These enhancer cluster regions have the potential to liberate RNA polymerase II from the promoter region of the SNCA gene, thereby exerting regulatory control over its expression [134]. One proposed idea posits that the eRNAs generated from these enhancers are capable of promoting transcription elongation by dissociating the negative elongation factor (NELF) complex. On the other hand, deletion of the intronic enhancer clusters of the SNCA gene has been shown to significantly downregulate SNCA expression in the brain by inhibiting its transcription elongation [135]. This finding highlights the potential of targeting these enhancer regions as a novel therapeutic strategy for treating neurodegenerative disorders associated with SNCA accumulation in the brain (Fig. 5).

Zhong et al. [130] investigated the prognostic and diagnostic applicability of circRNAs during the early and progressive stages of PD. The analysis conducted on the plasmatic circRNAs of three individuals diagnosed with PD, compared to a control group, used microarray technology. The results of this analysis revealed that two specific circRNAs, namely circ_0004381 and circ_0017204 (referred to as circARID1B and circTCONS-l200002816, respectively), exhibited the potential to accurately predict PD at an early stage of diagnosis. This predictive ability was achieved by employing risk score formulas and conducting receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve analysis, which demonstrated relatively high sensitivity and specificity. In addition, the circRNA molecules circ_0085869, circ_0004381, circ_0017204, and circ_0090668, which are respectively referred to as circFAM83H, circARID1B, circTCONS-l2-00002816, and circHUWE1, have potential discriminatory capabilities between late-stage and early-stage PD.

In conclusion, these findings suggest that a cell-free circRNA panel holds promise as a non-invasive biomarker for the early detection or continuous monitoring of PD progression [130]. Ravanidis et al. conducted a study to further explore the potential of circRNAs as biomarkers in a larger group of individuals with PD. The outcome of this study identified six circRNAs (MAPK9_circ_0001566, HOMER1_circ_0006916, SLAIN1_circ_0000497, DOP1B_circ_0001187, RESP1_circ_0004368, and PSEN1_circ_0003848) that are enriched in the brain and are significantly downregulated in PBMCs of PD subjects compared to healthy ones [136]. A different study suggested that circzip-2 can bind with miR-60, consequently hindering the advancement of PD. This neuroprotective characteristic of circzip-2 could position it as a potential candidate for creating targeted therapies and accurate diagnostic instruments for neurodegenerative disorders like PD [111] (Table 2 and Supplementary Table S2).

The production of an eRNA from intron 2 of the KANSL1 gene has been linked to Polymorphisms on chromosome 17q21 in dopamine neurons that are associated with PD. The dysregulation of KANSL1 hinders the process of autophagy, leading to the promotion of memory impairment and neurodegeneration [132, 133]. Studies have shown that the intronic enhancers of the SNCA gene form clusters of enhancers in the human brain, where single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) highly associated with PD risk are enriched. These enhancer cluster regions have the potential to liberate RNA polymerase II from the promoter region of the SNCA gene, thereby exerting regulatory control over its expression [134]. One proposed idea posits that the eRNAs generated from these enhancers are capable of promoting transcription elongation by dissociating the negative elongation factor (NELF) complex. On the other hand, deletion of the intronic enhancer clusters of the SNCA gene has been shown to significantly downregulate SNCA expression in the brain by inhibiting its transcription elongation [135]. This finding highlights the potential of targeting these enhancer regions as a novel therapeutic strategy for treating neurodegenerative disorders associated with SNCA accumulation in the brain (Fig. 5).

chunks.section: Clinical implications circRNAs and eRNAs in PD
chunks.text: Zhong et al. [130] investigated the prognostic and diagnostic applicability of circRNAs during the early and progressive stages of PD. The analysis conducted on the plasmatic circRNAs of three individuals diagnosed with PD, compared to a control group, used microarray technology. The results of this analysis revealed that two specific circRNAs, namely circ_0004381 and circ_0017204 (referred to as circARID1B and circTCONS-l200002816, respectively), exhibited the potential to accurately predict PD at an early stage of diagnosis. This predictive ability was achieved by employing risk score formulas and conducting receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve analysis, which demonstrated relatively high sensitivity and specificity. In addition, the circRNA molecules circ_0085869, circ_0004381, circ_0017204, and circ_0090668, which are respectively referred to as circFAM83H, circARID1B, circTCONS-l2-00002816, and circHUWE1, have potential discriminatory capabilities between late-stage and early-stage PD.

chunks.section: Clinical implications circRNAs and eRNAs in PD
chunks.text: In conclusion, these findings suggest that a cell-free circRNA panel holds promise as a non-invasive biomarker for the early detection or continuous monitoring of PD progression [130]. Ravanidis et al. conducted a study to further explore the potential of circRNAs as biomarkers in a larger group of individuals with PD. The outcome of this study identified six circRNAs (MAPK9_circ_0001566, HOMER1_circ_0006916, SLAIN1_circ_0000497, DOP1B_circ_0001187, RESP1_circ_0004368, and PSEN1_circ_0003848) that are enriched in the brain and are significantly downregulated in PBMCs of PD subjects compared to healthy ones [136]. A different study suggested that circzip-2 can bind with miR-60, consequently hindering the advancement of PD. This neuroprotective characteristic of circzip-2 could position it as a potential candidate for creating targeted therapies and accurate diagnostic instruments for neurodegenerative disorders like PD [111] (Table 2 and Supplementary Table S2).

Huntington’s Disease (HD) is an atypical autosomal dominant neurodegenerative condition characterized by the progressive loss of cortical and striatal neurons. Individuals with Huntington’s disease (HD) have expressed motor skills, memory function, and overall cognitive abilities [137]. HD is the most prevalent trinucleotide repeat disorder. The expansion of CAG repeats within exon 1 of the Huntingtin (HTT) gene has been identified as the underlying etiology of HD [138].

As a result of CAG expansion within the first exon, a polyglutamine mutant HTT (mHTT) protein is produced, which is prone to misfolding and aggregation and has a higher tendency to interact with proteins in ways that aren’t normal. The role of HTT encompasses various cellular processes, including axonal trafficking, gene regulation, disruption of the ubiquitin-proteasome system, and autophagy [139]. Dysregulation of autophagy has been implicated in the early cognitive changes observed in Huntington’s disease. Therefore, it undergoes alterations and qualifies as a potential candidate for Huntington’s disease treatment [140].

circRNAs have been shown that differentially expressed in HD. Table 3 summarizes the expression patterns of key circRNAs in HD. In addition, Several circRNAs have been shown to play a role in regulating autophagy in HD; examples include circLRP1B, circDLGAP4, and circHIPK2, which inhibit miR-27a-3p, miR-134-5p, and miR-124, respectively. Emerging evidence suggests that circRNAs may influence the pathogenesis of HD through potential modulation of the autophagy process [144]. Nonetheless, to validate this hypothesis, a rigorous investigation of the role of autophagy-related circRNAs in the progression of HD is necessary. In addition, when circ-016719 is overexpressed in neurons, it acts as a sponge for miR-29c, which starts the process of autophagy. Circ-016719 stimulates the expression of MAP2K6 and BECN1 and, as a consequence, increases the rate of autophagy within the neuronal cells. The function of circ-016719 was confirmed using knockdown experiments, which effectively inhibited the process of autophagy [145].Table 3Key circRNAs and eRNAs in Huntington’s disease and their applications as diagnostic/prognostic biomarkersncRNAExpression ChangeCell line studyAnimal studyClinical sampleTarget/PathwayFunctionClinical PotentialReferencescircHTT(2–6) (exons 2–6)UpSH-SY5Y, HEK293-7 HD patients, 4 controls (frontal cortex tissue)HTTBy modulating HTT nuclear localization and influencing nuclear morphology and proliferation, circHTT(2–6) recapitulates HD-like cellular phenotypes without altering repeat lengthUpregulation correlates with CAG burden and nuclear pathologies, suggesting utility as a biomarker of disease severity and a target to restore HTT localization[141]circHTT(2,3,4,5,6)UpST Hdh Q111/Q111 striatal cells, HEK293T and SH-SY5YzQ175 and Htt Q111/20 knock-in miceiPSC-derived terminally differentiated neurons (hTDNs) from HD patients (CAG 43–180) vs controls ( < 36)40S ribosomal subunitAlthough this circRNA is not translated in the adult mouse brain, its binding to ribosomes suggests that it may regulate the translation of other proteins.
