Key circRNAs and eRNAs in Huntington’s disease and their applications as diagnostic/prognostic biomarkers

The association between miR-142 and HD has been established through the circHectd1/miR-142/autophagy axis. In astrocytes, upregulated circHectd1 has been shown to enhance TIPARP expression level via the miR-142-TIPARP axis. Subsequently, elevated TIPARP levels trigger the production of LC3-II, hence promoting the activation of autophagy [146].

Based on evidence, the circHTT (2–6), which encompasses exons 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 of the HTT gene, has been upregulated in the brains of HD individuals. The upregulation of circHTT (2–6) in HEK293 and SH-SY5Y cells demonstrated no modification to the CAG repeat region, which is considered the primary cause of HD. However, this upregulation lead to a decrease in cell proliferation, nuclear size, and nuclear localization of the HTT protein. These effects were consistent with the pathophysiological abnormalities observed in HD [141].

It was demonstrated in a study that circAKAP7 specifically interacted with miR-155-5p, hence activating ATG12 as an autophagy-related gene and consequently stimulating autophagy [147]. Growing evidence have indicated the importance of autophagy in maintenance of the synapse in HD [140].

In a study conducted by Ayyildiz and colleagues using HD mouse models and RNA sequencing (RNA-seq), it was demonstrated that although neural progenitor cells (NPCs) predominantly express more circRNAs than embryonic stem cells (ESCs), cells harboring the mutant HTT gene exhibit an aberrant set of linear and back-splicing-generated circular RNA isoforms. Notably, circRNA expression levels in NPCs were inversely correlated with the length of the CAG repeat expansion in the HTT gene, indicating HTT mutation impairs RNA circularization. Furthermore, approximately 32.7% of the differentially expressed circRNAs showed N6-methyladenosine (m6A) modifications. This suggest that mutant huntingtin may disrupt circRNA biogenesis via m6A-mediated regulatory mechanisms, particularly in NPCs [148] (Fig. 3).Fig. 3The schematic illustration demonstrates the roles of different circRNAs in Huntington’s disease. circRNAs by sponging some miRNAs and influencing various genes, modulating HD

The schematic illustration demonstrates the roles of different circRNAs in Huntington’s disease. circRNAs by sponging some miRNAs and influencing various genes, modulating HD

In HD, eRNA dysregulation can abolish transcriptional networks to lead to mutant HTT-induced neurodegeneration. eRNA dysregulation could offer new insights into HD pathogenesis and therapeutic targeting [149]. A brain-specific ultraconservative eRNA called Evf2 can help DLX and MECP2 control the homeodomain transcription factor Dlx5/6 in their intergenic enhancer regions. Evf2 mutations during the early postnatal period decrease the number of GABAergic interneurons in the hippocampus and dentate gyrus. Additionally, these mutations have been found to diminish synaptic blockage in the brains of adult individuals [150].

GABA-regulated circuit dysfunction, which disrupts the balance between excitatory and inhibitory signaling, has been strongly implicated in a lot of neurological conditions, including neurodegenerative disorders such as Huntington’s disease and epilepsy, neurodevelopmental disorders like autism and Tourette’s syndrome, as well as other disorders, including dystonia and hepatic encephalopathy [151–153] (Table 3 and Fig. 5).

The study of circRNAs and eRNAs in Huntington’s disease, while still in its early stages, reveals unique diagnostic and therapeutic opportunities shaped by the monogenic nature of the disease. In contrast to sporadic neurological disorders, the clear genetic etiology of HD provides a clear context for interpreting ncRNA dysregulation. The inverse correlation between CAG repeat length and expression of specific circRNAs in neural progenitor cells suggests that circRNA signatures can serve as sensitive molecular readouts of “genetic burden,” potentially reflecting disease susceptibility and time to progression even before the onset of symptoms [148]. Furthermore, the accumulation of circHTT variants, which mimic cellular pathologies such as aberrant nuclear localization of HTT without themselves containing expanded CAG repeats, positions them as novel biomarkers that report cellular stress responses downstream of mutant HTT protein [142].

Regulatory networks formed by circRNAs, such as the circHectd1/miR-142/TIPARP and circAKAP7/miR-155-5p/ATG12 axes, highlight autophagy as a central pathway that is susceptible to targeted circRNA intervention [147, 154]. Modulation of these circuits could enhance clearance of mutant huntingtin protein. From a transcriptional perspective, dysfunction of GABAergic circuits, which are regulated in part by eRNAs such as Evf2, suggests a compelling strategy [150]. Several studies have conducted additional research to explore the clinical relevance of eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders. For instance, evidence indicates that eRNA affects neuronal genes related to HD within the striatum. This suggests targeting striatal enhancers to boost eRNA expressions and improving the neural gene repression observed in HD patients [149]. In the context of neurodegenerative disorders, most of the research on how eRNAs can be used in the clinic, such as for diagnosis or targeted therapy, is still in its early stages [155] (Tables 3). Targeted epigenetic therapies or engineered transcription factors designed to stabilize the expression of key eRNAs could restore inhibitory balance and counteract the excitotoxicity observed in HD, offering a novel approach to correct the underlying transcriptional defects caused by mutant HTT.

Nonetheless, to validate this hypothesis, a rigorous investigation of the role of autophagy-related circRNAs in the progression of HD is necessary. chunks.section: Clinical implications of circRNAs and eRNAs in HD
chunks.text: The study of circRNAs and eRNAs in Huntington’s disease, while still in its early stages, reveals unique diagnostic and therapeutic opportunities shaped by the monogenic nature of the disease. In contrast to sporadic neurological disorders, the clear genetic etiology of HD provides a clear context for interpreting ncRNA dysregulation. The inverse correlation between CAG repeat length and expression of specific circRNAs in neural progenitor cells suggests that circRNA signatures can serve as sensitive molecular readouts of “genetic burden,” potentially reflecting disease susceptibility and time to progression even before the onset of symptoms [148]. Furthermore, the accumulation of circHTT variants, which mimic cellular pathologies such as aberrant nuclear localization of HTT without themselves containing expanded CAG repeats, positions them as novel biomarkers that report cellular stress responses downstream of mutant HTT protein [142].

chunks.section: Clinical implications of circRNAs and eRNAs in HD
chunks.text: Regulatory networks formed by circRNAs, such as the circHectd1/miR-142/TIPARP and circAKAP7/miR-155-5p/ATG12 axes, highlight autophagy as a central pathway that is susceptible to targeted circRNA intervention [147, 154]. Modulation of these circuits could enhance clearance of mutant huntingtin protein. From a transcriptional perspective, dysfunction of GABAergic circuits, which are regulated in part by eRNAs such as Evf2, suggests a compelling strategy [150]. Several studies have conducted additional research to explore the clinical relevance of eRNAs in neurodegenerative disorders. For instance, evidence indicates that eRNA affects neuronal genes related to HD within the striatum. This suggests targeting striatal enhancers to boost eRNA expressions and improving the neural gene repression observed in HD patients [149]. In the context of neurodegenerative disorders, most of the research on how eRNAs can be used in the clinic, such as for diagnosis or targeted therapy, is still in its early stages [155] (Tables 3). Targeted epigenetic therapies or engineered transcription factors designed to stabilize the expression of key eRNAs could restore inhibitory balance and counteract the excitotoxicity observed in HD, offering a novel approach to correct the underlying transcriptional defects caused by mutant HTT.

While MS is classically considered a demyelinating autoimmune disease, progressive neurodegeneration is recognized as the main substrate for irreversible neurological disability in its chronic stages. The pathological continuum of MS includes inflammatory demyelination and subsequent axonal scission, neuronal loss, and synaptic damage, hallmarks shared with conventional neurodegenerative disorders. This neurodegenerative component is particularly prevalent in progressive forms of MS, where clinical deterioration continues independent of overt inflammatory activity [156]. Therefore, examining circRNAs and eRNAs in the context of MS offers a unique opportunity to investigate how ncRNA networks associated with neuroinflammatory stimuli are linked to downstream neurodegenerative processes. Specifically, these regulatory RNAs may modulate: (1) glial activation states and neuroinflammatory cascades; (2) mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress in neurons; (3) remyelination failure and oligodendrocyte pathology; and (4) direct mechanisms of axonal degeneration. Therefore, the investigation of circRNAs and eRNAs in MS provides important insights into the regulatory mechanisms that drive neurodegeneration in both autoimmune and proteinopathies [157].

The clinical subgroups encompass clinically isolated syndrome (CIS), relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (RRMS), primary progressive multiple sclerosis (PPMS), and secondary progressive multiple sclerosis (SPMS) [158]. The predominant manifestation of the disease is RRMS, which represents approximately 80% of cases and is characterized by recurring episodes of disease exacerbation. PPMS manifests in around 5–15% of cases, displaying a distinct clinical profile characterized by a gradual deterioration of neurological function without the occurrence of relapses. Following a period of 10–15 years, RRMS undergoes a transition to a secondary progressive form characterized by a slow advancement of the disease [159].

CircRNAs have been implicated in the development and progression of autoimmune diseases, such as MS. A large number of circular RNAs in peripheral blood exhibited differential expression in the MS cohort [160, 161]. This can lead to aberrant expression of genes, including latent viral genes (HERVs) and the inflammatory gene leptin (LEP).The Integrator complex is emerging as a novel player in MS pathology and a potential therapeutic target.[169]

Key circRNAs and eRNAs in multiple sclerosis and their applications as diagnostic/prognostic biomarkers

It has been observed that hsa_circ_0106803, originating from the GSDMB gene, exhibited a 2.8-fold increase in expression levels among individuals diagnosed with RRMS [167]. Multiple miRNAs were identified as having multiple target sites in hsa_circ_0106803, notably including miR-1275 and miR-149, both of which exhibit differential expression in the blood of individuals with MS [170].

miR-149 has been observed to interact with the protein ASIC1a, resulting in ASIC1a downregulation [171]. The ASIC1a gene is responsible for encoding a specific subunit of the acid-sensing ion channel. This particular subunit is found to be highly expressed in acute MS lesions, suggesting its potential involvement in neurological disease mechanisms underlying the development of this illness [112, 120, 123, 172]. Based on the available evidence, researchers have put forth the concept of a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) network, suggesting that hsa_circ_0106803 may have a role in influencing the development of MS via controlling the expression of ASIC1a mRNA through miR-149 [173]. miR-1275 is a microRNA coding gene that is functional and has been directly associated with multiple sclerosis. This microRNA is involved in the TGF-beta signaling pathway and has been confirmed to exhibit varying methylation statuses along with distinct expression patterns across different IDH expression glioma subtypes [174].

Studies have shown a significant association between the expression level of the circular RNA circINPP4B and the clinical prognosis of MS. In the experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE) mice, circINPP4B expression was markedly elevated, and this increase was directly correlated with the severity of clinical symptoms. Furthermore, circINPP4B acts as a sponge for miR-30a, a microRNA whose overexpression inhibits excessive Th17 cell differentiation, a key pathogenic factor in MS and EAE. By sequestering miR-30a, circINPP4B suppresses its anti-inflammatory effects, leading to enhanced Th17 differentiation and progression of EAE [175].

Recent evidence indicates that the circular RNA HECW2 (circ_HECW2) plays a pivotal role in the pathogenesis of MS. Elevated expression of circ_HECW2 has been observed in both in vitro and in vivo MS models and is associated with the induction of endothelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EndoMT), a process recognized as a critical contributor to blood-brain barrier (BBB) impairment and increased permeability. Yang et al. reported that circ_HECW2 functions as a sponge for microRNA-30D (miR-30D), thereby resulting in upregulation of ATG5, a core component of the autophagy pathway, activating the NOTCH signaling pathway, and ultimately promoting LPS-induced EndoMT [176]. In addition, Dong and his team revealed that circ_HECW2 also interacts with miR-30e-5p and, through modulation of neural growth regulator 1 (NGR1) levels, inhibits endothelial cell proliferation, increases apoptosis, and enhances LPS-induced EndoMT [177].

For the first time, a study based on transcriptomic data classified MS into three distinct molecular subtypes: MS-FCRL1 (C1), MS-BTG1 (C2), and MS-RPL38 (C3). Each of these subtypes is linked to various immune- and infection-related signaling pathways. In particular, the investigation of circRNA/miRNA regulatory networks in this study identified 18 differentially expressed circRNAs and 22 differentially expressed miRNAs implicated in subtype C3. Furthermore, EEF1D and TUBA1A were identified as central hub genes involved in neural and immune functions within this subtype, and finally, the circ_0045537/miR-196a-5p/TUBA1A axis was proposed as a potential regulatory pathway modulating disease severity in the C3 subtype [178]. Also, Lodde et al. reported a significant decrease in the expression level of hsa_circ_0018905 in patients with higher levels of disability and in progressive forms of multiple sclerosis (MS), suggesting its potential as a biomarker for MS [179] (Fig. 4).Fig. 4The schematic diagram represents the functional importance of circRNAs in multiple sclerosis and their related roles as a inhibitor, activator, and sponge by binding to miRNA

The schematic diagram represents the functional importance of circRNAs in multiple sclerosis and their related roles as a inhibitor, activator, and sponge by binding to miRNA

In another study, circ_0005402 and circ_0035560 from the ANXA2 gene exhibit reduced expression levels in patients with multiple sclerosis, indicating their potential as biomarkers for the disease. They discovered that circ_0005402 contains binding sites for several RNA-binding proteins, including AGO2, EIF4A3, LIN28A, IGF2BP1, and PTB. Additionally, it features two binding sites for miR-1248 and miR-766. This suggests the possibility of a collaborative regulation involving a circRNA-miRNA-mRNA axis in the pathophysiology of MS [166]. In a study by Mohammed, it was reported that ANXA2 is recognized for its involvement in the passage through the blood-brain barrier (BBB) and for enhancing miR-155 expression through the phosphorylation of STAT3 (Signal Transducer and Activator of Transcription 3). Notably, miR-155 serves as a biomarker for multiple sclerosis (MS), detectable in the bodily fluids of patients prior to the disease’s onset, and has been reported to rise in expression during relapse phases [157].

Bioinformatics research of circRNAs derived from non-coding regions of the genome linked to MS showed that these circRNAs might make people more likely to get MS [180]. Another study revealed an association between immunoglobulin (IgG) levels in the brain fluid and the presence of exosomal circRNAs in MS [181]. In addition, Zurawska et al. have recently provided a study on circRNAs that 3 circRNAs, hsa_circRNA_101348, hsa_circRNA_102611, and hsa_circRNA104361, are differentially overexpressed in individuals with RRMS in relapse and have the ability to influence B-cell activity [160]. Furthermore, these three circRNAs exhibited a significant correlation with MRI Gd-enhancing lesions, thereby suggesting their potential involvement in the activity of inflammatory diseases. Given the critical role of B cells in the pathogenesis of multiple sclerosis (MS), they have uncovered two transcripts related to B cells, namely AK2 and IKZF3 [160]. These observations were corroborated by the direct evidence of an upregulation of AK2 and IKZF3 in the peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) of patients experiencing a relapse of MS. The AK2 gene is recognized for encoding phosphotransferase adenylate kinase 2, fulfilling the cell-specific requirements necessary for mitochondrial functions, especially in the activation of B cells and the production of antibodies. The significance of AK2 has been particularly highlighted in B cells infected with the Epstein-Barr virus, which has been extensively discussed in relation to MS as a viral infection associated with the disease. IKZF3 regulates the development of lymphoid and myeloid cells as well as immune homeostasis [160].

While the bulk of circRNAs that were differentially expressed exhibited upregulation, it is worth noting that a subset of circRNAs showed downregulation in those affected by MS.

The results of the global hybridization experiment, which was then confirmed by PCR in a separate group of people, showed that there were two circRNAs, hsa_circRNA_101145 and hsa_circRNA_001896, with expression levels that were significantly lower in MS patients during remission and significantly associated with alleviation in disease-induced disability [165]. In people with RRMS who are in remission, reducing the amount of hsa_circRNA_101145 and hsa_circRNA_001896 could increase the function of all miRNAs that are vulnerable to being targeted by these two circRNAs. One example of the mentioned miRNAs is miR-181c, whose increased level in the cerebrospinal fluid has been linked to a higher chance that clinically isolated syndrome (CIS) will turn into confirmed MS [182].

There is new evidence suggesting that eRNA dysregulation may be involved in MS pathogenesis via alterations of immune response and neuronal function. Understanding of eRNAs has the potential to provide new insights into MS mechanisms and therapeutic targets [183]. Several study investigated the role of super-enhancers (SEs) and enhancer RNAs (eRNAs) in autoimmune diseases. Their findings indicated that increased activity or dysfunction of individual SEs could lead to the development of various autoimmune disorders [184, 185, 185–191]. However, despite growing interest in the regulatory roles of eRNAs in autoimmune diseases and neurodegenerative disorders, there is currently no direct evidence supporting the involvement of eRNAs in the pathogenesis of MS as a disease considered both autoimmune and neurodegenerative. Nevertheless, one study demonstrated that MS-associated single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) are located within vitamin D receptor (VDR) binding sites and in activated super-enhancers (SEs), especially in VDR-associated SEs (VSEs), in CD4+ T cells and monocytes [184, 188] (Table 4 and Supplementary Table S3). These disease-linked SNPs are believed to influence the regulatory function of these SEs. This spatial correspondence between MS risk SNPs and active enhancer markers strongly suggests that dysregulation of SEs and their associated eRNAs is a key component of MS pathophysiology. This hypothesis proposes that genetic risk is exerted in part by altering the transcriptional output of immune cells via enhancer elements, and provides a new perspective linking genetic susceptibility to functional dysregulation in MS, and identifies eRNAs generated from these SEs as potential biomarkers or therapeutic targets. However, it remains unclear how the presence of these SNPs affects the expression of these SE-derived eRNAs and what impact these eRNAs might have on MS pathogenesis. Thus, this area of research remains largely unexplored and requires further investigation in the future (Fig. 5).Fig. 5Mechanistic roles of eRNAs in neurodegenerative diseases
Mechanistic roles of eRNAs in neurodegenerative diseases

CircRNAs have been implicated in the development and progression of autoimmune diseases, such as MS. A large number of circular RNAs in peripheral blood exhibited differential expression in the MS cohort [160, 161]. Key circRNAs and eRNAs in multiple sclerosis and their applications as diagnostic/prognostic biomarkers

It has been observed that hsa_circ_0106803, originating from the GSDMB gene, exhibited a 2.8-fold increase in expression levels among individuals diagnosed with RRMS [167]. Multiple miRNAs were identified as having multiple target sites in hsa_circ_0106803, notably including miR-1275 and miR-149, both of which exhibit differential expression in the blood of individuals with MS [170].

miR-149 has been observed to interact with the protein ASIC1a, resulting in ASIC1a downregulation [171]. The ASIC1a gene is responsible for encoding a specific subunit of the acid-sensing ion channel. This particular subunit is found to be highly expressed in acute MS lesions, suggesting its potential involvement in neurological disease mechanisms underlying the development of this illness [112, 120, 123, 172]. Based on the available evidence, researchers have put forth the concept of a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) network, suggesting that hsa_circ_0106803 may have a role in influencing the development of MS via controlling the expression of ASIC1a mRNA through miR-149 [173]. miR-1275 is a microRNA coding gene that is functional and has been directly associated with multiple sclerosis. This microRNA is involved in the TGF-beta signaling pathway and has been confirmed to exhibit varying methylation statuses along with distinct expression patterns across different IDH expression glioma subtypes [174].

Studies have shown a significant association between the expression level of the circular RNA circINPP4B and the clinical prognosis of MS. In the experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE) mice, circINPP4B expression was markedly elevated, and this increase was directly correlated with the severity of clinical symptoms. Furthermore, circINPP4B acts as a sponge for miR-30a, a microRNA whose overexpression inhibits excessive Th17 cell differentiation, a key pathogenic factor in MS and EAE. By sequestering miR-30a, circINPP4B suppresses its anti-inflammatory effects, leading to enhanced Th17 differentiation and progression of EAE [175].

Recent evidence indicates that the circular RNA HECW2 (circ_HECW2) plays a pivotal role in the pathogenesis of MS. Elevated expression of circ_HECW2 has been observed in both in vitro and in vivo MS models and is associated with the induction of endothelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EndoMT), a process recognized as a critical contributor to blood-brain barrier (BBB) impairment and increased permeability. Yang et al. reported that circ_HECW2 functions as a sponge for microRNA-30D (miR-30D), thereby resulting in upregulation of ATG5, a core component of the autophagy pathway, activating the NOTCH signaling pathway, and ultimately promoting LPS-induced EndoMT [176]. In addition, Dong and his team revealed that circ_HECW2 also interacts with miR-30e-5p and, through modulation of neural growth regulator 1 (NGR1) levels, inhibits endothelial cell proliferation, increases apoptosis, and enhances LPS-induced EndoMT [177].

For the first time, a study based on transcriptomic data classified MS into three distinct molecular subtypes: MS-FCRL1 (C1), MS-BTG1 (C2), and MS-RPL38 (C3). Each of these subtypes is linked to various immune- and infection-related signaling pathways. In particular, the investigation of circRNA/miRNA regulatory networks in this study identified 18 differentially expressed circRNAs and 22 differentially expressed miRNAs implicated in subtype C3. Furthermore, EEF1D and TUBA1A were identified as central hub genes involved in neural and immune functions within this subtype, and finally, the circ_0045537/miR-196a-5p/TUBA1A axis was proposed as a potential regulatory pathway modulating disease severity in the C3 subtype [178]. Also, Lodde et al. reported a significant decrease in the expression level of hsa_circ_0018905 in patients with higher levels of disability and in progressive forms of multiple sclerosis (MS), suggesting its potential as a biomarker for MS [179] (Fig. 4).Fig. 4The schematic diagram represents the functional importance of circRNAs in multiple sclerosis and their related roles as a inhibitor, activator, and sponge by binding to miRNA

The schematic diagram represents the functional importance of circRNAs in multiple sclerosis and their related roles as a inhibitor, activator, and sponge by binding to miRNA

In another study, circ_0005402 and circ_0035560 from the ANXA2 gene exhibit reduced expression levels in patients with multiple sclerosis, indicating their potential as biomarkers for the disease. They discovered that circ_0005402 contains binding sites for several RNA-binding proteins, including AGO2, EIF4A3, LIN28A, IGF2BP1, and PTB. Additionally, it features two binding sites for miR-1248 and miR-766. This suggests the possibility of a collaborative regulation involving a circRNA-miRNA-mRNA axis in the pathophysiology of MS [166]. In a study by Mohammed, it was reported that ANXA2 is recognized for its involvement in the passage through the blood-brain barrier (BBB) and for enhancing miR-155 expression through the phosphorylation of STAT3 (Signal Transducer and Activator of Transcription 3). Notably, miR-155 serves as a biomarker for multiple sclerosis (MS), detectable in the bodily fluids of patients prior to the disease’s onset, and has been reported to rise in expression during relapse phases [157].

Bioinformatics research of circRNAs derived from non-coding regions of the genome linked to MS showed that these circRNAs might make people more likely to get MS [180]. Another study revealed an association between immunoglobulin (IgG) levels in the brain fluid and the presence of exosomal circRNAs in MS [181]. In addition, Zurawska et al. have recently provided a study on circRNAs that 3 circRNAs, hsa_circRNA_101348, hsa_circRNA_102611, and hsa_circRNA104361, are differentially overexpressed in individuals with RRMS in relapse and have the ability to influence B-cell activity [160]. Furthermore, these three circRNAs exhibited a significant correlation with MRI Gd-enhancing lesions, thereby suggesting their potential involvement in the activity of inflammatory diseases. Given the critical role of B cells in the pathogenesis of multiple sclerosis (MS), they have uncovered two transcripts related to B cells, namely AK2 and IKZF3 [160]. These observations were corroborated by the direct evidence of an upregulation of AK2 and IKZF3 in the peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) of patients experiencing a relapse of MS. The AK2 gene is recognized for encoding phosphotransferase adenylate kinase 2, fulfilling the cell-specific requirements necessary for mitochondrial functions, especially in the activation of B cells and the production of antibodies. The significance of AK2 has been particularly highlighted in B cells infected with the Epstein-Barr virus, which has been extensively discussed in relation to MS as a viral infection associated with the disease. IKZF3 regulates the development of lymphoid and myeloid cells as well as immune homeostasis [160].

While the bulk of circRNAs that were differentially expressed exhibited upregulation, it is worth noting that a subset of circRNAs showed downregulation in those affected by MS.

The results of the global hybridization experiment, which was then confirmed by PCR in a separate group of people, showed that there were two circRNAs, hsa_circRNA_101145 and hsa_circRNA_001896, with expression levels that were significantly lower in MS patients during remission and significantly associated with alleviation in disease-induced disability [165]. In people with RRMS who are in remission, reducing the amount of hsa_circRNA_101145 and hsa_circRNA_001896 could increase the function of all miRNAs that are vulnerable to being targeted by these two circRNAs. One example of the mentioned miRNAs is miR-181c, whose increased level in the cerebrospinal fluid has been linked to a higher chance that clinically isolated syndrome (CIS) will turn into confirmed MS [182].

There is new evidence suggesting that eRNA dysregulation may be involved in MS pathogenesis via alterations of immune response and neuronal function. Understanding of eRNAs has the potential to provide new insights into MS mechanisms and therapeutic targets [183]. Several study investigated the role of super-enhancers (SEs) and enhancer RNAs (eRNAs) in autoimmune diseases. Their findings indicated that increased activity or dysfunction of individual SEs could lead to the development of various autoimmune disorders [184, 185, 185–191]. However, despite growing interest in the regulatory roles of eRNAs in autoimmune diseases and neurodegenerative disorders, there is currently no direct evidence supporting the involvement of eRNAs in the pathogenesis of MS as a disease considered both autoimmune and neurodegenerative. Nevertheless, one study demonstrated that MS-associated single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) are located within vitamin D receptor (VDR) binding sites and in activated super-enhancers (SEs), especially in VDR-associated SEs (VSEs), in CD4+ T cells and monocytes [184, 188] (Table 4 and Supplementary Table S3). These disease-linked SNPs are believed to influence the regulatory function of these SEs. This spatial correspondence between MS risk SNPs and active enhancer markers strongly suggests that dysregulation of SEs and their associated eRNAs is a key component of MS pathophysiology. This hypothesis proposes that genetic risk is exerted in part by altering the transcriptional output of immune cells via enhancer elements, and provides a new perspective linking genetic susceptibility to functional dysregulation in MS, and identifies eRNAs generated from these SEs as potential biomarkers or therapeutic targets. However, it remains unclear how the presence of these SNPs affects the expression of these SE-derived eRNAs and what impact these eRNAs might have on MS pathogenesis. Thus, this area of research remains largely unexplored and requires further investigation in the future (Fig. 5).Fig. 5Mechanistic roles of eRNAs in neurodegenerative diseases
Mechanistic roles of eRNAs in neurodegenerative diseases

chunks.section: Clinical implications of circRNAs and eRNAs in MS
chunks.text:
Iparraguirre et al. identified six sex-dependent circRNAs as potential biomarkers for MS. Their study was conducted on a cohort consisting of 30 MS patients and 20 healthy controls and subsequently validated in an independent validation group of 70 additional MS patients. The identified circRNAs -hsa_circ_0141241, hsa_circ_0058514, circPADI4, hsa_circ_0001947, hsa_circ_0001707, and hsa_circ_0001459—exhibited significant upregulation in female MS subjects, whereas male subjects showed no comparable increase in their expression [168]. In addition, another study reported 166 differentially expressed circRNAs in individuals with MS, of which 125 showed decreased expression. Among these, hsa_circ_0007990 (derived from the PGAP3 gene) was notably downregulated. This reduction in expression was also confirmed in two independent replication cohorts, suggesting circPGAP3 as a promising biomarker for multiple sclerosis [162].

Numerous studies have highlighted the significance of circRNA expression levels, including circINPP4B, circ_0005402, circ_0035560, and hsa_circ_0018905, in predicting the clinical trajectory of MS patients. The expression levels of these transcripts may assist clinicians in selecting more effective treatment options tailored to the specific circRNA expression patterns observed in each patient, thereby indicating a possible advantage as biomarkers for the detection of MS and facilitating personalized therapeutic approaches.

chunks.section: Clinical implications of circRNAs and eRNAs in MS
chunks.text: The association between dysregulation of eRNAs and a range of human disorders suggests the potential of these molecules to be used as novel therapeutic tools in various diseases. The attractiveness of eRNA therapy lies in its ability to regulate transcription, in contrast to other non-coding RNAs such as miRNAs, which mostly exert their effects post-transcriptionally. Moreover, it should be noted that each eRNA-producing domain exerts a limited impact on a localized set of adjacent genes in a cis-acting manner, thereby ensuring a high degree of target specificity and reducing the likelihood of unintended consequences. The expression pattern of eRNAs in specific tissues and cell types is considered a valuable characteristic contributing to the mitigation of potential off-target effects [155].

chunks.section: Converging subjects and integrated regulatory networks
chunks.text:
Our comprehensive review reveals significant convergent patterns of circRNA and eRNA dysregulation across major neurological disorders, suggesting common molecular mechanisms underlying apparently distinct pathological processes. Three core regulatory subjects consistently emerge:

Several synapse-enriched circRNAs (e.g., circHOMER1, circGRIA1) show consistent downregulation in AD, PD, and HD, suggesting a common disruption in synaptic maintenance programs [192–194].

Activity-dependent eRNAs that regulate immediate early genes (e.g., Fos, Arc) show impaired induction in disease models, suggesting compromised activity-transcription coupling [195, 196].

The circHOMER1/miR-651/PSEN1-2 axis in AD [197] parallels the circSNCA/miR-7/SNCA axis in PD [198], revealing a conserved pattern of synaptic circRNAs that regulate key disease proteins through miRNA spongification.

Proinflammatory circRNAs (e.g., circ_0000950, circ_0005835) are upregulated in multiple disorders and drive microglial activation and cytokine production through shared miRNA pathways (miR-103, miR-576-3p) [63, 66].

Enhancer RNAs that regulate immune response genes (e.g., AANCR-APOE in AD, SNCA enhancer RNAs in PD) demonstrate disease-specific modulation of neuroinflammatory pathways [71, 119].

Consistent involvement of NF-κB and cytokine signaling pathways implicates neuroinflammation as a global enhancer of neurodegeneration [199].

chunks.section: Converging subjects and integrated regulatory networks
chunks.text:
Several circRNAs (circHectd1, circAKAP7, circLRP1B) converge in regulating autophagy in HD, PD, and AD [144, 146, 147], suggesting that maintaining proteostasis is a critical vulnerability node.

circRNA-mediated regulation of ubiquitin enzymes (e.g., ciRS-7/UBE2A in AD, circRIMS2/UBE2K in synaptic dysfunction) reveals common protein degradation defects [74, 88].

Enhancer RNAs that modulate proteostasis genes exhibit disease-specific expression patterns but target common biological processes.

Early studies indicate potential direct and indirect interactions between circRNA and eRNA networks. For example, eRNAs that regulate BDNF expression may influence the biogenesis of activity-dependent circRNAs, while specific circRNAs could potentially feed back to modulate chromatin accessibility through RBP interactions. This multi-layered regulatory crosstalk represents a promising area for future research.

A recurring pattern emerges throughout this review: eRNAs and circRNAs often converge on the same pathogenic pathways, but act at distinct regulatory levels. For example, in Alzheimer’s disease, the eRNA AANCR transcriptionally activates APOE expression [105], whereas several circRNAs (e.g., circCORO1C, circHOMER1) posttranscriptionally regulate APP processing and γ-secretase activity [48]. This multilevel regulation may provide robust homeostatic control in healthy states but amplify pathological cascades in disease.

chunks.section: Converging subjects and integrated regulatory networks
chunks.text:
The potential for eRNA-circRNA interference—in which enhancer-derived transcripts influence circRNA biogenesis or vice versa—demonstrates an intriguing, albeit still speculative, layer of complexity. For instance, neuronal activity-dependent eRNAs could potentially modulate the expression of RNA-binding proteins (e.g., QKI, MBL) that control circRNA biogenesis, thereby creating a feed-forward loop between transcriptional and post-transcriptional regulation [200, 201].

While miRNA sponging represents a well-known mechanism, our synthesis provides significant evidence for alternative circRNA functions in neurodegeneration:

circCwc27 modulates the nucleocytoplasmic trafficking of Pur-α, affecting its DNA binding capacity and transcriptional regulation of AD-related genes [60].

circFoxo3 acts as a protein scaffold and enhances interactions between senescence-related proteins in age-related cognitive decline [15].

circGigyf2 forms complexes containing CPSF6 to regulate mRNA processing and nuclear polyadenylation site selection [92].

circAβ-a potentially encodes pathogenic peptides that contribute to amyloid pathology through non-canonical translational mechanisms [75].

Nuclear circRNAs (e.g., circEIF3J, circPAIP2) interact with U1 snRNP and RNA Pol II to enhance transcription of the parental gene [16].

chunks.section: Converging subjects and integrated regulatory networks
chunks.text:
Emerging evidence suggests that circRNAs may participate in biomolecular condensations and potentially influence the subcellular organization of RNA-protein complexes in neurons. High local concentrations of RBPs on circRNA scaffolds could nucleate phase separation events associated with pathological aggregation.

Similarly, eRNAs exhibit mechanisms beyond the enhancer-promoter loop, including:

Dissociation of the NELF complex to release stalled RNA polymerase II [23].

Modulation of chromatin accessibility through recruitment of remodeling complexes [22].

Facilitation of three-dimensional genome organization as predicted by computational models [22].

This functional diversity suggests that these ncRNAs act as multifaceted regulators rather than unidimensional sponges, opening new avenues for therapeutic targeting beyond miRNA inhibition.

chunks.section: Beyond miRNA sponging: expanding the functional paradigm
chunks.text: While miRNA sponging represents a well-known mechanism, our synthesis provides significant evidence for alternative circRNA functions in neurodegeneration:

circCwc27 modulates the nucleocytoplasmic trafficking of Pur-α, affecting its DNA binding capacity and transcriptional regulation of AD-related genes [60].

circFoxo3 acts as a protein scaffold and enhances interactions between senescence-related proteins in age-related cognitive decline [15].

circGigyf2 forms complexes containing CPSF6 to regulate mRNA processing and nuclear polyadenylation site selection [92].

circAβ-a potentially encodes pathogenic peptides that contribute to amyloid pathology through non-canonical translational mechanisms [75].

Nuclear circRNAs (e.g., circEIF3J, circPAIP2) interact with U1 snRNP and RNA Pol II to enhance transcription of the parental gene [16].

Emerging evidence suggests that circRNAs may participate in biomolecular condensations and potentially influence the subcellular organization of RNA-protein complexes in neurons. High local concentrations of RBPs on circRNA scaffolds could nucleate phase separation events associated with pathological aggregation.

Similarly, eRNAs exhibit mechanisms beyond the enhancer-promoter loop, including:

Dissociation of the NELF complex to release stalled RNA polymerase II [23].

Modulation of chromatin accessibility through recruitment of remodeling complexes [22].

Facilitation of three-dimensional genome organization as predicted by computational models [22].

This functional diversity suggests that these ncRNAs act as multifaceted regulators rather than unidimensional sponges, opening new avenues for therapeutic targeting beyond miRNA inhibition.

Despite the growing interest in circRNAs and eRNAs as key regulators of neurodegeneration, we face several challenges before these findings can be applied in clinical settings:

circRNAs and eRNAs are often present in low concentrations in biofluids like blood, plasma, serum, or exosomes [202]. To identify disease-specific signatures amidst the background noise, we need highly sensitive techniques such as single-molecule sequencing [203] or digital PCR [204]. Many eRNAs are fleeting and unstable, which complicates functional testing [205]. We need better RNA-stabilization methods and nascent RNA capture techniques, like GRO-seq [206].

Bulk tissue studies often mix the circRNA/eRNA profiles from both neuronal and glial cells. By using single-cell or nucleus RNA sequencing on samples from patients, we can start to understand what these molecules do in specific cell types [207, 208].

While many studies link circRNA/eRNA dysregulation to various diseases, they don’t always show that one causes the other. To establish these connections, we need to use conditional knockout or overexpression models, like AAV-driven circRNA sponges [209].

In terms of specificity and standardization, some candidate circRNAs linked to Alzheimer’s disease also show changes in conditions like cancer or inflammation [210]. This means we need to validate these findings across different diseases. Moreover, the lack of standardized methods for isolating circRNAs from biofluids—whether they are exosomal or free—limits our ability to reproduce results.

Most current biomarker research is primarily cross-sectional. We really need large-scale longitudinal studies that include pre-symptomatic patients with Alzheimer’s or Parkinson’s disease to track circRNA and eRNA changes as the diseases progress.

Delivering RNA-based therapies across the blood-brain barrier remains difficult. Researchers are exploring nanoparticle and exosome-based delivery systems that specifically target brain-related circRNAs and eRNAs [211, 212]. Also, the use of artificial intelligence in bioinformatics is improving how we model antigen screening and delivery systems [213]. We need to understand the off-target effects of circRNA modulation, like unintended miRNA sponging, which requires thorough in vivo safety tests.

Since eRNAs operate in cis, it’s tricky to target them without affecting the nearby chromatin structure. Techniques such as CircRNA-based AntimiR therapy [214], CRISPR-dCas9 [215], or antisense oligonucleotides could enable precise modulation of enhancers.

Combining circRNA/eRNA data with epigenomic studies (like ATAC-seq and ChIP-seq) and proteomic data will reveal the regulatory networks involved in neurodegeneration.

Besides miRNA sponging, circRNAs can encode peptides or serve as scaffolds for protein complexes, like Tau aggregation inhibitors [1]. We need a detailed analysis of their protein-binding landscapes.

Current animal models often fail to accurately reflect human-specific circRNA/eRNA expression. Investigating cerebral organoids or xenotransplant models might help close this gap.

This means we need to validate these findings across different diseases. Moreover, the lack of standardized methods for isolating circRNAs from biofluids—whether they are exosomal or free—limits our ability to reproduce results.

Most current biomarker research is primarily cross-sectional. We really need large-scale longitudinal studies that include pre-symptomatic patients with Alzheimer’s or Parkinson’s disease to track circRNA and eRNA changes as the diseases progress.

Delivering RNA-based therapies across the blood-brain barrier remains difficult. Researchers are exploring nanoparticle and exosome-based delivery systems that specifically target brain-related circRNAs and eRNAs [211, 212]. Also, the use of artificial intelligence in bioinformatics is improving how we model antigen screening and delivery systems [213]. We need to understand the off-target effects of circRNA modulation, like unintended miRNA sponging, which requires thorough in vivo safety tests.

Since eRNAs operate in cis, it’s tricky to target them without affecting the nearby chromatin structure. Techniques such as CircRNA-based AntimiR therapy [214], CRISPR-dCas9 [215], or antisense oligonucleotides could enable precise modulation of enhancers.

Combining circRNA/eRNA data with epigenomic studies (like ATAC-seq and ChIP-seq) and proteomic data will reveal the regulatory networks involved in neurodegeneration.

Besides miRNA sponging, circRNAs can encode peptides or serve as scaffolds for protein complexes, like Tau aggregation inhibitors [1]. We need a detailed analysis of their protein-binding landscapes.

Current animal models often fail to accurately reflect human-specific circRNA/eRNA expression. Investigating cerebral organoids or xenotransplant models might help close this gap.

Our understanding of circRNA biogenesis and function in the brain is still in its early stages, requiring more rigorous investigation. Implementing sophisticated research methods and strategies to modulate circRNA expression and function while maintaining the integrity of parental gene transcription would greatly enhance our understanding of circRNA biology in the brain [41]. The complex regulatory potential of circRNAs is evident in various human diseases, where they operate within networks comprising circRNAs, RNA-binding proteins, and microRNAs [216].

Even with the progress in identifying and characterizing circRNAs, many questions remain unanswered. Healthy cells intricately regulate circRNA biogenesis through both cis sequences and trans-acting factors. However, changes in the activity of these factors under pathological conditions can either increase or decrease circRNA production. We still don’t fully understand how function-related circRNAs are generated, distributed, or what role tissue-specific expression plays in creating different regulatory patterns in various biological processes. Additionally, the mechanisms governing tissue-specific expression of circRNAs and the link between their specificity and abundance in particular tissues and developmental stages are still unclear [217].

As we pay more attention to RNA biomarkers in neurodegenerative diseases, circRNAs stand out as potential reliable and cost-effective candidates. They have a unique circular structure that makes them more resistant to RNase degradation and gives them distinct structural forms compared to linear RNAs. Notably, these molecules can be detected in the bloodstream, boosting their potential not just as indicators of disease but also as targets for molecular interventions. They also tend to accumulate in the brain, especially with age. This characteristic increases their appeal for research into neurodegenerative biomarkers. It has been found that the circRNAs differentially expressed in the brain also overlap with those found in the plasma of patients with neurodegenerative diseases. This opens up the possibility of using circRNAs as peripheral biomarkers, which could lead to non-invasive diagnostic methods in the future [217].

Moreover, active enhancers produce eRNAs, which play a crucial role in regulating gene expression by affecting the transcription of target genes and exhibit unique expression profiles. eRNAs come in various types with different structures, lengths, and post-transcriptional modifications. New evidence indicates that eRNAs are significant in various cellular processes and are involved in gene regulation through enhancer-promoter chromatin looping, modulating chromatin state, and interacting with transcriptional machinery [218].

Despite the recent surge in research into eRNAs, considerable gaps remain in our understanding of their biological functions, necessitating more investigation and clarity. Since most eRNAs are unstable, we must determine if they can function like other non-coding RNAs.

We also need to develop better frameworks for modulating eRNA expression to ensure successful functional eRNA research [19]. Research has recently clarified the role of eRNAs in neurodegenerative diseases using a mix of bioinformatic and experimental approaches. Bioinformatics has shown that neurodegenerative diseases have a broader range of eRNA expression patterns. This finding enhances our understanding of the regulatory components linked to neurodegenerative disorders [40]. Recent studies have connected eRNA dysregulation to neurodegenerative disorders like AD, PD, and HD. This association offers the potential to deepen our understanding of the underlying disease mechanisms and explore possible clinical applications of eRNAs [219–221].

In summary, this review goes beyond cataloging individual ncRNAs to provide an integrated model of neurodegenerative pathogenesis. We demonstrate that circRNAs and eRNAs, although acting at different regulatory levels, often converge on critical pathways governing proteostasis, neuroinflammation, and synaptic function. Their potential interactions—whether synergistic, compensatory, or sequential—create complex regulatory networks that both maintain neuronal health and, when disrupted, drive disease progression. Future research should prioritize mapping these inter-RNA networks, developing multitarget strategies, and using computational approaches to identify key nodal points for therapeutic intervention. By moving beyond the conventional silo approach to ncRNA research, we can unleash their full potential as biomarkers and targets in the fight against neurodegenerative disorders. Ultimately, we understand that this research on eRNAs in neurodegeneration is less comprehensive than that on circRNAs, a variation observed in the depth of discussion between parts of this review. More studies focused on describing the enhancer topography and effective eRNAs in the human brain are fundamental to obtaining a more balanced and integrated comprehensive understanding of ncRNA-based regulatory networks in health and condition.

Neuroinflammation is a common characteristic feature in neurodegenerative diseases (NDDs) and aging brains, which plays a major role in cognitive decline and neurological problems. NDDs such as Alzheimer’s disease (AD), Parkinson’s disease (PD), Huntington’s disease (HD), and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) have all been associated with neuroinflammation, which further speeds up disease progression.

Microglia, the brain resident immune cells, play an important role in the regulation of neuroinflammation and synaptic degeneration. Microglial activation leads to both beneficial and harmful effects [1, 2]. In a healthy brain, microglia maintain a dynamic balance between protective, anti-inflammatory functions and detrimental, pro-inflammatory signaling [1, 3, 4]. However, in NDDs, this microglia balance is disrupted, and the activation of pro-inflammatory microglia contributes to neuronal damage and synaptic loss [5–7]. The inflammatory response amplifies through the release of cytokines, chemokines, and reactive oxygen species (ROS), creating a cycle of chronic inflammation that accelerates neurodegeneration and results in cognitive and motor impairments [8, 9].

Mitochondria, “powerhouses” of cells, are critical for neuronal survival due to their central role in energy production, calcium buffering, redox balance, and regulation of apoptotic pathways. Mitochondria contain their own DNA (mitochondrial DNA, mtDNA) in multiple copies and encode for 13 proteins required for oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS). mtDNA is vulnerable to oxidative damage due to its proximity to ROS production sites and the relatively lower capacity of DNA repair pathways. Unlike nuclear DNA, mtDNA lacks histones and is therefore more susceptible to oxidative damage, deletions, and point mutations. Accumulation of mtDNA damage could impair the OXPHOS system, leading to defects in electron transport chain (ETC) activity, increased ROS generation, and progressive bioenergetic failure, which contribute to the pathogenesis and progression of NDDs [10]. Further, mitochondrial structural damage compromises membrane integrity, which allows the release of mitochondrial contents such as mtDNA, cardiolipin, and mitochondrial proteins into the cytosol. The released mitochondrial content in cytosol or extracellular spaces acts as danger-associated molecular patterns (DAMP) and triggers innate immune responses. Cytosolic or extracellular mtDNA induces an innate immune response, which triggers neuroinflammation via immune sensors like the cyclic GMP–AMP synthase (cGAS)-stimulator of interferon genes (STING) pathway and Toll-like receptors (TLR) [11, 12]. Therefore, understanding the mtDNA-mediated neuroinflammation is crucial to develop targeted therapies for NDDs and the aging brain.

In recent years, substantial progress has been made to understand the role of neuroinflammation in the aging brain and NDDs; however, many questions remain unanswered. Specifically, the mechanisms that activate and regulate neuroinflammation and interplay between mtDNA, immune signaling, and neuroinflammatory pathways are not fully understood. This review aims to explore the emerging role of mtDNA as a neuroinflammatory switch. Here, we review the mechanisms of mtDNA release that triggers immune responses in the brain, and we highlight its potential as a therapeutic target in aging brain and NDDs. Understanding how mitochondrial dysfunction contributes to mtDNA release and subsequent neuroinflammation will open new avenues for therapeutic interventions aimed at mitigating neuroinflammation and its detrimental effects on brain health.

In humans, mtDNA is a circular genome of 16,500 base pairs, which is located in many copies in the mitochondrial matrix [13]. mtDNA encodes a total of 37 genes, of which 13 code for proteins involved in OXPHOS, 22 code for tRNA, and 2 code for rRNA [14]. This distinct gene set on mtDNA enables mitochondria to have their own independent protein synthesis system, allowing them to carry out ATP synthesis efficiently. However, the structural, functional, and maintenance work of mitochondria are dependent on thousands of nuclear-encoded proteins. Neurons are among the most energy-demanding cells in the body, and their health and proper functioning depend on mitochondria to keep up with this constant demand. mtDNA is an important factor for OXPHOS and ATP production; therefore, it supports neuronal activity, synaptic transmission, and the maintenance of bioenergetic homeostasis [15]. Mitochondria synthesize ATP through OXPHOS, which involves the transfer of electrons through the ETC. However, during ETC, excessive ROS is generated, including superoxide anions and hydrogen peroxide. mtDNA proximity to these ROS production sites makes them more vulnerable to damage, mutations, deletions, and genetic instability [16]. This constant mtDNA exposure to ROS increases the oxidative damage probability, which accumulates over time, and leads to mitochondrial dysfunction [17, 18]. Mitochondria possess some DNA repair enzymes, such as mitochondrial polymerase gamma (POLG) and a version of base excision repair; however, these systems are not as efficient as those in the nucleus [19]. The combination of high exposure to ROS and inefficient repair systems results in a higher mutation rate in mtDNA and a decline of mitochondrial ability to produce energy [20]. Some mtDNA regions are more prone to mutations, particularly in genes encoding respiratory complex components. One of the most frequently observed mtDNA mutations is the large-scale 4977-bp deletion, also known as the “common deletion”, which spans several OXPHOS genes and is often flanked by direct repeats that predispose these regions to slipped-strand mispairing during replication [21, 22]. These deletions accumulate with age and have been observed at high levels in NDD [23–25]. mtDNA copy number is identified as a dynamic parameter that represents mitochondrial biogenesis and turnover. In aging brains and NDDs, mtDNA copy number alterations have been documented, which further compromise OXPHOS and respiratory capacity [26–30]. The functional consequences of accumulated mtDNA damage are profound, as oxidization, mutation, or deletion of mtDNA molecules can impair the assembly and function of respiratory chain complexes, leading to deficient ATP synthesis and increased ROS production [19]. Beyond the bioenergetic defects, damaged mtDNA may leak from mitochondria into the cytoplasm or extracellular space [31]. Such extranuclear mtDNA acts as a potent DAMP, capable of activating innate immune sensors including cGAS, TLR9, and the NOD, LRR and pyrin domain-containing protein 3 (NLRP3) inflammasome, thus triggering neuroinflammatory cascades [32–34]. Neurons, defined by their high metabolic demand, long lifespan, and limited regenerative capacity, are especially vulnerable to mitochondrial dysfunction. Accumulation of mtDNA damage is particularly detrimental and has been implicated in the pathogenesis of NDDs such as AD, PD, HD, and ALS. Over time, progressive mtDNA mutations compromise mitochondrial integrity, leading to increased neuroinflammation, synaptic dysfunction, and neuronal loss. This vicious cycle of mtDNA damage accumulation and mitochondrial dysfunction further accelerates neurodegeneration and disease progression.

The mtDNA is normally stored in the mitochondrial matrix, enclosed by the inner and outer mitochondrial membranes (OMM), protecting it from recognition by cellular immune sensors. Under physiological conditions, mitochondrial outer and inner membranes ensure that mtDNA primarily functions for mitochondrial gene expression and energy metabolism. However, during mitochondrial stress (physical, chemical, and biological) and damage, mtDNA could be abnormally released into the cytosol or extracellular space. This cytosolic or extracellular mtDNA functions as a potential DAMP, triggering innate immune responses and neuroinflammation. Diverse physical, chemical, and biological insults can trigger mtDNA release from mitochondria by different mechanisms (Fig. 1).Fig. 1Schematic illustration depicting major cellular pathways that promote mtDNA escape from mitochondria into the cytosol and extracellular space under neurodegenerative conditions. Defective mitophagy, apoptosis, and necrosis further exacerbate mtDNA accumulation

mPTP is a multi-protein complex that forms a non-selective channel across the inner mitochondrial membrane [35]. When mPTP is open, it allows the passage of solutes ≤ 1.5 kDa, which leads to mitochondrial depolarization, swelling, rupture of the outer membrane, and release of material including mtDNA and cytochrome c [36]. mPTP opening can be caused by several factors, including higher mitochondrial Ca2⁺ level, increased oxidative stress, inorganic phosphate, and depletion of adenine nucleotide [35]. Opening of the mPTP plays a crucial role in cell death pathways, particularly in ischemia–reperfusion injury and different NDDs [37, 222]. Under pathological conditions such as calcium overload, higher ROS, ATP depletion, or ischemic injury, the mPTP can open transiently or persistently, leading to dissipation of the mitochondrial membrane potential, matrix swelling, and rupture of OMM [38–41]. This rupture of OMM permits the release of mitochondrial contents, which includes mtDNA fragments, into the cytosol, where they act as a potent DAMP [42, 43]. Although the precise molecular composition of the mPTP remains under investigation, cyclophilin D (CypD) is recognized as a key regulatory component [44, 45]. Pharmacological inhibition of CypD by cyclosporin A or genetic deletion reduces mPTP opening, mtDNA release, and downstream inflammation in various disease models [46–48]. In neurons, mPTP-mediated mtDNA release has been implicated in excitotoxicity and neuroinflammation, linking mitochondrial dysfunction to neurodegenerative pathology [12, 49].

In NDDs, persistent mPTP opening is increasingly recognized as a common mechanism driving neuronal injury [50]. In AD, Aβ accumulates in mitochondria and interacts with CypD, and promotes oxidative stress, collectively lowering the calcium threshold for mPTP opening. CypD deletion or inhibition rescued mitochondrial function and cognitive performance in murine models [51]. In PD, mitochondrial toxins like MPTP (1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine) and rotenone induce oxidative stress and calcium overload, while α-synuclein (α-syn) accumulates at mitochondria and induces mPTP opening [52]. In HD, mutant Huntingtin (mHTT) disrupts calcium levels and increases mitochondrial ROS, lowering the threshold for calcium-induced mPTP opening in mitochondria [53, 54]. Together, these studies establish a common pathological cascade in NDDs. Disease-specific proteins (Aβ, α-syn, mHTT) induce mitochondrial calcium overload and oxidative stress, which sensitize the mPTP, leading to mitochondrial failure, mtDNA release, and neuronal degeneration.

Mitochondria maintain their functional integrity through a coordinated quality control network, which includes mitochondrial fission and fusion, proteolytic degradation of oxidized/damaged proteins, and the removal and/or recycle of dysfunctional mitochondria via mitophagy [55, 56]. Mitophagy is crucial for removing depolarized, dysfunctional, or oxidized mitochondria that contain oxidatively damaged mtDNA [57, 58]. The canonical PINK1–Parkin-mediated mitophagy enables the recognition and ubiquitination of damaged mitochondria, facilitating their autophagic clearance, while the receptor-mediated pathways involving BNIP3, NIX, and FUNDC1 provide additional layers of regulation, particularly under stress conditions relevant to aging and NDDs [59–62]. Compromised mitophagy, which is increasingly observed with aging and in NDDs, allows damaged mitochondria to accumulate, increasing the possibility of mtDNA instability and release [60, 63]. In microglia, age-associated decline in mitophagic efficiency exacerbates mitochondrial dysfunction, promoting excessive ROS production and calcium dysregulation. These conditions sensitize mitochondria to prolonged mPTP opening, leading to matrix swelling, rupture of the OMM, and subsequent release of mtDNA fragments into the cytosol [42, 45, 48]. The cytosolic mtDNA can then act as a potent DAMP, activating innate immune pathways and sustaining chronic neuroinflammatory signaling in disorders such as AD, PD and HD.

In NDDs, impaired mitophagy causes accumulation of defective mitochondria containing oxidatively damaged mtDNA, which, upon release, can activate innate immune sensors, like cGAS, and promote neuroinflammation [63, 64]. Notably, the presence of Aβ exacerbates mitochondrial ROS production and impairs mitophagy, further enhancing mitochondrial dysfunction [65, 66]. In PD, mutations in the PINK1 and Parkin genes, which encode key regulatory proteins of mitophagy, prevent the efficient clearance of damaged mitochondria [60]. As a result, neurons accumulate damaged or dysfunctional mitochondria, leading to excessive ROS production, increased sensitivity to mPTP opening, and cytosolic release of mtDNA, which activates innate immune signaling in microglia and accelerates neurodegeneration [67]. Similarly, in HD, mHTT not only impairs autophagy initiation and mitochondrial trafficking, but also directly inhibits the TIM23 protein import complex, reducing the import of nuclear-encoded mitochondrial proteins and causing early mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress [68, 69]. This combination of defective mitophagy and impaired protein import exacerbates the proteostatic stress, promotes aggregate formation, and further compromises mitochondrial dynamics. The released mtDNA can activate cGAS–STING signaling, creating a self-reinforcing loop where inflammation further impairs mitochondrial quality control, promotes additional mtDNA release, and accelerates neuronal degeneration. Collectively, these observations highlight a convergent pathogenic mechanism in NDDs, in which mitochondrial quality control failure, through impaired mitophagy, defective protein import, and ROS-driven dysfunction, links mitochondrial damage, mtDNA-mediated inflammation, and protein aggregation [68–73].

Mitochondria are known to be a center for neuronal survival and death, which integrates metabolic signals and stress responses to decide and activate apoptosis. In the intrinsic apoptotic pathway, the BCL-2 family proteins play a critical regulatory role. Pro-apoptotic effectors BAX and BAK oligomerize upon activation, inducing mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization (MOMP), a crucial event during apoptosis [73, 74]. Traditionally, MOMP is recognized for cytosolic release of cytochrome c, Smac/DIABLO, and other intermembrane space proteins, thereby activating caspase cascades and executing apoptosis [75]. However, recent work has demonstrated that MOMP facilitates the mtDNA release into the cytosol [76, 77]. Mechanistically, BAX and BAK assemble into large, lipidic pores within the OMM that can dynamically enlarge over time [78]. During apoptosis, the BAX/BAK pores permit protrusion or “herniation” of the inner mitochondrial membrane, carrying mitochondrial matrix content, including mtDNA, into the cytosol [76]. This process effectively breaks the mitochondrial compartmentalization that normally keeps mtDNA away from innate immune sensors. Unlike transient mPTP observed during mitophagy, apoptotic MOMP often results in continuous membrane disruption, greatly increasing the possibility of mtDNA release. Importantly, even in the absence of full caspase activation, BAX/BAK-mediated membrane permeabilization can still cause mtDNA leakage and trigger inflammatory responses [79]. This phenomenon is particularly relevant in post-mitotic neurons, where caspase activation is often tightly controlled, yet sub-lethal mitochondrial permeabilization can still promote chronic inflammation.

In the context of NDDs, this mechanism has profound implications. In AD, Aβ accumulation within neurons increases mitochondrial ROS production and sensitizes mitochondria to apoptosis by promoting BAX activation [80]. The activated BAX forms large enough pores to release mtDNA into the cytosol, which then amplifies neuroinflammation through the cGAS–STING signaling. Similarly, in PD, α-syn aggregates localize to mitochondria, impair mitochondrial dynamics, and increase oxidative stress, which together trigger BAX/BAK-mediated MOMP [81]. In HD, mHTT impairs mitochondrial calcium buffering and promotes oxidative stress, facilitating BAX recruitment to mitochondria [82]. BAX/BAK pore formation in HD models can similarly enable mtDNA release, feeding into a cycle of inflammation and neurodegeneration. These insights reveal a dual pathogenic role of BAX/BAK-mediated MOMP in neurological diseases beyond executing apoptosis: it can directly promote mtDNA release, which can activate neuroinflammation and drive disease progression. This convergence of mitochondrial dysfunction, apoptosis, and innate immune activation highlights therapeutic opportunities. Strategies that stabilize mitochondrial membranes, modulate BAX/BAK pore dynamics [83], or block downstream mtDNA sensing pathways [84] could inhibit inflammation without fully blocking apoptosis.

Unlike the controlled, typically non-inflammatory process of apoptosis, necrotic and necroptotic cell death are characterized by plasma membrane integrity loss, resulting in the extracellular release of intracellular components, including intact mitochondria or mitochondrial fragments that harbor mtDNA [85, 86]. Similarly, pyroptosis, a caspase-1-dependent form of cell death, induces gasdermin-D pore formation in the plasma membrane, culminating in cellular swelling and rupture [87, 88]. These lytic cell death pathways promote the release of DAMPs, notably mtDNA, into the extracellular milieu [33, 89]. Extracellular mtDNA could engage pattern recognition receptors (PRR) on microglia, astrocytes, and infiltrating immune cells, serving as a potent pro-inflammatory signal that drives neuroinflammation in acute brain injury, ischemia, and chronic NDDs.

Recent studies have revealed that cells actively secrete EVs, such as exosomes and microvesicles [90]. These EVs can encapsulate whole mitochondria, mitochondrial fragments, or naked mtDNA, shielding them from extracellular degradation and enabling intercellular communication [90]. Under conditions of cellular stress, neurons, astrocytes, and microglia have all been shown to release mtDNA-containing EVs [67, 91]. This EV-mediated transfer of mtDNA may amplify neuroinflammatory responses by activating PRR in recipient cells, thereby facilitating the spread of pathological signals across neural networks. Mechanistically, EV biogenesis depends on the endosomal sorting complexes required for transport machinery and is modulated by stress-responsive pathways, although the precise molecular mechanisms that govern selective packaging of mtDNA into EV remain incompletely understood [92].
